meta.title: Latticework — master mental-model catalog
meta.premise: Charlie Munger is the seed of this catalog, not its boundary. Munger said the latticework held '80 or 90 important models' (USC Business School, 1994) and 'the best 100 or so models' (Stanford Law School, 1996), but he never published a list and declined to produce one on the record at least twice. Every 'complete Munger list' in circulation is therefore a community reconstruction — which is why no two agree. This catalog records what Munger demonstrably said, and then extends past him.

provenance.munger-named: Munger explicitly named or labelled the model in his own words, in a retrievable primary source.
provenance.munger-used: Munger applied or described the model on the record without naming it as a model. Also used where the only evidence is an attendee's third-person paraphrase of a meeting rather than his direct speech.
provenance.munger-adjacent: A compiler attributes it to Munger but the evidence is indirect or plausible only; he never named it.
provenance.community: Added to the latticework genre by a compiler (Farnam Street, Griffin, Bevelin, Hagstrom, Sources of Insight), not by Munger.
provenance.canon-addition: Added during this research for discipline completeness; absent from both Munger's record and the audited compilations.

fields.id: stable catalog id (M001…), assigned by discipline then provenance strength then name
fields.name: canonical model name
fields.aka: variant labels from the source pool that merged into this entry
fields.definition: what the model asserts and when to reach for it
fields.mungerQuote: verbatim Munger line, only where one existed in the mined source data; never reconstructed
fields.listedBy: which pooled sources carried the model

primary_sources_mined: Poor Charlie's Almanack Talks One and Four through Ten, and Chapter Two (The Munger Approach) incl. the Investing Principles Checklist and Mungerisms
A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom (USC Business School, 1994)
Worldly Wisdom Revisited / Talk Three (Stanford Law School, 1996)
The Psychology of Human Misjudgment — 1995 Harvard speech (24 Standard Causes) and the 2005 Almanack rewrite (25 Tendencies)
Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (UCSB Herb Kay Memorial Lecture, 2003)
Late interviews and speeches: Harvard-Westlake 2010, BBC, Michigan Ross 2010/2017, Caltech 2020, Acquired podcast 2023, CNBC
compilations_audited: Farnam Street / Shane Parrish, ~100 models (101 items audited: 26 direct-Munger, 24 plausible, 51 community additions)
Sources of Insight, 'Charlie Munger's 129 Mental Models' (129 items: 60 direct-Munger)
Tren Griffin, Charlie Munger: The Complete Investor + 25iq list (165 items: 63 direct-Munger)
Peter Bevelin, Seeking Wisdom: From Darwin to Munger (49 items: 27 direct-Munger)
Robert Hagstrom, Investing: The Last Liberal Art / Latticework (54 items: 7 direct-Munger)
method.verification: Extractions were adversarially re-checked against the sources of record: 312 items checked, 4 dropped (fabricated provenance claim, misattributed maxim, wrong-source, mis-filed duplicate), 26 attribution corrections. Claims from the first research pass were verified 3-0 by independent adversarial voters.
method.known_gaps: Compilation items were audited for provenance but their definitions were not individually re-verified against each compiler's text. Quotes exist for only 57 entries because most mined instance records carried citations without quote text; absent quotes were left empty rather than reconstructed.

models.name: Depreciation
models.definition: The systematic spreading of a long-lived asset's cost across its useful life. It is a genuine economic cost of using up equipment, not a bookkeeping fiction. Use it to test whether reported "cash earnings" are real: if a business must keep spending to replace what wears out, ignoring depreciation overstates what the owner can actually take home.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting, 2003
models.mungerQuote: bullshit earnings

models.name: Double-Entry Bookkeeping
aka: Accounting as a Rule-Laying System of Civilization
Accounting as a foundational rule-laying system of civilization
Double-entry bookkeeping (Pacioli) as a virtue technology
Double-entry bookkeeping as a civilization-boosting virtue technology (Venice)
Double-entry bookkeeping as a civilizational advance; accounting integrity as infrastructure
Double-entry bookkeeping as a virtue technology
models.definition: Every transaction is recorded twice, as a debit and an offsetting credit, so the books must balance. That makes errors and omissions structurally detectable rather than merely improbable. Reach for it as the archetype of a self-checking system: any process that forces two independent records of the same fact to reconcile will catch mistakes that a single-entry record silently swallows.
models.mungerCitation: USC Business School, 1994

models.name: EBITDA Critique
models.definition: EBITDA strips out interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization. But interest and taxes are real cash outflows, and depreciation stands in for a real obligation to replace assets, so EBITDA systematically flatters capital-intensive and leveraged businesses. Use it as a red flag: when a promoter leads with EBITDA rather than free cash flow or owner earnings, ask which real costs the metric is being used to hide.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Misuse of EBITDA)
models.mungerQuote: bullshit earnings

models.name: Float
aka: Insurance float / low-cost other-people's-money (the hedgehog's one big thing)
models.definition: Money you hold and can invest but do not own: insurance premiums collected before claims are paid, customer deposits, deferred revenue, negative working capital. Use it to spot businesses financed for free, or better, by their own operations. Float is a liability on the books that works like an interest-free (sometimes negative-cost) loan of indefinite duration.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Berkshire Is a Hell of a Business)

models.name: Accounting Numbers vs Economic Reality
aka: Limits of accounting (real costs invisible to conventional accounting)
models.definition: Financial statements are a compressed model of a business, and like all models they omit, estimate, and distort. Book value ignores intangibles, historical cost ignores inflation, and reported earnings embed dozens of discretionary judgments. Reach for this whenever a number is being treated as the territory rather than the map: ask what the accounting is measuring, what it structurally cannot measure, and who chose the estimates.
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998

models.name: Stock-Based Compensation as a Real Expense
aka: Corrupt accounting as systemic force (unexpensed employee stock options as febezzle)
Phony earnings analogized to classic inventory-overcounting fraud
Stock options are compensation expense — unexpensed options create phony earnings
Unexpensed stock options / corrupt accounting as a systemic force
models.definition: Equity issued to employees is compensation paid by diluting existing owners; excluding it from "adjusted" earnings does not make it free, it merely moves the cost from the income statement to the share count. Reach for it whenever a company reports large non-GAAP profits: if the payment is not compensation, what is it, and if it is, why is it not an expense?
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000; The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight)

models.name: Balance Sheet
models.definition: A point-in-time statement of what a business owns, what it owes, and the residual left for owners, constrained by the identity Assets = Liabilities + Equity. Use it to assess solvency, leverage, and the capital actually tied up in a business. That capital is the denominator that turns a flattering profit number into an honest return on capital.
models.mungerCitation: supporting usage: USC 1994 (accounting as 'the language of practical business life'; individual statements not named as models)

models.name: Cash Flow Statement
models.definition: A reconciliation of accrual earnings to actual cash moved, split into operating, investing, and financing activities. Reach for it as the hardest-to-fake of the three statements: profits are an opinion shaped by estimates, but cash is a fact, and a persistent gap between net income and operating cash flow is one of the most reliable early warnings of accounting trouble.

models.name: Generally Accepted Accounting Principles (GAAP)
models.definition: The rulebook that standardizes how financial results are measured and presented, making companies comparable across firms and periods. Use it as a shared language and a floor, not a ceiling: GAAP compliance certifies conformity to rules, not that the numbers describe economic reality. A great deal of value-destroying behavior is perfectly GAAP-legal.

models.name: Income Statement
models.definition: A period statement matching revenues against the expenses incurred to earn them, yielding profit. It is the primary lens for judging whether operations create value over a span of time. Reach for it to see margin structure and cost behavior, but remember it is an accrual construct full of estimates (useful life, reserves, recognition timing), so it answers "did we earn?" not "did we collect?"

models.name: Accrual vs Cash Accounting
models.definition: Accrual accounting records revenue when earned and expense when incurred; cash accounting records them when money moves. Reach for the distinction whenever you need to know whether a reported result reflects timing judgment or actual cash. Accrual gives the better economic picture, but it hands management the levers (recognition, reserves, capitalization) that most manipulation runs through.

models.name: Capitalization vs Expensing
models.definition: The choice of whether a cost hits the income statement now (expense) or sits on the balance sheet and bleeds out over years (capitalize). The spending is economically identical; the reported profits are wildly different. Reach for it as a manipulation detector: a firm quietly capitalizing what peers expense is borrowing earnings from the future, and rising capitalized costs alongside flat revenue is a classic prelude to a writedown.

models.name: Cost of Capital / Opportunity Cost of Capital
models.definition: The return foregone on the next-best use of the same money, which sets the hurdle any project or acquisition must clear to create rather than destroy value. Reach for it as the discipline that kills empire-building: growth funded at 10% capital earning 6% returns makes a company bigger and its owners poorer, no matter how good the earnings-per-share optics look.

models.name: Financial Leverage
models.definition: Using borrowed money to magnify returns on equity. It magnifies losses identically, and unlike operating leverage, it comes with a creditor who can force the outcome at the worst moment. Use it with the asymmetry in mind: leverage converts a survivable bad year into a terminal one, and the risk is not the average outcome but the covenant tripped at the bottom of the cycle.

models.name: Goodwill (accounting vs economic)
models.definition: Accounting goodwill is the plug entry booked when one firm pays more than the fair value of another's net assets. Economic goodwill is the real earning power of intangibles like brand, habit, and position, which lets a business earn far above the tangible capital it employs. Use the distinction to avoid two errors: writing off a wonderful business because its balance-sheet goodwill is impaired, and crediting a mediocre one because an overpayment sits capitalized on its books.

models.name: Off-Balance-Sheet Obligations
models.definition: Real economic commitments that the rules permit to sit outside reported liabilities: operating leases (historically), guarantees, special purpose entities, pension shortfalls, derivative exposures. Reach for this whenever leverage looks suspiciously low. The footnotes, not the balance sheet, are where the obligations that killed Enron and the 2008 banks were disclosed.

models.name: Operating Leverage
models.definition: The ratio of fixed to variable costs, which amplifies how changes in revenue flow through to profit. High fixed costs mean small volume swings produce violent earnings swings in both directions. Reach for it to understand cyclicality and fragility: airlines, steel, and semiconductor fabs are dangerous not because demand is uniquely volatile but because their cost structure magnifies it.

models.name: Owner Earnings / Free Cash Flow
models.definition: Cash from operations, less the capital expenditure genuinely required to maintain competitive position. It is what an owner could extract each year without shrinking the business. Reach for it as the corrective to reported net income: it forces you to separate maintenance capex (a real cost) from growth capex (a discretionary investment), which no accounting statement does for you.

models.name: Return on Invested Capital
models.definition: After-tax operating profit divided by the capital actually employed. It is the single best summary of whether a business converts money into more money at an attractive rate. Use it as the primary quality screen: over long holding periods an investor's return converges on the business's ROIC, so a high-ROIC compounder bought at a fair price beats a low-ROIC business bought cheaply.

models.name: Revenue Recognition
models.definition: The rules and judgments governing when a sale counts as revenue: on shipment, on delivery, over a contract's life, gross or net. Use it as the first place to look when growth seems too smooth or too fast. Pulling revenue forward, channel stuffing, and booking gross instead of net are the most common ways a business appears to grow without actually growing.

models.name: Working Capital Cycle
models.definition: The time and cash consumed between paying suppliers and collecting from customers: inventory days plus receivable days minus payable days. Use it to see the hidden capital appetite of growth. A firm with a long cycle must fund every incremental sale, while one with a negative cycle (paid before it pays) grows by generating cash instead of consuming it.

models.name: Metaphor & Analogy
aka: Metaphor and analogy as engines of understanding
models.definition: Understanding moves by mapping the structure of something known onto something unknown — this is the primary mechanism for teaching anything genuinely new. Reach for it when an explanation is technically complete and still not landing, but track where the mapping breaks: every analogy imports false implications along with true ones, and the failure mode is riding it past its domain.

models.name: Audience
models.definition: No message has meaning independent of who receives it — the same words land differently depending on the audience's prior knowledge, incentives, identity, and emotional state. Reach for it before composing anything: decide who you are speaking to and what they already believe, because a message optimized for no one persuades no one.

models.name: Character
models.definition: People engage with agents who want something and are constrained in getting it — character is desire plus obstacle plus the choices revealed under pressure. Use it whenever an abstraction needs to be felt: a statistic about a population moves no one, while a person with a want does.

models.name: Contrast (aesthetic)
models.definition: Perception is comparative: attention goes to difference, so meaning is created by juxtaposition — light reads as light against dark, quiet lands after loud, a plain sentence hits hardest among ornate ones. Reach for it when nothing in your work stands out, which usually means everything is competing at the same intensity. Distinct from Munger's Contrast-Misreaction Tendency, which is a bias about distorted judgment; this is a craft tool for directing attention.

models.name: Genre
models.definition: Genre is a set of shared conventions that pre-loads the audience with expectations about form, content, and how to interpret what follows — it is compression, letting you skip what the convention already supplies. Use it to know which rules you are inheriting, and remember that violating a genre is only powerful when the audience knows the rule you broke.

models.name: Melody
models.definition: A melody is a sequence whose individual elements are unremarkable but whose ordered shape is memorable and reproducible — the line someone can hum back. Reach for it when you need an idea to survive retransmission: give it a contour people can carry, because arguments are forgotten while shapes are repeated.

models.name: Performance
models.definition: Delivery is part of the message — the same content transmitted with different timing, presence, conviction, or vulnerability produces different effects, and it cannot be separated out as mere packaging. Reach for it when a correct argument keeps failing to land, and recognize its dark side: performance can carry content that deserves to fail.

models.name: Plot
models.definition: Plot is causal sequence: not one thing after another but one thing because of another, which is what turns events into explanation. Reach for it when you need to make a chain of causation legible — and audit it when the causal links you are asserting are really just chronology dressed up as consequence.

models.name: Representation
models.definition: Every depiction is a lossy stand-in for the thing itself, chosen by someone who decided what to keep and what to drop — the map, the model, the chart, the portrait. Use it to ask what a representation omits and whose choices shaped it, especially when the representation has become more real to you than the territory.

models.name: Rhythm
models.definition: Patterned repetition in time creates expectation, and expectation is what makes both satisfaction and surprise possible — in prose, music, speech, or product cadence, the pattern must be established before breaking it means anything. Reach for it when work feels monotonous (the pattern never varies) or chaotic (no pattern was ever set).

models.name: Setting
models.definition: The environment is not backdrop but constraint — it determines what is possible, what is at stake, and what a given action costs, so the same act means different things in different worlds. Reach for it when behavior looks inexplicable: you are probably missing the setting that makes it rational.

models.name: Chekhov's Gun
models.definition: Every element you include makes an implicit promise that it matters — a rifle on the wall in act one must fire by act three, or it should not be on the wall. Use it as an economy test for any communication: unused elements are not neutral, they actively misdirect attention and drain trust in your judgment about what is important.

models.name: Composition
models.definition: The spatial or structural arrangement of elements directs the eye and encodes hierarchy before any content is read — what is large, centered, or isolated is understood as important. Reach for it whenever presenting anything visual or structured: if everything is emphasized, nothing is, and a layout that does not choose a hierarchy has still communicated one by accident.

models.name: Curse of Knowledge
models.definition: Once you know something, you cannot reconstruct what it was like not to know it, so experts systematically overestimate how much of their explanation is landing. Reach for it whenever you are the one who understands the material best — that expertise is precisely what disqualifies your intuitions about clarity, which is why testing on an actual naive listener beats reasoning about one.

models.name: Narrative Arc
models.definition: Sustained attention follows a shape of rising tension and release — a question opened, complicated, and answered — which is why arcs hold audiences that flat recitations of fact lose. Distinct from plot (the causal chain); the arc is the tension curve laid over it. Reach for it when material is accurate, complete, and still boring: usually nothing was ever put at stake.

models.name: Narrative Fallacy
models.definition: Humans compulsively impose causal stories on random or overdetermined events, so a satisfying explanation is evidence of narrative skill rather than truth — and the better the story, the more suspicious you should be. Reach for it as the counterweight to every other model in this bucket: they all make stories more persuasive, and this is the one that asks whether the story is real.

models.name: Negative Space
models.definition: What is left out actively shapes what is perceived — silence, white space, the unsaid, and the omitted detail are compositional choices, not absences. Use it when adding more has stopped helping: the constraint is often that nothing has been removed, and an audience given room to complete the meaning holds it more firmly than one handed it whole.

models.name: Point of View
models.definition: Every account is delivered from a position with a horizon — someone sees this and not that, and the choice of vantage determines what can be known and how much the audience trusts it. Reach for it when evaluating any account (whose eyes is this through, and what can they not see) and when constructing one, since an unacknowledged POV reads as objectivity while smuggling in a perspective.

models.name: Rhetorical Triangle (Ethos, Pathos, Logos)
models.definition: Persuasion runs on three channels — credibility of the speaker, emotion of the audience, and logic of the argument — and an argument strong on only one usually fails. Reach for it as a diagnostic when persuasion stalls: sound logic without ethos gets ignored, and ethos plus pathos without logos wins the room while deserving to lose it.

models.name: Rule of Three / Repetition
models.definition: Repetition drives retention, and three is the minimum count that establishes a pattern while staying inside working memory — enough to feel deliberate, few enough to hold. Reach for it when something must be remembered rather than merely understood, and note the tension with Signal vs. Noise: repetition of the wrong thing entrenches the wrong thing.

models.name: Show, Don't Tell
models.definition: Conclusions the audience reaches themselves from concrete evidence are held far more strongly than conclusions asserted at them, because assertion invites resistance while inference recruits the listener as co-author. Reach for it when your claim is true and being rejected anyway: replace the adjective with the detail that earned it.

models.name: Suspension of Disbelief
models.definition: Audiences will voluntarily grant implausible premises if the work is internally consistent — they forgive the impossible but not the incoherent. Reach for it when asking how much license you have: the budget is spent on premises and is destroyed not by big improbabilities but by small contradictions with your own established rules.

models.name: The Medium Is the Message
models.definition: The channel itself shapes cognition and social effect independent of any content it carries — the same argument delivered as a memo, a chart, a thread, or a conversation produces different thinking, not just different reception. Reach for it when choosing a channel, and note that each medium's constraints silently select which ideas can be expressed in it at all.

models.name: Voice & Tone
models.definition: Voice is the consistent identity behind a message; tone is its situational register. Together they carry most of the trust signal, largely below conscious notice. Reach for it when content is right but the reception is wrong — mismatch between tone and occasion (breezy about a crisis, ponderous about a triviality) discredits the content it carries.

models.name: Bureaucracy as Cancer (Metastasis of Organizational Overhead)
models.definition: Bureaucracy behaves like a malignancy: it grows on its own account, consumes the host's resources, spreads to healthy divisions, and is not stopped by anything internal to the organism. Reach for it when diagnosing why large organizations decay from within and why the remedy has to be excision from outside rather than incremental reform.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2018, Morning Session, https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2018/05/05/morning-session--2018-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html

models.name: Economy as Ecosystem
models.definition: A free-market economy behaves less like a physics equation and more like an ecosystem: a web of interdependent, co-evolving participants where energy flows, competitive pressure and environmental change decide who thrives, and where no single equilibrium law governs. Reach for it when a market resists mechanical modeling — ask who eats whom, what the flows are, and what happens when one node is removed.
models.mungerCitation: USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom' 1994; UCSB 2003 (economics 'more like biology than physics')
models.mungerQuote: I find it quite useful to think of a free market economy... as sort of the equivalent of an ecosystem

models.name: Effort Minimization (Biological Energy Economy)
aka: Tendency to Minimize Energy Output (Mental and Physical)
models.definition: Every creature, including the human brain, is built to conserve energy and will default to the cheapest available routine — which is why institutions drift toward the path of least resistance and why people accept received answers rather than compute their own. Use it to predict where sloth and mental shortcutting will appear by default, and to recognize that fighting it requires deliberately imposed structure, not exhortation.
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations (Talk Six), 1998

models.name: Evolution by Natural Selection
aka: Evolution / natural selection (Darwin) applied to markets and economic selection
Evolution Part One: Natural Selection and Extinction
models.definition: Where variation, heredity and differential survival exist, designs that fit their environment better propagate and the rest are culled — producing apparent design with no designer. Reach for it wherever a population of competing things (firms, products, strategies, ideas, habits) is being filtered over time: ask what is varying, what is being selected for, and how fast the cycle turns, because the selection criterion — not anyone's intent — determines what survives.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998; USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom' 1994; Darwin held up as a thinking exemplar in Harvard School Commencement 1986 and throughout Poor Charlie's Almanack (2005)

models.name: Evolved Neural Preprogramming of Preferences
aka: Darwinian natural selection / evolved neural preprogramming of preferences
models.definition: Human wants are not blank slates: natural selection hardwired us to crave sugar, fat, salt, caffeine, novelty and status because those cravings paid in the ancestral environment. Use it when designing or valuing a consumer product — Munger's Coca-Cola case turns on selling to preferences evolution already installed, which is far cheaper and more durable than manufacturing new ones.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four, Poor Charlie's Almanack), July 20, 1996

models.name: Extinction
aka: Evolution Part One: Natural Selection and Extinction
models.definition: The overwhelming majority of species — and of firms, technologies and business models — die out; survival is the exception and the base rate for permanence is close to zero. Use it as an antidote to extrapolating an incumbent forever, and as the reason Munger studied failure: the fossil record of dead businesses teaches more per unit of study than the survivors.
models.mungerCitation: Substance in USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom' 1994 ('competitive destruction' of incumbent businesses); FS lists extinction with natural selection

models.name: Interconnectedness / Relatedness of Systems
aka: Interconnectedness / relatedness of systems (Huxley: 'one damn relatedness after another')
models.definition: Biological and social reality is not a set of independent boxes but a dense mesh of relations, so a fact isolated from its relations is nearly useless — knowledge has to be hung on a lattice of connections to be retrievable and true. This is the structural argument for the whole latticework project: learn disciplines for how they relate, not as separate silos.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 2 (Munger's Approach), quoting Julian Huxley
models.mungerQuote: one damn relatedness after another

models.name: Malthusian Trap
models.definition: Population expands to consume any gain in output, so productivity improvements get absorbed by more mouths rather than higher living standards, pinning the system back at subsistence. Reach for it when an efficiency gain is quietly eaten by expanded demand or headcount instead of producing surplus — and note that Munger invoked it chiefly to explain why the modern world's escape from it was so improbable.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2020, Q8, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2020/02/28/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journal-annual-meeting-2020/; A Conversation with Charlie Munger, University of Michigan Ross, 2017, https://worldlypartners.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/2017-a-conversation-with-charlie-munger-at-university-of-michigan-ross.pdf

models.name: Niches and Specialization
models.definition: In a crowded ecosystem, survival goes to those who become extremely good at one narrow thing rather than mediocre across many — competition is fiercest where niches overlap, and a defended niche yields outsized returns on modest capability. Use it in career and business strategy: find the corner of the map where you can be the best, and beware of businesses whose niche is dissolving.
models.mungerCitation: USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom' 1994 (ecosystem passage)
models.mungerQuote: just as animals flourish in niches, people who specialize... frequently find good economics

models.name: Physiological Satiation (Stop-Consumption / Aftertaste Mechanisms)
aka: Physiological satiation — evolved stop-consumption (aftertaste) moderation mechanisms
models.definition: Organisms evolved shutoff valves — satiety, aftertaste, nausea — that halt consumption before a good thing becomes harmful, which means each additional unit delivers less than the last and eventually turns negative. Munger's practical use: engineer the aftertaste out (Coke's flavor is designed so satiation arrives late) to raise lifetime consumption; more generally, ask what the natural stopping mechanism is in any consumption system and whether it has been defeated.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four, Poor Charlie's Almanack), July 20, 1996

models.name: Genetics / Heritability of Tendencies
models.definition: Much of behavior is inherited hardware rather than learned software — the standard human misjudgment tendencies exist because genes coding for them outcompeted alternatives, not because people chose them. Use it to set expectations: heritable tendencies can be channeled or countered by structure and checklists, but they cannot be argued away, in yourself or anyone else.
models.mungerCitation: 'The Psychology of Human Misjudgment', Harvard 1995 — tendencies attributed to gene inheritance and evolutionary origins

models.name: Invasive Species / Introduced Predator (Northern Pike Model)
models.definition: A new entrant dropped into a system that evolved without it faces prey and incumbents with no defenses, so it can take over the whole niche with startling speed. Reach for it when a competitor, technology, or capital source arrives from outside a market's normal evolutionary pressures and the existing players' adaptations are simply irrelevant to it.
models.mungerCitation: 2010 Wesco annual meeting notes, Part I (unattributed), p.325, https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf

models.name: 'Autopsy' Learning (Post-Mortem Study of Failure)
models.definition: Borrowed from pathology: dissect the dead case in detail and name the cause of death, rather than only studying the living and successful. Munger's practice of collecting business failures and asking exactly what killed each one is a systematic defense against survivorship bias — and the lesson usually generalizes better than any success story.
models.mungerCitation: Repeated on-record advocacy of studying failure ('I like to study failure'); the 'autopsy' framing is the compiler's

models.name: Hierarchical Organization / Dominance Hierarchies
models.definition: Social primates evolved to live in ranked groups, which left humans with a deep disposition to defer to whoever occupies the top slot — the biological root of Munger's Authority-Misinfluence Tendency. Use it to anticipate that a confident leader's error will propagate unchallenged through subordinates, and to build explicit dissent channels precisely because deference is instinctive rather than reasoned.
models.mungerCitation: 'The Psychology of Human Misjudgment' — Authority-Misinfluence Tendency grounded in humans 'living in dominance hierarchies'

models.name: Adaptation
aka: Evolution Part Two: Adaptation and The Red Queen Effect
models.definition: Traits get shaped to fit the environment as it was, not as it is — so adaptation always lags, and a trait that is optimal today is a liability once the environment moves. Reach for it when evaluating a well-adapted incumbent: the tighter the fit to the old environment, the more brittle it usually is when conditions shift.

models.name: Adaptive Market Hypothesis
aka: Adaptive Market Hypothesis (Andrew Lo) — reconciling EMH with behavioral finance
models.definition: Andrew Lo's reframing: market efficiency is not a fixed law but an evolutionary outcome — it varies with how many competing strategies occupy the niche, and inefficiencies appear and vanish as participants adapt, die out, or flood in. Use it to think about where edge exists now rather than in principle: an arbitrage is a niche that gets crowded and then extinguished.

models.name: Cooperation (Reciprocal Altruism)
models.definition: Cooperation survives evolutionarily wherever interactions repeat and defectors can be identified and punished — reputation and retaliation, not goodwill, make it stable. Use it to design and diagnose relationships: shorten cycles, make behavior visible, and ensure defection is detectable, or the equilibrium collapses to mutual defection no matter what the parties intend.

models.name: Fitness Landscapes
models.definition: Picture strategies as points on a rugged terrain of peaks (high payoff) and valleys (low): incremental improvement climbs the nearest hill, which means you get stuck on a local peak and can only reach a higher one by first going downhill. Use it to explain why competent organizations refuse obviously better strategies — the transition cost is a valley they won't cross — and why occasional deliberate exploration beats pure optimization.

models.name: Physiology
models.definition: The study of how the parts of a living body actually function and regulate one another — pumps, feedback loops, signaling, energy budgets, failure modes. As a thinking tool it supplies the vocabulary for treating any organization or system as a body: what is the circulation, what is the nervous system, what organ is failing, and is the presenting symptom the disease or a compensation for it.

models.name: Population Dynamics
models.definition: Populations grow, oscillate and crash according to reproduction rates, resources and predation — exponential growth is always temporary and boom is usually followed by overshoot and bust. Use it on anything with a growth curve (customers, credit, bacteria, headcount): identify the limiting resource and the lag between overshoot and correction, because the lag is what makes the crash violent.

models.name: Punctuated Equilibrium
aka: Punctuated equilibrium (Gould/Eldredge), applied to bursts of market/industry change
models.definition: Change is not smooth: long stretches of near-stasis are interrupted by short, violent bursts of transformation, usually triggered by an environmental shock. Reach for it when a stable industry has been quiet for a decade — the quiet is not evidence of durability, and linear extrapolation is at its most dangerous precisely then.

models.name: Red Queen Effect
aka: Evolution Part Two: Adaptation and The Red Queen Effect
models.definition: When rivals co-evolve, everyone must run hard just to stay in place: heavy investment yields no relative gain because competitors match it, and the surplus flows to customers or suppliers rather than to profit. Reach for it in capital-intensive commodity industries — Buffett's textile mills are the canonical case — to distinguish spending that builds advantage from spending that merely maintains parity.

models.name: Replication
models.definition: Life's core trick is copying with high but imperfect fidelity — the copies compound exponentially, and the errors supply the variation selection then acts on. Reach for it wherever something self-copies (cells, memes, franchises, code, culture): fidelity determines stability, error rate determines adaptability, and copying rate determines who takes over the substrate.

models.name: Self-Preservation
models.definition: Every organism — and every bureaucracy, department and profession built by organisms — has a hardwired drive to survive, which reliably overrides its stated mission when the two conflict. Use it to predict institutional behavior: ask what threatens the entity's existence, and assume it will fight that harder than it pursues its official purpose.

models.name: Allometric Scaling (Kleiber's Law)
models.definition: Biology does not scale linearly: metabolic rate rises roughly as mass to the 3/4 power, so bigger organisms are more energy-efficient per unit but slower — pulse, lifespan and pace of change all stretch with size. Use it as the biological complement to advantages and defects of scale: the efficiency gain is real, and so is the loss of speed and responsiveness, and both follow predictably from getting bigger.

models.name: Carrying Capacity
aka: Limiting factor (Liebig's law of the minimum)
models.definition: Every environment supports a maximum sustainable population set by its scarcest resource; growth past that ceiling is borrowed and gets repaid via crash. Reach for it when a market, platform or organization is growing fast: name the binding constraint and the current headroom, since the question is never whether growth stops but whether it plateaus or overshoots and collapses.

models.name: Contagion and Epidemic Dynamics (R0)
models.definition: Spread is governed by the basic reproduction number — how many new cases each case generates — with R0 above one producing exponential takeoff and below one producing burnout, and the curve bending as susceptibles are exhausted. Reach for it for anything transmitted through a network (disease, rumors, panics, viral products, bank runs): the leverage points are contact rate, transmission probability and duration, and small changes to any of them flip the regime entirely.

models.name: Convergent Evolution
models.definition: Unrelated lineages facing the same problem arrive independently at the same solution — eyes, wings and streamlined bodies evolved many times over. Use it as evidence about constraints: when rivals with different origins all converge on one design, the design is likely forced by the physics of the problem rather than by imitation, and 'we'll do it differently' needs a very good reason.

models.name: Exaptation
models.definition: Features that evolved for one purpose get co-opted for another — feathers for insulation became flight, jaw bones became inner ear. Use it to resist assuming that current function reveals original purpose (a live source of just-so-story error), and as the mechanism behind genuine innovation: most new capabilities are repurposed old ones, not built from scratch.

models.name: Genetic Drift
models.definition: In small populations, traits spread or vanish by sheer chance rather than fitness — sampling noise, not selection, decides. Use it as a corrective to over-attributing outcomes to merit: in any small-n arena (startups, funds, careers, small teams), luck dominates skill in the observed record, and a founder effect can lock in an arbitrary trait permanently just because it happened to be present at the start.

models.name: Homeostasis
models.definition: Living systems hold key variables inside a narrow band via negative feedback, actively resisting displacement in either direction. Use it to explain why interventions get absorbed with no visible effect — the system is compensating — and to look for the regulating loop before concluding that a push failed; also a warning that a system holding steady may be working furiously to do so and near the limit of its compensation.

models.name: Hormesis
models.definition: A small dose of a stressor that would be harmful in large doses makes the organism stronger — exercise, fasting, vaccines, controlled fires. Reach for it when deciding whether to suppress volatility or allow it: systems shielded from all stress (over-managed forests, over-protected children, bailed-out banks) lose the adaptive response and fail catastrophically when a large shock finally lands.

models.name: Immune Response
models.definition: A layered defense that detects foreign material, mounts an escalating attack, and — crucially — remembers the intruder so the next response is faster. Use it as the template for organizational defense (detection, response, memory) and for its failure modes, which are the more instructive part: autoimmunity is a system attacking its own healthy tissue, and rejection is a healthy graft destroyed for being foreign — both common in companies absorbing outsiders or acquisitions.

models.name: Keystone Species and Trophic Cascades
models.definition: Some nodes matter far out of proportion to their size: remove the keystone and the whole web reorganizes, with effects cascading down through every level. Use it to locate the load-bearing element of a system — a person, supplier, product or norm whose removal would restructure everything — and to remember that the cascade is usually discovered only after the removal.

models.name: Kin Selection / Inclusive Fitness
models.definition: Genes propagate through relatives too, so self-sacrifice for kin is favored in proportion to relatedness times benefit versus cost (Hamilton's rule). Use it to explain the biological pull of nepotism, family firms and in-group favoritism — and to recognize that fairness rules must be structurally imposed because the instinct runs the other way.

models.name: Sexual Selection
models.definition: Traits can spread because they win mates rather than because they aid survival, producing costly signals — the peacock's tail — that are honest precisely because only a fit individual can afford the waste. Reach for it to decode conspicuous expenditure (headquarters, luxury goods, credentials, marketing spend): ask whether the cost is buying capability or buying a signal, and whether the signal is honest because it is unaffordable to fakers.

models.name: Symbiosis and Mutualism
models.definition: Two different species can become structurally interdependent to mutual benefit — mitochondria in cells, gut flora, pollinators — often to the point where neither survives alone. Distinct from cooperation among peers, this is cross-species integration; use it to think about deep partnerships and platform ecosystems, and to notice that mutualism sits on a spectrum with parasitism, sliding from one to the other as the balance of value shifts.

models.name: r/K Selection
models.definition: Two viable reproductive strategies: r — many cheap offspring, most of which die (dandelions, venture bets); K — few expensive offspring heavily invested in (elephants, concentrated positions). Use it to check strategy-environment fit: r wins in unstable, uncrowded environments with cheap experiments, K wins in stable, saturated ones — and the fatal error is running an r portfolio with K-sized bets, or vice versa.

models.name: Advantages of Scale
aka: Advantages of scale — big store, big selection, low price, plus capital-cost entry barrier (Nebraska Furniture Mart)
Supply-side economies of scale and scope (moat source 1)
models.definition: Munger's own model: bigness produces real advantages — lower cost per unit, the ability to amortize a national TV ad over vastly more units, more shelf space, more distribution reach. Use it to explain why the largest player in a scale-sensitive category tends to compound its lead rather than converge with rivals, and why sub-scale competitors in such categories are usually value traps.
models.mungerCitation: USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom', 1994 — extended 'advantages of scale' section (cost per unit, TV advertising, P&G)

models.name: Cancer Surgery Formula
models.definition: Munger's named turnaround model: when a business has one healthy core buried under a mass of bad operations, cut everything else away and keep only what is sound — as Berkshire did with GEICO. Reach for it in restructurings where the instinct is to fix every division; the model says find the part that was always good and excise the rest.
models.mungerCitation: USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom', 1994 — 'cancer surgery formula' named (GEICO turnaround)

models.name: Disadvantages of Scale (Bureaucracy)
models.definition: Munger's deliberate counterweight to advantages of scale: past some size, organizations develop bureaucracy, internal politics, and territoriality that make them slower and dumber than smaller rivals — Sears against Walmart, CBS in its decline. Reach for it whenever a scale thesis is being told without an accompanying account of why this particular giant will not rot.
models.mungerCitation: USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom', 1994 — 'disadvantages of scale' section (Sears vs. Walmart, CBS)

models.name: Economic Moats
aka: Barriers to imitation / deliberate differentiation (moat construction)
Eighth Variable: Determining what businesses to own, in whole or in part
Moats / durable competitive advantage (and their erosion)
models.definition: A moat is a structural characteristic of a business that lets it earn returns above its cost of capital for a long time while competitors try and fail to take those returns away. Reach for it as the first question in any business analysis: not 'is this a good business today?' but 'what specifically stops it from being competed to zero, and how long does that thing last?'
models.mungerCitation: BBC interview, 2009; term used routinely at Berkshire and Wesco meetings
models.mungerQuote: a business with some characteristics that give us a durable competitive advantage

models.name: Surfing (Riding a Long Wave)
aka: Surfing — catching and riding a big wave (Oracle, Japanese tire invasion)
models.definition: Munger's named model: get on a big rising wave early — a new technology or category — and ride it, and you will look brilliant for a very long time with quite ordinary skill (National Cash Register). Its corollary is the whole lesson: when the wave ends, the surfer's advantage ends with it, so never mistake the wave for the ability.
models.mungerCitation: USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom', 1994 — 'surfing' named as a model (National Cash Register)

models.name: The Rare Exceptional Manager
aka: The rare exceptional manager (Right Stuff in a Business 5)
models.definition: The fifth 'right stuff' screen: occasionally a fanatic — obsessed with cost, or service, or quality past all reason — creates advantage that no structural analysis of the industry would have predicted. Use it sparingly and asymmetrically: pay up when you have identified one, but never underwrite a thesis on the assumption that you can find or hire one.
models.mungerCitation: Fanatic-manager remarks, USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom', 1994 (e.g., on service fanaticism)

models.name: Brand Power
models.definition: A brand is a stored informational and social-proof advantage: it lets a buyer choose without investigating, and lets the seller charge more for a physically similar good — See's, Wrigley, Coca-Cola. Reach for it when you need to explain a price premium that no cost or technology difference accounts for; test it by asking whether the customer would still pay up if the label were removed.
models.mungerCitation: USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom', 1994 (See's Candies, Wrigley — informational and social-proof advantages of brand); 'Practical Thought About Practical Thought?', 1996 (Coca-Cola)

models.name: Rational vs. Cutthroat Competition
models.definition: Munger's puzzle: structurally similar oligopolies behave completely differently — cereal makers stay profitable while airlines compete themselves into ruin — so structure alone does not predict conduct. Reach for it before assuming concentration equals pricing power, and look for what actually disciplines behavior: whether capacity is perishable, whether the product is a commodity, and how the players' incentives are wired.
models.mungerCitation: USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom', 1994 (cereal vs. airline comparison); compiler's phrasing is a paraphrase, not a verbatim quote

models.name: Technology as Friend vs. Killer
aka: Benefits of technology improvements flow to customers, not owners, in commodity businesses (textile looms)
Technology's benefits flow to customers, not owners, in commodity businesses
Who captures the benefit of new technology
models.definition: New technology can enrich you or destroy you, and the deciding question is who captures the surplus: if the improvement is available to every competitor in a commodity business, the savings pass straight through to the customer and you have spent capital to stay in the same place. Reach for it before any efficiency-driven capex case — ask whether the gains will stick to you or be competed away.
models.mungerCitation: USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom', 1994 — new technology that helps you vs. kills you (textile looms)
models.mungerQuote: the money still won't come to you

models.name: Winner-Take-All / Winner-Take-Most Markets
models.definition: Some markets settle into a single dominant survivor rather than a stable set of competitors — the monopoly daily newspaper that ends up with all the classifieds while the number two folds. Reach for it when share advantage feeds on itself: in such markets second place is not 'a smaller version of first', it is a different and much worse business.
models.mungerCitation: USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom', 1994 (monopoly daily newspapers); the 'winner-take-all' label was popularized by Frank & Cook, 1995

models.name: Capacity & Supply Discipline
models.definition: In capital-intensive commodity industries, everyone's rational expansion at the top of the cycle produces collective overcapacity and the destruction of industry returns — Berkshire's textile business is the case study. Reach for it whenever an industry's profits depend on nobody adding a plant, because someone always does.
models.mungerCitation: Substance in Berkshire textile and commodity overcapacity discussions; label is the compiler's

models.name: Cost Advantage (Low-Cost Producer)
models.definition: A durable ability to deliver the same good at a structurally lower cost than rivals — from cheaper inputs, better location, superior process, or scale. It is the only moat that works in a commodity, because when the product is undifferentiated the low-cost producer is the last one still profitable at the price that kills everyone else.
models.mungerCitation: Substance covered within the USC 1994 advantages-of-scale discussion; not separately named

models.name: Industry Structure
models.definition: The durable configuration of an industry — concentration, entry barriers, capital intensity, buyer and supplier power — that sets the range of returns available to anyone operating in it. Use it to check the more important variable first: a good operator in a structurally bad industry (airlines) loses to a mediocre operator in a structurally good one.
models.mungerCitation: Porter framing; USC 1994 discusses market structures (newspapers, airlines vs. cereals) in substance

models.name: Learning / Experience Curve
models.definition: BCG's observation that unit cost falls by a roughly constant percentage with each doubling of cumulative output, as the organization learns. It is a function of accumulated volume rather than current size, which is why an early leader who ships more can hold a cost lead a later, larger entrant cannot buy — and why aggressive early pricing to win volume can be rational rather than reckless.
models.mungerCitation: BCG label; USC 1994 scale discussion covers efficiency gains from volume in substance

models.name: Lock-In via Distribution / Physical Network
models.definition: Control of a physical distribution network — Coca-Cola's worldwide bottling and shelf presence, a railroad's right-of-way — that a competitor could only match by spending decades and enormous capital, if regulators and geography even permitted it. Reach for it when the moat lives in irreplaceable infrastructure rather than in the product itself.
models.mungerCitation: Substance in 'Practical Thought About Practical Thought?', 1996 (Coca-Cola worldwide distribution) and railroad remarks; label is the compiler's

models.name: Moat Durability
models.definition: Distinct from whether a moat exists: how many years it survives, and whether it is widening or narrowing. A wide moat that erodes in five years is worth less than a narrow one that holds for thirty, so durability — not current margin — is what discounted cash flow actually prices, and most moat mistakes are duration errors rather than existence errors.
models.mungerCitation: Durability of competitive advantage discussed at Berkshire and Wesco meetings; label is the compiler's

models.name: Moat-Widening Skill
aka: Moat-widening skills (Right Stuff in a Business 3)
models.definition: The third 'right stuff' screen: management that actively deepens the barrier — spending today on price, service, or brand in ways that hurt current earnings and raise the cost of attacking them later. It is distinct from having a moat: most managements harvest the moat they inherited, and the rare ones reinvest in it.

models.name: Network Effects
aka: Demand-side economies of scale — network effects (moat source 2)
models.definition: Demand-side economies of scale: the product becomes more valuable to each user as more users join, so market share itself becomes the barrier. Reach for it when the asset being defended is the user base rather than the cost structure — and check whether the effect is global (defensible) or merely local, and how cheap it is for users to multi-home.
models.mungerCitation: Munger discussed the scale advantages of TV networks and monopoly newspapers at USC 1994 without using the term; 'network effects' is a modern platform-strategy label

models.name: Patents and Intellectual Property
models.definition: A legally granted, time-limited monopoly on an invention or work. Treat it as the most explicit and the most fragile moat: it is defensible in court but has a known expiry, can be designed around, and is only worth the cash flow that survives the cliff — so the real question is what you build behind the patent while it lasts.
models.mungerCitation: Patent-economics remarks at Wesco meetings

models.name: Regulation as a Moat
models.definition: Licenses, franchises, rate regulation, and compliance regimes can bar entry more effectively than any private advantage — but the same authority that granted the protection sets your returns and can revoke it. Reach for it in utilities, banks, and licensed businesses; the model asks you to price both the barrier and the political risk that comes attached to it.
models.mungerCitation: Regulated-utility and licensed-business discussions at Berkshire and Wesco meetings

models.name: Five Forces (Porter)
models.definition: Porter's frame that industry profitability is set by five forces — rivalry, new entrants, substitutes, supplier power, and buyer power — and that profits leak toward whichever force is strongest. Use it as a structured first pass on why an industry's returns are what they are; its blind spot is that it says nothing about complements, and it describes structure rather than the conduct Munger cared about.

models.name: Platform Economics (Two-Sided Markets)
models.definition: A platform intermediates two or more distinct groups whose value to each other rises with the other side's participation, which forces two hard choices: which side to subsidize and which to tax, and how to solve the chicken-and-egg cold start. Distinct from simple network effects because the cross-side dependency means the pricing structure, not just the price level, determines whether the thing works at all.

models.name: Switching Costs
models.definition: The money, time, data, retraining, or risk a customer must absorb to leave you for a rival, which lets you hold a customer whose product preference has already shifted. Use it to judge why churn is low despite an inferior product — and remember that switching costs raise the price of winning new customers exactly as much as they raise the price of losing existing ones.

models.name: Aggregation Theory
models.definition: Thompson's claim that when distribution and transaction costs go to zero, the winner is whoever owns the customer relationship, not the supply — so aggregators commoditize their suppliers and capture the surplus at the demand end. Reach for it to explain why the intermediary earns more than everyone producing the actual thing.

models.name: Capital Cycle
models.definition: High returns attract capital, which builds capacity, which crushes returns, which drives capital out, which starves supply and restores returns. Reach for it in cyclical and capital-intensive industries, where the useful signal is not current profitability but the direction of capital flows — the best time to buy is when everyone is closing plants.

models.name: Commoditization
models.definition: The gravitational tendency of every differentiated product to become a substitutable one as knowledge diffuses and rivals copy, until price is the only remaining variable and returns fall to the cost of capital. Reach for it as the default forecast: absent an identified mechanism holding it off, assume any excess return decays.

models.name: Core Competence
models.definition: Prahalad and Hamel's idea that a firm's real advantage is a small set of hard-won capabilities that span products, are hard to imitate, and open onto multiple markets — Honda's engines, Corning's glass. Reach for it when deciding what to outsource and what to never let go of; the failure mode is defining the competence so broadly ('customer focus') that it excludes nothing.

models.name: Cornered Resource
models.definition: Helmer's power: preferential access to a coveted asset on terms better than any rival can get — a mineral deposit, a key patent, an irreplaceable creative team. Reach for it when the advantage is attached to a specific thing rather than a system, and test it by asking what happens if that thing walks out or runs out.

models.name: Counter-Positioning
models.definition: Helmer's power: a newcomer adopts a business model the incumbent cannot copy without damaging its existing business, so the incumbent chooses not to respond — and is right to, on its own numbers, until it is too late. Distinct from disruption in that the barrier is the incumbent's own economics, not its inattention.

models.name: Disruptive Innovation (Christensen)
models.definition: Entrants win not by beating incumbents at their own game but by serving the least attractive customers with a cheaper, initially worse product, then moving upmarket as it improves — while the incumbent, rationally listening to its best customers, cedes the low end every step of the way. Reach for it when an incumbent's most sensible decisions are what will kill it.

models.name: Flywheel / Autocatalysis
models.definition: A self-reinforcing loop where each turn makes the next turn easier — Amazon's lower prices bringing more customers, bringing more sellers, lowering costs again. It is slow to start and nearly unstoppable once spinning, so reach for it when you must decide whether an unimpressive early growth curve is failure or just the first revolutions.

models.name: Generic Strategies (Cost Leadership vs. Differentiation)
models.definition: Porter's claim that a business must choose to be either the low-cost producer or meaningfully differentiated, and that trying to be both leaves it 'stuck in the middle' with neither advantage. Reach for it when a strategy promises premium quality and the lowest price — usually a sign that no choice has actually been made.

models.name: Pricing Power
aka: Price elasticity as a moat test
models.definition: The ability to raise price without losing meaningful volume — the single cleanest observable test that a moat exists, since a business with no advantage cannot survive a price increase. Use it diagnostically: ask when the company last raised prices and what happened, and treat 'we've never tried' as an unresolved question rather than an answer.

models.name: Process Power
models.definition: Helmer's power: embedded organizational routines that deliver superior cost or quality and cannot be copied even when fully disclosed, because they require years of hysteretic change — Toyota's production system, which competitors studied openly and still could not replicate. Reach for it when the advantage survives publication of the playbook.

models.name: Unit Economics
models.definition: Whether a single unit of the business — one customer, one store, one order — earns more over its life than it cost to acquire and serve, expressed as contribution margin, payback period, or LTV against CAC. Reach for it whenever growth is being used as evidence of health: scaling a negative-margin unit does not fix it, it multiplies it.

models.name: Value Chain
models.definition: Decompose an industry into the sequence of activities from raw input to end customer, then ask where the profit actually pools and why — it is rarely spread evenly, and it is rarely where the work is. Use it to locate the choke point you want to own and to notice when your own step of the chain is the one being commoditized.

models.name: Vertical Integration
models.definition: Owning your suppliers or your distribution instead of transacting with them — worth doing when the market for that input is thin, when hold-up risk is real, or when controlling the customer experience is the product. The cost is capital intensity and lost flexibility, so integrate where the transaction genuinely fails, not merely to capture someone else's margin.

models.name: Abstraction Layers
models.definition: Complex systems are made tractable by stacking levels, each of which hides the mechanics of the level below and exposes only a simplified interface — you drive a car without modeling combustion. Use it to pick the right altitude for a problem, and remember the corollary: all abstractions leak, so when behavior gets inexplicable you must drop a level and look at what the abstraction concealed.

models.name: Algorithms
models.definition: A finite, unambiguous procedure that reliably transforms inputs into outputs, so that a result depends on the process rather than on the judgment or mood of whoever runs it. Reach for it when a decision recurs: encoding it as an explicit repeatable rule beats re-deriving it each time and makes errors diagnosable at the step where they occur.

models.name: Conditional Logic (If-Statements)
models.definition: Behavior expressed as explicit condition-action branches — if this state holds, take that action — rather than as vague intent. Writing policy as if-then rules in advance (trigger points, stop-losses, escalation thresholds) removes discretion at the moment when emotion is highest, and forces you to notice the branches you never specified.

models.name: Recursion
models.definition: Solving a problem by applying the same procedure to smaller instances of itself until a base case terminates it. It is the right lens for self-similar structures (org charts, ownership chains, nested arguments, second-order effects of second-order effects) — and the warning attached is that recursion without a base case never halts, which is exactly what runaway feedback and infinite regress look like in the wild.

models.name: Amdahl's Law and Bottlenecks
models.definition: Total speedup is capped by the fraction of work that cannot be parallelized or accelerated, so optimizing anything other than the bottleneck yields nearly nothing. Before adding people, machines, or money, identify the serial constraint — throwing capacity at a non-bottleneck is the most common and most expensive form of busywork.

models.name: Brute Force vs. Heuristics
aka: Exhaustive search vs. rules of thumb
models.definition: Brute force enumerates every possibility and is guaranteed correct but often unaffordable; a heuristic is a cheap rule that is usually right and occasionally badly wrong. The skill is knowing which regime you are in — use brute force when the space is small and the stakes are high, heuristics when the space explodes and being approximately right fast beats being exactly right too late.

models.name: Caching and Memoization
models.definition: Store the result of expensive work so the next request is answered from memory instead of recomputed — the central trade of space for time. It explains why expertise, checklists, and institutional memory are so valuable, and why the hard part is always invalidation: a cache that quietly serves stale answers is worse than no cache at all.

models.name: Combinatorial Explosion
models.definition: The number of combinations grows exponentially with the number of elements, so systems that look modestly complicated have state spaces no one can ever enumerate or test. It is the reason to prefer few moving parts, to distrust plans that assume all interactions were considered, and to expect that any sufficiently configurable system is shipping in a state it was never tested in.

models.name: Compression and Kolmogorov Complexity
models.definition: The shortest description that reproduces a thing is a measure of how much structure it has; regularities compress, noise does not. This makes compression a test of comprehension — if you can restate a body of knowledge far more briefly without losing predictions, you have found the theory, and if you cannot compress it at all, you may be memorizing noise.

models.name: Divide and Conquer
models.definition: Repeatedly split a problem in half and discard the half that cannot contain the answer, turning a linear slog into a logarithmic one. It is the fastest general debugging and diagnosis technique available — bisect the timeline, the codebase, the process, or the candidate list, and always ask what single question would eliminate the most possibilities.

models.name: Exploration vs. Exploitation
models.definition: Every choice under uncertainty trades gathering information about untried options against harvesting the best option known so far, and the optimal mix depends on how much time remains to use what you learn. Explore heavily when the horizon is long (early career, new market, young portfolio) and exploit when it is short — regret usually comes from exploiting too early.

models.name: Garbage In, Garbage Out
models.definition: No amount of processing sophistication rescues bad inputs; a flawless model fed corrupt data produces confident nonsense, and the polish of the output disguises the rot at the source. Reach for it whenever a number arrives with more precision than its inputs could possibly support — audit the feedstock before admiring the machinery.

models.name: Greedy Algorithms and Local Optima
models.definition: Always taking the best immediate step reliably lands you on the nearest hilltop, which is usually not the mountain — improvement stalls not because you are at the top but because every neighboring move looks worse. When progress plateaus, the model says the fix is not more effort in the current direction but a deliberate jump: accept a temporary loss to reach a better basin.

models.name: Information and Entropy (Shannon)
models.definition: Information is measured by how much uncertainty a message removes: the expected message carries almost nothing, the surprising one carries a lot. Use it to price news and evidence — a data source that confirms what you already believed has low information content, and a channel's capacity, not its volume, sets how much can actually get through.

models.name: Redundancy and Error Correction
models.definition: Reliable systems are built from unreliable parts by adding deliberate excess — duplicate components, checksums, parity — so that failures are detected and absorbed rather than propagated. The design question is not whether parts fail but whether a single failure can take the whole system down; buy redundancy where the correlated failure would be fatal, not uniformly.

models.name: Signal-to-Noise Ratio
models.definition: What matters is not the amount of data but the ratio of meaningful variation to random variation within it, and adding volume often adds noise faster than signal. Use it to justify ignoring most inputs — more frequent measurement of a slow-moving quantity mostly increases noise, which is why checking a portfolio daily makes you worse-informed, not better.

models.name: Technical Debt
models.definition: Expedient shortcuts borrow speed today against a compounding interest payment of future friction, and the interest is paid in every subsequent change, not in a single visible bill. It generalizes far past software — deferred maintenance, undocumented processes, hacked-together org structures — and the lesson is that debt is a legitimate instrument only if you are tracking it and intend to repay it.

models.name: Undecidability and the Halting Problem
models.definition: Some questions have no general procedure that answers them — you cannot in general determine whether an arbitrary program will finish without just running it and waiting. The transferable lesson is epistemic humility about prediction: for certain systems there is no shortcut to the outcome, and demands for a guaranteed advance answer are demands for something that provably does not exist.

models.name: A great business at a fair price beats a fair business at a great price
aka: Cigar butts vs. buying better businesses
models.definition: Statistical cheapness ('cigar butts') caps your return at the discount and consumes attention, while a business with durable high returns on capital compounds for you after purchase — so pay up for quality rather than down for junk. Reach for it when choosing between a cheap mediocre asset and a reasonably priced excellent one.
models.mungerCitation: A Conversation with Charlie Munger, University of Michigan Ross, 2017; Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2018, Afternoon Session

models.name: Agency costs (principal-agent problem)
aka: Agency problem / capture of professionals by their paymasters
models.definition: Whenever one party acts for another, the agent's interests diverge from the principal's and the gap is paid for in bad decisions, hidden fees, and self-dealing — especially where the agent is paid by someone other than the person he advises. Reach for it before trusting any recommendation and when designing who reports to whom.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 50th Reunion, 1998; The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight)

models.name: Avoid style companies (fashion risk)
models.definition: Businesses whose demand rests on taste or fashion have no durable claim on the customer — the thing that made them work is the thing that will leave. Reach for it when a company's competitive position is really a current preference.
models.mungerCitation: Acquired podcast, 2023

models.name: Behavioral economics (psychology-informed economics)
aka: Behavioral-economics correction of rational-man models (Thaler; psychology in microeconomics)
models.definition: The rational-man model is a useful first approximation that systematically fails where human psychology predictably deviates from it; economics is only correct when the psychology is bolted back on. Use it when a market or organization behaves in a way "rational actor" reasoning cannot explain.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 50th Reunion, 1998; Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Beta-as-risk / CAPM / modern portfolio theory critique
aka: CAPM / Modern Portfolio Theory (Sharpe; Samuelson randomness)
Capital asset pricing model (named and rejected)
Critique of beta / modern portfolio theory / efficient markets
Skepticism of volatility/'beta' as risk (modern portfolio theory critique)
models.definition: Volatility is not risk; risk is the permanent loss of capital, and models that substitute price wobble for danger (beta, CAPM, MPT) are precise where they should be approximately right. Use it when someone quantifies risk with a number that has nothing to do with the underlying business.
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998; Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Bezzle (Galbraith)
models.definition: Galbraith's insight: between the moment an embezzlement occurs and the moment it's discovered, two people feel rich on the same money — the inventory of undiscovered theft is a real, spending-stimulating asset until it isn't. Reach for it when apparent prosperity rests on claims that haven't yet been checked.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000; The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight)

models.name: Bureaucratic dysfunction (bureaucracy as a cancer)
models.definition: Large organizations generate internal processes that consume effort, diffuse responsibility, and produce stupidity that no individual in them would produce alone — bureaucracy behaves like a cancer that must be actively cut back. Reach for it when explaining why a well-staffed, well-funded institution fails at simple things.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2019, Q14

models.name: Casino take + Ponzi + bubble decomposition
aka: Casino take + Ponzi scheme + speculative bubble decomposition of a high-friction market
models.definition: Munger's way of taking apart a frothy market: separate the frictional skim taken by intermediaries, the chain-letter component paying early entrants from late ones, and the pure speculative premium. Use it to see how much of an apparent return is production and how much is transfer.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Seven Revisited (2006 addendum), Poor Charlie's Almanack

models.name: Comparative advantage (Ricardo) — and its second-order effects
aka: Ricardo's principle of comparative advantage — plus its unexamined second-order effects (China trade)
models.definition: Both parties gain from trade when each specializes where its relative cost is lowest, even if one is absolutely better at everything. Munger accepted the theorem and insisted on the part economists skip: the dynamic, second-order consequences of who accumulates capability over decades.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003; Berkshire AGM remarks on China trade (mid-2000s)

models.name: Creative / competitive destruction
models.definition: New technologies and business models don't just add to the economy, they kill the incumbents — buggy whips, cash registers, textile mills — and the same force that creates your position will eventually come for it. Reach for it when projecting any franchise's life beyond the current decade.
models.mungerCitation: Elementary Worldly Wisdom (USC), 1994 (as 'competitive destruction')

models.name: Culture as a compounding competitive force
models.definition: A strong, transmitted culture (trust, thrift, candor, seamless delegation) lowers an organization's real costs and error rate in ways competitors cannot copy or buy, and it compounds — but it can be destroyed far faster than built. Reach for it when explaining performance differences that the financials and the strategy don't account for.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2021, Q62; Acquired podcast, 2023

models.name: Decentralization short of abdication
aka: Decentralization to the point just short of total abdication (seamless trust management)
models.definition: Push authority down to trustworthy operators until just before the point of total abdication — you buy speed, ownership, and low overhead at the price of selecting people ruthlessly up front. Reach for it in organizational design where a control apparatus would cost more than the errors it prevents.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Managing Subsidiaries)

models.name: Deworsification
models.definition: Expansion into businesses outside your competence — usually by acquisition, usually because the other field looks more attractive from a distance — reliably makes the enterprise worse, not more diversified. Reach for it when a company justifies a deal by breadth, balance, or the appeal of a hotter industry.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2023, Morning Session; 2010 Wesco annual meeting notes, Part I, p.322; Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2010, Afternoon Session (Xerox/Crum & Forster)

models.name: Division of labor / specialization (Adam Smith's pin factory)
aka: Adam Smith's pin factory — division of labor as planned system
models.definition: Splitting a task among specialists multiplies output far beyond what the same people produce working end-to-end — Smith's pin factory as a designed system, not a spontaneous one. Reach for it in organizational design and in choosing a niche where you can be uniquely good.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003; Elementary Worldly Wisdom (USC), 1994

models.name: Double-entry bookkeeping as a civilizational technology
models.definition: Double-entry bookkeeping is one of the few inventions that made large-scale commerce possible at all — an error-checking system that lets strangers trust an enterprise's numbers; degrade the convention and you degrade the economy that runs on it. Reach for it when weighing how much accounting rules matter, or how much a permissive convention costs.
models.mungerCitation: Harvard-Westlake School talk, 2010

models.name: EBITDA and adjusted earnings as corrupted accounting
aka: Accounting as the language of business
EBITDA is lying about the earnings
models.definition: Metrics that exclude real costs — depreciation, stock compensation, 'one-time' charges — are not earnings but advocacy; accounting is the language of business, and a corrupted dialect corrupts the thinking of everyone who speaks it. Reach for it whenever a company's headline profit figure is a non-GAAP construct.
