models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2020, opening remarks; Acquired podcast, 2023; Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2017

models.name: Economics as a complex adaptive system (not physics)
models.definition: Economies are adaptive systems whose participants react to the models made about them, so they have no invariant constants and cannot be reduced to physics-style equations; precision here is usually false precision. Reach for it when an economic or financial model claims physics-grade rigor or exact prediction.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2019, Q28

models.name: Economies of scale / advantages of scale
models.definition: Size confers real cost, information, distribution and psychological advantages (spreading fixed costs, shelf presence, social proof of ubiquity), which is why scale tends to be self-reinforcing in many industries. Reach for it when asking why an incumbent's cost or share position is stable.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 1995, Morning Session

models.name: Extreme maximization or minimization of one or two variables
aka: Extreme maximization or minimization of one or two variables (Costco model)
models.definition: Some businesses win by taking a single variable to a ludicrous extreme — Costco's relentless minimization of price and markup — creating a position competitors cannot match without destroying their own model. Reach for it when designing a strategy that must be structurally, not incrementally, better.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Extreme of good performance over many factors
aka: Extreme of good performance over many factors (Toyota / Les Schwab model)
models.definition: The other winning template: no single dramatic variable, but many small advantages executed relentlessly (Toyota, Les Schwab), whose combination is nearly impossible to copy because there is nothing single to copy. Use it when an obviously excellent competitor has no obvious secret.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Fat pitch discipline (waiting for no-brainers)
models.definition: There is no penalty for not swinging, so wait — sometimes for years — until an opportunity is so obviously good it requires no elaborate argument, then act decisively. Use it when the pressure to be busy is being mistaken for the obligation to be right.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Focus Investing; Berkshire's Acquisition Strategy)

models.name: Febezzle / febezzlement
aka: Febezzlement — the functional equivalent of embezzlement (extends Galbraith's 'bezzle')
models.definition: Munger's extension of the bezzle: the functional equivalent of embezzlement, where no one steals anything yet someone feels rich on wealth that isn't there — unexpensed options, phantom investment gains, foolish spending booked as value. It behaves like a bezzle economically and, being lawful, can grow far larger before it unwinds.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000; Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Float / negative working capital as automatic leverage
aka: Other people's money held at no cost
models.definition: Businesses that collect cash before they pay it out (insurance float, subscriptions, supplier terms) are financed by their counterparties — leverage without a lender, without margin calls, and often at negative cost. Reach for it when asking how a business funds its own growth.
models.mungerCitation: Acquired podcast, 2023

models.name: Foolexures
aka: Foolexures (foolish expenditures praised as economic stimulus)
models.definition: Munger's coinage for foolish expenditures defended on the grounds that they stimulate the economy — waste dressed up as macroeconomic virtue. Use it whenever "it creates jobs" is offered as the justification for spending that produces nothing.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000

models.name: Game theory
models.definition: Model outcomes as the equilibrium of interacting strategies rather than as one actor's plan — your competitor's best response is part of your own payoff. Munger invoked it for antitrust and competitive dynamics: ask what happens after your rivals react.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Gresham's law (bad drives out good)
models.definition: Bad money drives out good — and, in Munger's extension, bad lending practice, bad accounting, and bad morality drive out the good, because the reckless competitor sets the terms everyone else must match to survive. Reach for it whenever the worst actor in a market is defining the standard.
models.mungerCitation: Wesco shareholder letters, 1984 and 1990; Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003
models.mungerQuote: a sort of Gresham's law: bad loan practice drives out good

models.name: Hard-form efficient market theory (named and rejected)
aka: Efficient Market Hypothesis (Fama) as equilibrium finance
Efficient market theory, hard form (named and rejected)
Hard-form efficient-market theory (named as intellectual folly)
models.definition: The claim that prices always fully reflect all information, so no one can beat the market — which Munger treated as a real insight pushed to a false extreme by physics-envy. His usable version: markets are partly efficient and partly inefficient; efficiency is the default, not a law.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 50th Reunion, 1998; Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003
models.mungerQuote: partly efficient and partly inefficient

models.name: Herb Stein's law
models.definition: If something cannot go on forever, it will stop — trends that require impossible arithmetic to continue do not need a trigger to end, only time. Reach for it when a projection's only defense is that it hasn't broken yet.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Indexing
aka: Indexing (passive investing as the default for average investors)
models.definition: Because active management is a negative-sum game after costs, the average investor's rational default is to own the whole market cheaply rather than pay for the attempt to beat it. Use it as the honest baseline any active approach must beat, not merely match.
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998

models.name: Invisible foot (Hardin)
aka: Invisible foot (harmful counterpart to the invisible hand)
models.definition: The malign twin of the invisible hand: the same self-interested behavior, under different rules, produces harm nobody intended — pollution, commons collapse, arms races. Use it to check any invisible-hand argument by asking what the same incentives produce when the costs fall outside the transaction.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 50th Reunion, 1998; Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Invisible hand (Adam Smith)
models.definition: Self-interested actors trading in markets are led, without intending it, to produce broadly beneficial coordination and prosperity. Use it as the baseline expectation for market systems — and as the thing whose failures the invisible foot then explains.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 50th Reunion, 1998; Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Keynesian multiplier
aka: Keynesian stimulus effect (spending multiplier from perceived wealth)
models.definition: One person's spending is another's income, so an increment of expenditure ripples through the economy at some multiple of itself. Munger used it mainly to trace how perceived (even fake) wealth converts into real activity — and to ask what happens when the perception reverses.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000; Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Liquidity as bubble fuel
aka: Liquidity as bubble fuel (skepticism of liquidity's virtue)
models.definition: Contrary to orthodoxy, easy tradability is not an unmixed good: it enables the churn, leverage, and speculation that inflate manias and make crashes violent. Use it to question the assumption that more liquidity always improves a market.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Splitting the stock / liquidity)

models.name: Margin of safety / price versus intrinsic value (Graham)
aka: Buy something worth more than you pay
models.definition: Insist on a gap between what a thing is worth and what you pay, sized to your uncertainty, so that ordinary error or bad luck does not become ruin. Reach for it whenever a decision's payoff depends on an estimate you could plausibly have gotten wrong.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2002, Afternoon Session; Charlie Munger at Caltech, 2020

models.name: Marginal utility / diminishing returns
models.definition: Value is set at the margin, and each additional unit of anything delivers less satisfaction than the one before. Use it to explain why the tenth dollar, hour, or feature is worth far less than the first — and why averages mislead when decisions are made at the edge.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Microeconomics before macroeconomics
aka: Microeconomics before macroeconomics (micro as the anatomy/chemistry of economics)
models.definition: Micro is the anatomy and chemistry of economics; macro is a derived story, and anyone reasoning about the whole economy without command of firm-level behavior is guessing. Reach for it by building economic understanding bottom-up from businesses you can actually observe.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Moat / durable competitive advantage
aka: Moats and the breaching of moats
Win by avoiding change (durability as a moat)
models.definition: A business's structural protection against competitors — brand, scale, switching costs, licence, habit — that lets it keep earning above-normal returns on capital for a long time; the moat, not the manager, is what must be underwritten. Reach for it when valuing a business's future economics, and pair it with the inverse question of what would breach the moat.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2020, Q32; Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2019, Afternoon Session; 2008 Wesco annual meeting notes (Peter Boodell), opening remarks, p.300

models.name: Modern financial engineering (anti-model)
aka: 'Modern financial engineering' (anti-model: maximizing reported earnings by any arguably lawful means)
models.definition: Munger's label for the practice of maximizing reported earnings by every arguably lawful means — an activity that produces nothing, corrupts the reporting system, and eventually detonates. Carry it as a named anti-model: recognize it as a warning sign, never a method.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight), written 2000

models.name: Modern monetary theory
models.definition: The claim that a sovereign currency issuer can print and spend far more than orthodoxy allowed without triggering the predicted consequences — a theory Munger treated as empirically surprising, partly vindicated, and dangerously unbounded at the edges. Reach for it when reasoning about the limits of deficits, printing, and long-run currency depreciation.
models.mungerCitation: CNBC, "Charlie Munger: A Life of Wit and Wisdom," 2023

models.name: Monetary depreciation / long-run inflation
aka: Monetary depreciation / long-run inflation (Roman drachma analogy)
models.definition: Governments debase currency over long spans as reliably as the Romans clipped the drachma, so nominal figures across decades are near-meaningless and real purchasing power is the only honest unit. Use it whenever comparing prices, wages, or returns across long time horizons.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four), 1996

models.name: Mr. Market (Graham)
aka: Making the market your servant, not your guide
models.definition: Treat the quoted market as a manic-depressive counterparty who offers you a price every day, not as an authority on value — his moods create your opportunities and are never your instruction. Reach for it whenever price movement is tempting you to revise your estimate of worth.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2012, Afternoon Session; 2003 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), "Views on Ben Graham's Ideas," p.185

models.name: Niche capacity (crowded strategies leach away)
aka: Too much money chasing the same idea
models.definition: Any profitable niche has a finite capacity: as more capital and imitators pile into the same approach, the excess return is competed away and the strategy stops working at the size that attracted them. Reach for it when a demonstrated edge is being scaled up or has become widely known.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2017, Q22

models.name: Non-egality — concentrate resources on the most able
aka: Non-egality / concentrate playing time in the most able (John Wooden model)
models.definition: John Wooden won by giving the great majority of playing time to his seven best players: output rises when resources flow to the highest-performing units rather than being spread evenly for fairness. Use it in staffing, capital allocation, and any system where equal treatment silently taxes excellence.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Ten, USC Gould School of Law Commencement, 2007

models.name: Opportunity cost
aka: Alternatives (opportunity cost as the decision standard)
Hidden cost of inaction / opportunity cost (Warner & Swasey machine-tool ad)
Hurdle rate as your next best idea
Opportunity cost / decisions by comparison with alternatives
models.definition: The true cost of any choice is the best alternative forgone, so every decision should be made by comparison against your next-best available option — including the option of doing nothing, whose cost is also real but invisible. Munger treated it as a master filter: if a candidate isn't better than what you already own, it fails, whatever its standalone merits.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003; Berkshire AGM remarks (e.g., 2003)
models.mungerQuote: intelligent people make decisions based on opportunity costs

models.name: Pick a tailwind
models.definition: Position yourself where the underlying current runs your way — a growing industry, a demographic trend, a falling cost curve — because ordinary effort in a rising field beats extraordinary effort in a shrinking one. Reach for it when choosing an arena rather than a tactic.
models.mungerCitation: Charlie Munger at Caltech, 2020

models.name: Ponzi / chain-letter mechanics
models.definition: A structure that pays existing participants out of new entrants' money looks like a going concern until the inflow stops, and its collapse is arithmetic, not bad luck. Reach for it whenever returns depend on recruitment rather than production — including lawful versions inside ordinary businesses.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight), written 2000

models.name: Rembrandt effect (stocks priced partly as collectibles)
aka: Dual valuation of stocks: partly like bonds, partly like Rembrandts
Rembrandt effect & wealth effects (price-momentum valuation vs. cash-flow valuation)
models.definition: A stock is valued partly like a bond — off its cash flows — and partly like a Rembrandt, off what it recently sold for and what others will pay. When the Rembrandt component dominates, price momentum becomes the valuation method and the market becomes self-referential.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000; Poor Charlie's Almanack, Ch. 3 (Stocks, Rembrandts, and Bubbles)

models.name: Self-insurance
aka: Self-insurance (rich entities should retain rather than pay to transfer risk)
models.definition: An entity wealthy enough to absorb a loss should retain the risk rather than pay an insurer's margin to transfer it — buying insurance you don't need is a permanent negative-expectancy trade. Reach for it when deciding what to hedge, insure, or outsource.
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998

models.name: Sit-on-your-ass investing
models.definition: Once you own a few excellent businesses, doing nothing beats doing something: you avoid frictional costs, defer taxes, and let the compounding run — activity is mostly a transfer to intermediaries and to the tax authority. Reach for it when the urge is to trade, rebalance, or take a profit.
models.mungerCitation: Acquired podcast, 2023

models.name: Super-competition
models.definition: Some arenas attract so much talent and capital that competitive intensity destroys returns for nearly everyone in them, regardless of skill — the skill just becomes table stakes. Reach for it when choosing which game to enter: the quality of the pond dominates the quality of the fish.
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998

models.name: Switching costs / stickiness
aka: Hard businesses are hard to dislodge from
models.definition: When a customer's cost, risk or hassle of changing suppliers is high — especially in technically hard or deeply embedded businesses — the incumbent keeps the customer without keeping the lowest price. Reach for it when assessing whether a revenue stream is durable or merely current.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2017, Q1 follow-up

models.name: Tax deferral & compounding advantage
models.definition: Holding a compounder for decades and paying tax once at the end beats an identical pre-tax return realized and taxed annually — the deferred tax is an interest-free loan working for you. Use it when comparing strategies with equal gross returns but different turnover.
models.mungerCitation: Elementary Worldly Wisdom (USC), 1994

models.name: The Peter Principle
aka: Promotion to the level of incompetence
models.definition: In hierarchies people are promoted on past performance until they reach a job they can't do, and there they stay — so organizations accumulate incompetence at exactly the levels that matter. Reach for it when diagnosing why capable institutions are staffed with people over their heads.
models.mungerCitation: Harvard-Westlake School talk, 2010

models.name: Tragedy of the commons
aka: Tragedy of the commons (Hardin), including his own 'new form' (locked-in trade with China)
models.definition: When a valuable resource is open to all and owned by none, each user's rational self-interest drives collective ruin — the herdsman adds one more cow because he gets the gain and shares the loss. Reach for it wherever incentives are private but costs are pooled: fisheries, aquifers, shared codebases, open budgets.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 50th Reunion, 1998; Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Transaction costs / boundaries of the firm (Coase)
aka: Coase's transaction costs / boundaries of firm vs. government
models.definition: Firms exist because internal coordination is sometimes cheaper than market transactions; the boundary of the firm sits where those two costs cross. Reach for it in any build-vs-buy, insource-vs-outsource, or market-vs-government question.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Virtuous circle / vicious circle
aka: Virtuous circle / vicious circle (reciprocal feedback between morals and economic growth)
models.definition: Morals and prosperity feed each other in both directions: good behavior raises growth, which supports good behavior — and the same loop runs in reverse once corruption pays. Reach for it when a system's trajectory looks self-reinforcing rather than mean-reverting.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Nine Revisited (c. 2006), Poor Charlie's Almanack

models.name: Wealth effect
aka: Wealth effect (stock-price-driven spending, including pension/401(k) channel)
Wealth effect — and febezzlement as 'wealth effect on steroids'
models.definition: Rising asset prices make people feel richer and spend more, even without realized gains — a channel that runs through pensions and 401(k)s as well as direct holdings, and that links the stock market to the real economy. Reach for it when asset prices and consumption move together.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000; Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Asymmetric wealth effects
aka: Asymmetric wealth effects (downside stronger per dollar than upside)
models.definition: A dollar of paper loss cuts spending harder than a dollar of paper gain lifted it, so the downswing is not the mirror image of the upswing. Use it when forecasting the aftermath of an asset bust from the behavior of the boom.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000

models.name: Benchmark where incentives are strongest and results are measured
models.definition: To learn what actually works, study the settings where the payoff for being right is largest and outcomes are objectively scored — those environments strip out cant faster than any theory. Use it when choosing which evidence or which institution's practice to trust.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Cash-generating vs. capital-consuming businesses
aka: Cash-generating vs. capital-consuming businesses ('profit rusting in the yard')
models.definition: Two firms can report identical earnings while one hands you cash and the other's profit sits in receivables, inventory, and machinery that must be replaced. Reach for it to distinguish a business that funds your life from one that eats every dollar it earns.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (What is the better business)
models.mungerQuote: profit rusting in the yard

models.name: Closed-bid auctions / winner's-curse avoidance
aka: Closed-bid auction mispricing / winner's-curse avoidance ('Don't go' to open outcry auctions)
models.definition: An open-outcry auction is engineered to make the most optimistic bidder win — which means winning is evidence you overpaid. Munger's rule was simply not to go; reach for it in any competitive process designed to extract your maximum.
models.mungerCitation: Harvard Faculty Club Q&A, 1994 (reprinted in Poor Charlie's Almanack)

models.name: Design systems that are hard to defraud
aka: Design systems that are hard to defraud (cash register; easily-gameable systems ruin civilization)
models.definition: The cash register did more for civilized behavior than many sermons: build processes whose structure makes cheating hard, rather than relying on the honesty of the people inside them, because easily-gameable systems corrupt everyone who touches them. Reach for it when designing controls, metrics, or compensation.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Diseconomies of scale (size as an anchor)
aka: Bigness as a drag on returns
Size as an anchor to performance
models.definition: Past some point size cuts the other way: a large capital base shrinks the opportunity set, bureaucracy and territoriality set in, and returns regress toward the average. Reach for it when extrapolating a small entity's past returns onto a much larger one.
models.mungerCitation: 2002 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), "Berkshire's future outlook," p.163

models.name: Distrust of formal projections
aka: Distrust of formal projections (incentive-tainted forecasts as sales tools)
models.definition: Forecasts are produced by people paid for the deal to happen, so the spreadsheet is a sales tool with decimal points; the projection's precision is inversely related to its reliability. Reach for it whenever a decision's case rests on someone else's model of the future.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Failure as capitalism's necessary discipline
aka: Failure as capitalism's necessary discipline ('religion without hell')
models.definition: Capitalism without bankruptcy is like religion without hell — if bad actors and bad decisions are never allowed to die, the system loses the mechanism that makes good behavior pay. Reach for it in any bailout, rescue, or too-big-to-fail argument.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Arthur Andersen)
models.mungerQuote: religion without hell

models.name: Focus investing (concentration)
aka: Few bets, big bets, infrequent bets
models.definition: When genuine edge is rare, size the few positions you understand heavily rather than spreading thinly across many you don't; wide diversification is a confession of ignorance and a guarantee of average results. Reach for it at the position-sizing step, once an opportunity has already passed the quality filter.
models.mungerCitation: 2000 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), "Why Don't More Companies and Investors Copy Berkshire Hathaway?", p.140

models.name: Forensic ratio analysis (effective tax rate as fraud red flag)
aka: Forensic ratio analysis — effective tax rate as fraud red flag (and its deliberate evasion)
models.definition: Fake earnings usually can't pay real taxes, so a firm reporting big profits while paying little cash tax is telling on itself — until it learns to pay real tax on fake income to hide the tell. Reach for it when checking whether reported profit corresponds to anything in the world.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight), written 2000

models.name: Goodhart's law
models.definition: When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure — people optimize the proxy and abandon the thing it stood for. Reach for it before attaching an incentive to any metric; it is the mathematical form of Munger's warning about the power of incentives.
models.mungerCitation: Harvard-Westlake School talk, 2010 (gaming an accounting convention)

models.name: Incentive system design (tailored rewards vs. indiscriminate options)
aka: Incentive system design — precise, tailored performance rewards vs. indiscriminate options
models.definition: A reward should be precisely coupled to the behavior you actually want from that specific person; broad, undifferentiated grants (like blanket stock options) pay people for things they didn't cause and cost more than they buy. Use it when constructing compensation, not merely diagnosing it.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight), written 2000

models.name: Investor behavior gap (manager churning)
models.definition: Investors reliably fire managers after bad stretches and hire after good ones, converting decent fund returns into poor investor returns. Reach for it when explaining why realized outcomes lag reported performance in any system with performance-chasing selection.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000

models.name: Large cash reserves as optionality
aka: Large cash reserves as optionality against adversity and for opportunity
models.definition: Cash earns a low visible return and an enormous invisible one: it lets you survive adversity and buy assets precisely when everyone else is a forced seller. Reach for it when "idle capital" is criticized purely on carry cost.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight), written 2000

models.name: Leverage avoidance (debt fragility)
models.definition: Debt converts survivable setbacks into terminal ones by removing your ability to wait; the leveraged player is solvent only at the market's pleasure. Reach for it whenever a strategy's returns depend on nothing bad happening at the wrong moment.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Berkshire's Culture; Derivatives)
models.mungerQuote: three ways a smart man can go broke: liquor, ladies and leverage

models.name: Market's premium for smooth earnings growth
aka: Market's irrational premium for perfectly smooth earnings growth
models.definition: Investors pay irrationally more for perfectly steady growth than for lumpy growth of equal value, which hands management an enormous incentive to manufacture the smoothness. Reach for it when a company's results are suspiciously regular in a lumpy world.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight), written 2000

models.name: Misallocation of talent
aka: Misallocation of talent (brainpower drawn into unproductive money management)
models.definition: When one activity pays far above its social product, it vacuums up a nation's ablest minds into work that reshuffles claims rather than creating anything. Use it to evaluate whether a sector's compensation reflects value created or rents captured.
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998

models.name: Moral hazard
aka: Temptation from Lax Controls (moral hazard by design)
models.definition: Insulate someone from the consequences of risk and they take more of it — deposit insurance, bailouts, and limited liability all quietly subsidize recklessness. Reach for it when designing any protection, and when explaining why a rescue makes the next crisis worse.
models.mungerCitation: Financial-crisis commentary; Wesco letters on S&L incentives
models.mungerQuote: Bushel baskets of money through the ghetto.

models.name: Obsolescence risk
models.definition: Assets, skills, and business models have finite economic lives, and technology is the usual killer; the cost of obsolescence rarely appears on any balance sheet until it is total. Reach for it when a durable-looking asset's value depends on the world not changing.
models.mungerCitation: Elementary Worldly Wisdom (USC), 1994 (technology-as-killer discussion)

models.name: Price as a quality signal
aka: Price as quality signal (raising price raises volume — Beckman Instruments)
models.definition: For goods whose quality buyers cannot judge — or that are bought to signal status or care — raising the price can raise volume, inverting the ordinary demand curve. Reach for it in luxury, professional services, and gifts, and whenever a low price is reading as evidence of inferiority.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003; Elementary Worldly Wisdom (USC), 1994

models.name: Pricing power (first-sale price control; subcontractor structure)
aka: Pricing power via first-sale price control and subcontractor (not vendee/franchise) structure
models.definition: The party that controls the price at the first sale, and keeps its downstream partners as subcontractors rather than franchisees or vendees, captures the economics of the whole chain. Reach for it when designing distribution or diagnosing who in a value chain actually keeps the money.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four), 1996

models.name: Professions as a conspiracy against the laity
aka: Professions as a conspiracy against the laity (Shaw; professional self-dealing)
models.definition: Shaw's line, taken seriously: professional bodies drift toward restricting supply, obscuring their work, and serving members' income rather than clients' interests. Use it as a prior when a profession's own rules are the only defense of a practice.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Racetrack tout (conflicted helpers)
aka: Racetrack tout analogy for investment helpers
models.definition: An adviser paid for activity, like a tout selling tips at the track, will always have a recommendation — and the average buyer of advice ends up worse off than someone who bet the favorite and went home. Use it to discount any counsel whose giver is paid whether or not it works.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Six Revisited (2006 addendum), Poor Charlie's Almanack

models.name: Raise the price and bribe the purchasing agent
aka: Raise the price and bribe the purchasing agent (agency exploitation; functional equivalents in sales incentives)
models.definition: The other reason a higher price sells more: use the extra margin to reward whoever makes the buying decision on someone else's behalf — legally, via commissions, rebates, and channel incentives. Reach for it wherever the chooser isn't the payer.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Recognizing an achieved optimum (don't tinker)
aka: Recognizing an achieved optimum — don't tinker with a working system/culture
models.definition: When a system or culture is already working near its best, further intervention is almost certainly destructive — the improver's instinct is itself the hazard. Reach for it when the case for change is that change is expected, not that something is broken.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight), written 2000

models.name: Rent-seeking
models.definition: Spending resources to capture a larger share of existing wealth rather than to create new wealth — lobbying, licensing restrictions, litigation — is privately rational and socially pure waste. Reach for it when an industry's profits correlate with political access rather than with what it makes.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2016 (value extraction vs. value creation, 'rats in a granary')

models.name: Scarcity
models.definition: Wants exceed means, which is why prices exist at all and why every allocation is a choice against something else. The root premise beneath opportunity cost, trade-offs, and price formation — reach for it when a plan implicitly assumes an unlimited resource.
models.mungerCitation: Foundational micro Munger endorsed (USC 1994; UCSB 2003)

models.name: Synergy-claims skepticism
models.definition: Synergies are real occasionally and asserted always; the ones in the deal model exist to justify the price, and the dis-synergies never make the slide. Reach for it in any merger, integration, or consolidation argument.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Synergies)

models.name: The business beats the manager (bad economics defeat good management)
aka: A bad business beats a good manager
Reputation of the business survives intact
models.definition: Where the underlying economics are terrible, managerial talent is spent and the industry's reputation survives intact — structure dominates effort. Reach for it whenever a turnaround thesis rests on installing better people into a structurally poor business.
models.mungerCitation: A Conversation with Charlie Munger, University of Michigan Ross, 2017

models.name: Trade-offs
models.definition: There are no solutions, only trade-offs: every gain is bought with something, and a proposal with no listed cost is a proposal whose cost hasn't been found yet. Reach for it as the discipline of naming what you're giving up before you commit.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Ch. 2

models.name: Zero-based budgeting (justify every cost from zero)
models.definition: Rather than inheriting last year's spending and arguing at the margin, require every cost to be re-justified from nothing each period — which surfaces the accreted waste that incremental budgeting protects. Reach for it when an organization's cost base is defended by precedent rather than by present value.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2015 (Forbes notes)

models.name: Adverse selection
models.definition: Offer a single price to a mixed pool and you attract disproportionately the bad risks, because they know what you don't — the reason insurers underwrite rather than just quote. Reach for it whenever the people most eager to accept your terms are the ones you least want.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire insurance-underwriting discussions

models.name: Bubbles
models.definition: Prices detach from underlying value when social proof, easy credit, and recent gains convince participants that buying is safe because others are buying; the correction is proportional to the detachment. Reach for it when the justification for a price is the price's own history.
models.mungerCitation: Wesco/Berkshire meeting remarks, dot-com era

models.name: Elasticity
models.definition: How much quantity moves when price moves — inelastic demand means you can raise price and keep the volume, elastic means you can't. Reach for it before any pricing change, and note that elasticity can be negative-in-sign for status goods.
models.mungerCitation: Implicit in his USC 1994 pricing discussion

models.name: Information asymmetry
models.definition: When one side of a transaction knows more than the other, the informed side extracts the surplus and the uninformed side's rational defense — assuming the worst — can collapse the market entirely (Akerlof's lemons). Reach for it whenever you're the less-informed party, or building trust mechanisms for one.

models.name: Marginal cost / marginal analysis
models.definition: Decisions should turn on the cost and revenue of the next unit, not on averages or sunk totals — and industries where marginal cost is far below average cost invite ruinous price competition. Reach for it in any pricing, capacity, or "should we do one more" question.

models.name: Monopoly, oligopoly and competition
models.definition: Market structure determines profitability more than effort does: monopolists and stable oligopolists keep their surplus, while firms in fragmented commodity markets compete it away to customers. Reach for it to predict returns from the shape of an industry before looking at any single company.
models.mungerCitation: Elementary Worldly Wisdom (USC), 1994 (newspaper/franchise economics)

models.name: Supply and demand
models.definition: Price is the variable that clears the gap between how much is offered and how much is wanted; shortages and gluts are almost always price problems in disguise. Reach for it first in any question about prices, queues, or persistent surpluses — then check the exceptions (price-as-signal, agency) that invert it.
models.mungerCitation: Elementary Worldly Wisdom (USC), 1994 (pricing discussion; not named as a model)

models.name: Time value of money / discounting
models.definition: A dollar now is worth more than a dollar later, so any stream of future value must be discounted to be compared with a present one — the arithmetic underneath every valuation and every capital decision. Reach for it whenever payoffs arrive at different times.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire meeting remarks on discounted-cash-flow logic

models.name: Cumulative advantage (Matthew effect)
models.definition: Early advantage compounds into larger advantage because success attracts the resources that produce more success — to him that hath shall be given. Reach for it when explaining winner-take-most outcomes and why initial conditions matter more than the merit gap suggests.

models.name: Economic efficiency
models.definition: Getting the most output from given inputs, and allocating resources so no one can be made better off without making someone worse off (Pareto efficiency). Reach for it as a yardstick — while remembering it says nothing about whether the resulting distribution is tolerable.

models.name: Externalities
models.definition: Costs and benefits that fall on people outside the transaction don't enter the price, so markets over-produce the harms and under-produce the goods. Reach for it when private accounting says a thing is profitable and the world says otherwise.

models.name: Interdependence
models.definition: In a specialized economy no participant is self-sufficient; each depends on chains of strangers, which makes the system enormously productive and quietly fragile at its links. Reach for it when assessing supply-chain risk or the hidden dependencies behind an apparently simple good.

models.name: Optimization under constraints
models.definition: Economic reasoning is the search for the best attainable outcome given real limits — you cannot maximize everything, only one objective subject to the rest. Reach for it to force a stated objective function when a plan claims to improve every dimension at once.

models.name: Pareto principle (80/20)
models.definition: In most systems a small share of the inputs produces most of the output — customers, defects, revenue, effort. Reach for it to find where concentration of attention pays, but check the actual distribution rather than assuming the ratio.

models.name: Price discrimination
models.definition: Charging different buyers different prices for the same thing — by segment, timing, version, or hurdle — captures surplus that a single price leaves on the table. Reach for it in pricing design, and to explain airline fares, student discounts, and enterprise tiers.

models.name: Prisoner's dilemma
models.definition: A structure where each party's dominant strategy is to defect, producing an outcome worse for both than mutual cooperation would have been. Reach for it when explaining price wars, arms races, or why an obviously beneficial cooperation keeps failing without an enforcement mechanism.

models.name: Public and private goods
models.definition: Goods that are non-rival and non-excludable (defense, basic research, clean air) get under-supplied by markets because no one can charge for them — the structural reason some things are provided collectively or not at all. Reach for it when asking why a valuable thing has no business model.

models.name: Baumol's cost disease
models.definition: Sectors with little scope for productivity growth (live music, teaching, nursing) see costs rise anyway, because they must pay wages competitive with sectors whose productivity is soaring. Reach for it to explain why education and healthcare get relentlessly more expensive without getting worse.

models.name: Deadweight loss
models.definition: Taxes, price controls, and monopoly pricing destroy trades that both parties would have wanted — value that simply vanishes rather than being transferred. Reach for it to distinguish an intervention that redistributes from one that shrinks the pie.

models.name: Free-rider problem
models.definition: When a benefit can't be withheld from non-contributors, each individual's rational move is to let someone else pay — so the good gets under-funded even though everyone wants it. Reach for it in any voluntary collective effort, from open-source maintenance to industry standards.

models.name: Jevons paradox
models.definition: Making a resource cheaper to use often increases total consumption of it, because efficiency lowers the effective price and demand expands to fill the gain. Reach for it whenever efficiency is proposed as a way to reduce total usage.

models.name: Price signals and the knowledge problem (Hayek)
models.definition: Prices aggregate dispersed local knowledge no planner could ever collect, which is why markets coordinate strangers better than committees do — and why suppressing prices destroys the information along with the incentive. Reach for it when a system's coordination failure traces to someone overriding its price signal.

ref.title: The Use of Knowledge in Society

models.name: Regulatory capture (Stigler)
models.definition: Regulators come to be operated for the benefit of the industry they regulate, because the industry has concentrated interest and expertise while the public has diffuse attention. Reach for it before assuming a rule protects the people it names.

models.name: Margin of Safety
aka: Variable 2: Determining the appropriate margin of safety
models.definition: Design the system to withstand loads far beyond the maximum you expect, because your estimate of the load — and of the system's strength — is itself uncertain. Reach for it whenever failure is irreversible or catastrophic: build the bridge for 30,000 pounds when the heaviest truck weighs 10,000, and buy the asset well below your estimate of its worth so that being wrong is survivable.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Derivatives)
models.mungerQuote: The idea of a margin of safety, a Graham precept, will never be obsolete.

models.name: Redundancy / Backup Systems
models.definition: Duplicate the components whose failure would take down the whole system, so that no single failure is fatal — the aircraft carries two engines and three hydraulic circuits. Use it where the cost of an outage vastly exceeds the cost of the spare, and remember that redundancy only helps if the backup fails independently of the primary.
models.mungerCitation: USC Business School, 1994 (named among the engineering models: 'the engineering idea of a backup system')
models.mungerQuote: the engineering idea of a backup system is a very powerful idea
listedBy: Farnam Street (via Margin of Safety citation)

models.name: Technology Is a Killer as Well as an Opportunity
aka: New technology gains flow to the customer
models.definition: New technology is as often a destroyer of the incumbent's economics as a source of advantage: the productivity gain frequently passes straight through to customers via competition, leaving the owner who paid for it no better off — and sometimes worse. Reach for it when assessing whether a technological improvement will actually be retained as durable profit or merely competed away.
models.mungerCitation: Charlie Munger at Caltech, 2020, transcript by Empire Financial Research, https://assets.empirefinancialresearch.com/uploads/2020/12/Munger-Caltech-Interview-transcript-12-18-20-Empire-Financial-Research.pdf

models.name: Engineering-Grade Quality Standards Applied Across Domains
aka: Engineering quality standards applied across domains — bad accounting is bad engineering
Engineering-grade quality control / failure-response standard (Boeing analogy)
models.definition: Engineering holds itself to a standard no other field does — if a plane falls out of the sky, the failure is traced to root cause and permanently eliminated, and nobody accepts 'that's just how it goes.' Import that standard into fields that tolerate chronic failure (accounting, education, medicine, management): bad accounting is simply bad engineering, and should be met with the same intolerance and the same root-cause discipline.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four), July 20, 1996; The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight, written summer 2000)

models.name: Skin in the Game (Pack Your Own Parachute)
models.definition: Reliability improves when the person who designs or sells a system personally bears its failure — the parachute packer who must jump with the chute checks it differently. Reach for it when evaluating incentives around safety-critical or financial systems where the decision-maker can offload the consequences of failure onto others.
models.mungerCitation: 2008 Wesco annual meeting notes (Peter Boodell), opening remarks, p.298, https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf

models.name: Steel Out of the Concrete (Removing Hidden Safety Margin)
aka: Steel-out-of-the-concrete analogy — removing hidden structural safety is moral fraud (and unsound accounting is worse because it spreads)
models.definition: The contractor who quietly pulls rebar out of a bridge pockets a real gain today while transferring an invisible risk to strangers later — the structure looks identical until it collapses. Reach for it whenever performance improves with no visible cost: ask what unseen margin was consumed, and note that unsound accounting is worse than the crooked contractor because the practice spreads by imitation across the whole system.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Eight Revisited (Munger's postscript, Poor Charlie's Almanack)

models.name: Whole-System Engineering Trade-offs
aka: Engineering trade-offs considered as a whole
Optimizing the system, not the component
models.definition: Good engineering optimizes the whole system's trade-offs rather than maximizing one variable in isolation — Munger's defense of the windowless dormitory was that the design must be judged on the totality of what it buys, not on the single feature that offends. Reach for it when a critique or a design decision fixates on one attribute while ignoring the compensating gains elsewhere in the system.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2022, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2022/02/28/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journals-2022-annual-meeting/

models.name: Bathtub Curve / Mean Time Between Failures
models.definition: Failure rates are not constant over life: they are high early (infant mortality from defects), low and flat through useful life, then rise as wear-out sets in. Reach for it when judging the reliability of anything with a lifespan — new and old are the risky phases, burn-in weeds out early defects, and MTBF quoted without reference to the phase is close to meaningless.

models.name: Defense in Depth (Swiss Cheese Model)
models.definition: Stack multiple independent, imperfect barriers so that an accident requires the holes in every layer to line up at once — no single control has to be perfect. Reach for it where perfect prevention is impossible (safety, security, fraud, checklists), and audit the layers for hidden correlation, since barriers sharing a common cause are really one barrier.

models.name: Design Limits and Stress Testing
models.definition: Every system has a rated envelope; you learn where it truly is by deliberately loading it to failure under controlled conditions rather than discovering it in production. Reach for it when the stakes are high and the envelope is assumed rather than measured — the untested limit is a guess, and guesses fail at the worst moment.

models.name: Failure Modes and Effects Analysis (FMEA)
models.definition: Before building, enumerate every way each component can fail, then trace the consequence of each failure through the system and rank by severity times likelihood times undetectability. It is inversion made procedural — reach for it when designing anything whose failure matters, because the failures you never listed are the ones that get you.

models.name: Fault Tolerance and Graceful Degradation
models.definition: Design systems to lose capability in proportion to the damage taken rather than collapsing entirely — the plane that can fly on one engine, the service that serves stale data when the cache dies. Reach for it when total failure is far more costly than degraded operation, and prefer it over brittleness even at the price of peak performance.

models.name: Feedback Control (Closed-Loop Systems)
models.definition: A governor measures the gap between actual and desired output and adjusts input to close it — thermostats, cruise control, PID loops. Reach for it when designing any self-correcting process: the design questions are the setpoint, the sensor's accuracy, the loop's gain, and above all its lag, since delayed feedback turns a stabilizing loop into an oscillating or unstable one.

models.name: Kaizen (Continuous Incremental Improvement)
models.definition: Reliability and cost advantage come mostly from thousands of small compounding improvements made by the people doing the work, not from occasional grand redesigns. Reach for it when you are tempted by a big-bang rewrite: a 1% weekly gain beats a heroic project that may never land, and the incremental path generates learning the leap does not.

models.name: Modularity and Loose Coupling
models.definition: Partition a system into modules with narrow, well-defined interfaces so failures, changes, and understanding stay local instead of propagating system-wide. Reach for it when a system has grown too tangled to change safely — modularity trades a little efficiency for containment of damage and the ability to replace parts independently.

models.name: Normal Accidents (Tight Coupling and Interactive Complexity)
models.definition: Perrow's finding that in systems that are both tightly coupled (no slack, fast propagation) and interactively complex (unexpected interactions between parts), serious accidents are not aberrations but statistically inevitable, and adding safety devices can add complexity that makes things worse. Reach for it when arguing about whether a disaster was someone's fault: often the architecture, not the operator, was the cause — the remedy is loosening coupling or reducing complexity.

models.name: Over-Engineering
models.definition: Margin and complexity have costs — weight, expense, delay, and new failure modes in the safety machinery itself. Reach for it as the counterweight to margin of safety and redundancy: past some point additional protection buys less risk reduction than the risk it introduces, and the correct amount of safety is finite.

models.name: Root Cause Analysis
models.definition: Keep asking why past the first plausible answer until you reach a cause whose removal actually prevents recurrence, rather than treating the symptom or blaming the operator. Reach for it when the same problem keeps coming back — recurrence is proof the previous fix addressed a proximate cause, not the real one.

models.name: Single Point of Failure
models.definition: Any element whose failure alone brings down the whole system — one supplier, one database, one key employee, one bolt. Reach for it as a survey question rather than a design principle: map the system, find the elements with no alternate path, and either duplicate them, decouple from them, or accept the exposure knowingly.

models.name: Statistical Quality Control
models.definition: Measure variation in a process, distinguish common-cause noise from special-cause signal, and fix the process rather than inspecting defects out at the end. Reach for it whenever output quality is erratic — and note Deming's warning that reacting to ordinary noise as if it were signal (tampering) makes variation worse.

models.name: Tolerance Stacking
models.definition: Each part is within spec, but small deviations accumulate across an assembly until the whole no longer fits or works — the failure belongs to no single component. Reach for it whenever many individually acceptable approximations, estimates, or compromises are chained together (financial models, forecasts, org processes): budget the total error, not each piece.

models.name: 'Then I'll do it myself' (Little Red Hen self-education)
models.definition: When the institutions that should have taught you something refuse to, teach yourself and stop waiting. Munger's response to academia's failure to deliver a multidisciplinary education — the model licenses building the toolkit privately rather than lobbying for reform.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 2

models.name: A problem thoroughly understood is half solved
aka: Understanding the problem is half the solution
models.definition: Most of the work of solving a hard problem lies in framing and genuinely understanding it; once stated correctly, the answer is often forced. Reach for it when a team is generating solutions before it can state the problem in one clean sentence.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2020, opening remarks

models.name: Accidental educational experiment (n=1 natural experiment)
aka: Life as an n=1 natural experiment
models.definition: Treat an unplanned life or institutional history as a natural experiment and read its result, rather than waiting for a designed study that will never be run. Munger read his own unorthodox self-education as data; the tool is recognizing that the experiment has already happened.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Being right when the professor is wrong
aka: Properly educated = being right when the professor is wrong
Skepticism toward authority and credentialed nonsense
models.definition: Munger's test of real education: not reciting the expert consensus but being able to identify the occasions when the credentialed authority is wrong, and knowing why. Related to but distinct from Planck-vs-chauffeur knowledge — that one detects fake expertise in others, this one demands you out-reason genuine experts on their own turf when the reasoning fails.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2019, opening remarks; CNBC, 'Charlie Munger: A Life of Wit and Wisdom,' 2023

models.name: Better roughly right than precisely wrong
models.definition: Keynes's rule: an approximate answer to the right question beats an exact answer to the wrong one. Use it to license crude estimation on the questions that matter rather than fleeing to a decimal-place answer on a question that doesn't.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: Big ideas carry the freight
aka: Big ideas carry 95 percent of the freight
Truly big ideas carry most of the freight
models.definition: A small number of truly fundamental ideas explain the overwhelming majority of outcomes, so learning effort should be allocated ruthlessly to them rather than spread evenly across a field. The corollary is that mastering the top ~90 ideas across disciplines beats deep expertise in the trivia of one.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 50th Reunion, 1998; USC Law Commencement 2007; USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom' 1994
models.mungerQuote: eighty or ninety important models will carry about 90 percent of the freight

models.name: Checklist routines
aka: Checklist method — run through the full kit of tools/factors checklist-style
Checklist procedure (organization of psychology into a checklist)
Checklist procedures (different checklists for different companies)
models.definition: Convert your models and factors into an explicit written list and run through it every time, because memory under pressure silently omits exactly the item that matters. Use it whenever a decision is high-stakes, recurring, and multi-factor — different checklists for different classes of problem.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Four Revisited (c. 2006); Talk Five, Harvard Law School 1998; Academic Economics (UCSB) 2003; USC Law Commencement 2007; USC 1994; Wesco annual meeting 2007
models.mungerQuote: I'm a great believer in solving hard problems by using a checklist

models.name: Circle of competence
aka: Circle of competence / three baskets: in, out, and too tough
Know the edge of your competence
Variable 3: Determining the scope of the investor's circle of competence
models.definition: Know the perimeter of what you actually understand and operate inside it; the boundary matters far more than the area. Munger's operational form is three baskets — in, out, and too hard — and the discipline is being willing to throw things in the third basket without embarrassment.
models.mungerCitation: USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom' 1994; Poor Charlie's Almanack Ch. 2–3; BBC interview 2009
models.mungerQuote: three baskets: in, out, too hard

models.name: Darwinian objectivity — destroy your own best-loved ideas
aka: Destroying your own best-loved ideas (Einstein's self-criticism; Keynes on discarding old ideas)
Objectivity and self-criticism (destruction of your own well-loved ideas; Einstein)
models.definition: Make a habit of actively trying to demolish the conclusions you are most attached to, treating a year without a killed favorite idea as a wasted year. It is the working antidote to commitment bias: the ideas you love most are precisely the ones your reasoning has stopped auditing.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four), 1996; Academic Economics (UCSB) 2003; Harvard School Commencement 1986
models.mungerQuote: any year you don't destroy one of your best-loved ideas is a wasted year

models.name: Decide the big 'no-brainer' questions first
models.definition: Simplify a problem by settling the decisions that are obvious once identified, before touching the hard ones — this collapses the search space and often dissolves the remaining difficulty. Munger's first algorithm for practical thought.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four), July 20, 1996

models.name: Decision quality over outcomes (properly playing the hand)
models.definition: Judge a decision by the quality of the reasoning available at the time, not by how the cards fell — Zeckhauser's bridge standard. Essential for learning from results at all: outcome-judging teaches you superstition in any domain with real variance.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 2 (Properly Playing the Hand sidebar)

models.name: Don't risk what you have and need for what you don't have and don't need
aka: Don't live so that a bad day can kill you
You only have to get rich once
models.definition: Refuse any bet whose downside touches what is essential to you, regardless of how attractive the upside looks, because the asymmetry between ruin and incremental gain is total. Reach for it as a gate before sizing any decision with a non-trivial chance of ending the game.
models.mungerCitation: Acquired podcast, 2023 (transcript excerpt via My Money Blog writeup); CNBC, 'Charlie Munger: A Life of Wit and Wisdom,' 2023

models.name: Einstein's simplicity principle
aka: As simple as possible, but no simpler
Simplification (focus on what's knowable and important; avoid unnecessary complexity)
We have a passion for keeping things simple
models.definition: Make everything as simple as possible, but no simpler — strip a model to its load-bearing elements while refusing the false economy of dropping a factor that actually drives the result. Paired with Munger's practice of focusing only on what is both knowable and important and discarding the rest.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003
models.mungerQuote: as simple as possible but no simpler

models.name: Fatal unconnectedness of academic disciplines
models.definition: Whitehead's charge that departments teach their material as if the others did not exist, producing minds with no route from one big idea to another. It is the institutional cause of man-with-a-hammer thinking, and the reason a private latticework has to be built by the individual.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB) 2003; Poor Charlie's Almanack Ch. 3 (Critique of Academia)
models.mungerQuote: fatal unconnectedness

models.name: Fish where the fish are (choose an arena with weak competition)
aka: Don't play where the other people are better
models.definition: Returns come far more from picking a pond stocked with fish and weak fishermen than from out-fishing strong competitors in a crowded one; competitive selection of the arena dominates skill within it. Reach for it before optimizing effort — ask whether the game itself is winnable, not just how to play it better.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2018, opening remarks; Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2017; 2010 Wesco annual meeting notes, Part II, p.328

models.name: Foolish-in-one-way rule
models.definition: An institution or person foolish in one visible respect is rarely foolish only there — folly generalizes within a complex system because the same defective process produced it. Use it as a cheap screening heuristic: one clear instance of nonsense licenses a much broader prior of nonsense.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000

models.name: Full attribution ethos (anti-Kipplingism)
aka: 'Take what you wish' / 'Kipplingism' (borrowing without attribution — anti-model)
Fundamental full attribution ethos of hard science
models.definition: When you borrow an idea from another discipline, credit its source — not for politeness but because unattributed borrowing ('Kipplingism') severs the idea from the theory structure that makes it usable and checkable. Hard science enforces this; soft disciplines mostly don't, and their reasoning degrades accordingly.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 1998; Academic Economics (UCSB) 2003

models.name: Functional equivalence test (substance over form)
models.definition: Ask what a thing actually does, not what it is labelled or how it is accounted for; if two arrangements have the same economic effect they should be judged the same way regardless of formal differences. Munger's blade against accounting fictions, legal formalism, and any scheme whose respectability rests on its label.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Eight Revisited (postscript), Poor Charlie's Almanack

models.name: Fundamental four-discipline combination
aka: Math, physics, chemistry, engineering as the fundamental base
models.definition: Mathematics, physics, chemistry and engineering form the fundamental base of hard science, and each is more fundamental than the ones layered above it — explanations should flow down to that base, never be blocked by disciplinary pride. The ordering principle behind 'reduce to the more fundamental discipline where possible'.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Fundamental organizing ethos of hard science (four-rule system)
models.definition: Munger's four-rule discipline drawn from hard science: (1) rank disciplines by fundamentalness and use the more fundamental to explain the less, (2) master the essential big ideas of each, (3) never reason using only your own discipline's tools, (4) take from other fields, but always with full attribution. Use it as the constitution governing how the whole latticework is assembled.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Gold nuggets vs. placer mining
models.definition: Thomas Hunt Morgan's heuristic: pick up the big obvious findings lying on the ground before grinding through laborious statistical sifting for marginal ones. Reach for it when the analytic machinery is expensive and the large effects are already visible — most fields ignore this and grind anyway.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: If it's trite it's right
aka: Banal maxims survive because they're true
models.definition: Maxims become clichés precisely because they keep proving true; the staleness of a piece of folk wisdom is weak evidence of its correctness, not of its emptiness. Reach for it as a counterweight to the intellectual's reflex of discarding the obvious answer in search of a cleverer one.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2019, Q31

models.name: Inversion
aka: Inversion (forward and reverse thinking, from algebra)
Inversion (invert, always invert; also under Mathematics in the 25iq list)
Inversion / avoidance-first thinking (deciding what NOT to do)
Inversion — 'Invert, always invert' / thinking in reverse (Jacobi)
Inversion — using algorithms forward and backward
models.definition: Solve a problem backward as well as forward: instead of asking how to succeed, ask what would guarantee failure, then avoid it. Reach for it whenever the forward path is crowded or vague — the failure modes are usually fewer, more concrete, and more tractable than the success recipes.
models.mungerCitation: Harvard School Commencement (Talk One), June 13, 1986; also USC 'Elementary Worldly Wisdom' 1994; Academic Economics (UCSB) 2003; USC Law Commencement 2007
models.mungerQuote: Invert, always invert

models.name: Iron prescription (steelmanning)
aka: Iron prescription (state the other side's case better than they can)
models.definition: You are not entitled to hold an opinion until you can state the opposing case better than its ablest proponent can. It converts argument from a defense of your position into a test of it, and it is the cheapest available cure for confirmation bias.
models.mungerCitation: USC Law Commencement, May 13, 2007

models.name: Knowledge filing system (theory-structured retention)
aka: Models that answer the question 'Why?' (theory-structured knowledge)
models.definition: Facts stick only when hung on a latticework of theory that answers 'why'; ideas without a why are unretrievable when you need them. Build the explanatory scaffolding first and file particulars onto it, rather than accumulating particulars and hoping for structure later.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Latticework of mental models
aka: Mental latticework (models used automatically, not exam-prattle)
Models of reality (a latticework of multidisciplinary mental models)
Multiple mental models / big ideas from the big disciplines (~100 models)
models.definition: Hold a framework of roughly eighty to ninety big ideas from the major disciplines, arranged so facts hang on a theory structure rather than floating free, and consult the whole lattice on every problem. It is the master model of this catalog: isolated models are forgettable and misapplied, interlocked ones compound.
models.mungerCitation: A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom, USC Marshall School, April 1994; USC Law Commencement 2007; Poor Charlie's Almanack Ch. 2–3
models.mungerQuote: you must have a latticework of models in your head

models.name: Learning machine
aka: Lifelong learning machine / get a little wiser every day
models.definition: Treat yourself as a system that must get measurably wiser every day, compounding small increments of understanding over a long runway; nobody who stopped learning stayed ahead. Munger's claim is that Berkshire's record is unexplainable without it — the returns came from continuous re-learning, not from a fixed 1960s toolkit.
models.mungerCitation: USC Law Commencement, May 13, 2007; Poor Charlie's Almanack Ch. 3 (Learning Process)

models.name: Luck vs. skill (the duck on the rising pond)
aka: The duck on the rising pond
models.definition: Separate the contribution of a rising tide from the contribution of your own paddling before attributing an outcome to skill — the duck that rose with the pond did not fly. Reach for it when evaluating a track record, your own or someone else's, in a period when the whole environment was moving in one direction.
models.mungerCitation: A Conversation with Charlie Munger, University of Michigan Ross, 2017; Acquired podcast, 2023

models.name: Method of invention → method of learning (Whitehead)
models.definition: Whitehead's observation that civilization advanced most when people discovered the method of invention itself; the personal analogue is that the biggest return comes from learning the method of learning rather than any particular content. Reach for it when deciding what to study: meta-skill beats subject matter.
models.mungerCitation: USC Law Commencement, May 13, 2007

models.name: Mistakes of omission vs. commission
models.definition: The errors that never show up in the record — the great opportunity you understood and didn't take, the position you bought with an eyedropper — usually cost more than the visible blunders. Munger called these his worst mistakes; reach for it because omission errors are invisible unless you deliberately audit for them.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Mistakes)
models.mungerQuote: sucking my thumb

models.name: Multidisciplinary approach
aka: Elementary academic wisdom used in a multidisciplinary manner (anti-balkanization of disciplines)
Multidisciplinary approach (big ideas of the big disciplines)
Multidisciplinary approach / grabbing the big ideas from all disciplines
Multidisciplinary worldly wisdom (A + B: narrow doctrine plus big ideas from other disciplines)
models.definition: Deliberately import the big ideas of every major discipline into your own, because problems do not respect departmental boundaries and your home discipline's toolkit is always incomplete. Munger's formula: master your own narrow doctrine (A), then add the big ideas from outside it (B), and refuse to explain anything using only A.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 50th Reunion, 1998; Academic Economics (UCSB) 2003; USC Law Commencement 2007

models.name: Opportunity comes to the prepared mind
models.definition: Pasteur's principle: chance favors the prepared mind — the big opportunities arrive rarely and unannounced, and only a mind already stocked with the right models will recognize one in time to act. The justification for continuous learning during periods when nothing is happening.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Focus Investing)

models.name: Organized common (and uncommon) sense
models.definition: Much of worldly wisdom is just common sense held in an organized structure and applied reliably — plus the uncommon sense to tune out the folly around you. Munger's framing of what the latticework actually produces: not cleverness, but ordinary sense that stays switched on.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Common (and uncommon) Sense)

models.name: Patience plus aggressive opportunism
aka: Gumption — courage to bet big on a cinch
The Munger system: sift life for a few opportunities, then act aggressively
The trip to the pie counter
You make your money by the waiting
models.definition: A two-phase decision system: wait, mostly idle, through long stretches of unattractive opportunities, then bet very heavily on the rare occasions when a near-certainty appears. Patience without the willingness to swing hard is as useless as aggression without the discipline to wait — reach for it when the temptation is to stay busy rather than stay ready.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2017, Q22; Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2016; Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2013; CNBC, 'Charlie Munger: A Life of Wit and Wisdom,' 2023; 2005 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), p.236; 2003 Wesco annual meeting notes, p.178

models.name: Physics envy / false precision
aka: Craving for false precision in complex systems
Limits of mathematical modeling outside physics (physics-envy critique)
models.definition: The craving to make soft, complex systems look like physics produces elaborate mathematics resting on assumptions that do not hold, and the rigor is entirely in the notation. Reach for this whenever a model's precision greatly exceeds the precision of its inputs — economics, finance, and risk modeling are the usual sites.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003; Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000

models.name: Pilot-training education system (six-element model)
aka: Pilot-training six-element education system (aviation model for elite education)
models.definition: Aviation's training design is the template for any elite education: broad formal knowledge, fluency to the point of automaticity, thinking both forward and backward, training weighted to where failure is most costly, and mandatory routine maintenance of perishable skills. Use it as a design spec whenever the cost of a mental malfunction is high.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Planck knowledge vs. chauffeur knowledge
aka: Planck's chauffeur story (real knowledge vs. memorized patter)
models.definition: Distinguish people who genuinely understand a subject from those who have merely memorized the patter and can perform it convincingly. Ask a question off the script: chauffeur knowledge collapses instantly, and most confident public expertise is chauffeur knowledge.
models.mungerCitation: USC Law Commencement, May 13, 2007; Academic Economics (UCSB) 2003

models.name: Practice-based fluency
aka: Continuous practice of mental routines (concert pianist)
models.definition: Knowledge must be drilled to automatic recall, not merely to test-passing level, or it will not be there under load — the concert pianist practices daily precisely because the skill is not stored, it is maintained. Distinguishes usable models from exam-prattle.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School 1998; USC Law Commencement 2007

models.name: Ragging the problem
aka: Iterative attack with incubation on hard problems
models.definition: On a hard problem, attack it repeatedly, then set it down and let it incubate below conscious attention, returning again and again until it yields. Munger's model for problems that do not surrender to a single sitting — the answer often arrives during the interval, not the effort.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: Raisins-and-turds contamination heuristic
aka: Small admixtures of fraud corrupt the whole
models.definition: Mix raisins with turds and you still have turds — a small quantity of the contaminant defines the whole, and the good parts do not redeem it. Use whenever someone argues that a mostly-sound arrangement can carry a corrupt component; in institutions and accounting, it can't.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Earnings Manipulation)

models.name: Rub your own nose in your own mistakes
models.definition: Deliberately revisit your own errors rather than repress them, institutionalizing the review so the lesson is paid for only once. Distinct from vicarious learning (which mines others' mistakes), this asserts that first-person shame, faced directly, is the highest-yield teaching device available — reach for it after any loss you'd rather explain away.
models.mungerCitation: Charlie Munger at Caltech, 2020 (Empire Financial Research transcript); 2006 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), section 'Learn at the Temple of Rationality,' p.264; Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2017

models.name: Second- and higher-order effects
aka: Consequences (evaluate immediate and long-term, intended and unintended effects)
Consequences of consequences (second-order effects; hedging spiral)
Second-order effects / undesired side effects of good things
models.definition: Ask 'and then what?' repeatedly — the consequences of consequences frequently swamp and reverse the first-order effect you were optimizing for. Essential wherever incentives, markets, or regulations feed back on themselves; Munger's derivatives-hedging spiral is the canonical case of first-order prudence producing second-order catastrophe.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB) 2003; Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998; Poor Charlie's Almanack Ch. 3 (Derivatives)
models.mungerQuote: consequences of consequences

models.name: Seek disconfirming evidence (Darwin's golden rule)
aka: Evidence (seek disconfirming evidence; Darwin's golden rule; falsification)
models.definition: Record and dwell on facts that cut against your hypothesis the moment you encounter them, because contrary evidence is what the mind discards fastest and quietest. Distinct from general self-criticism: this is a mechanical recording habit, not an attitude — Darwin wrote such facts down immediately, knowing he would otherwise forget them.
models.mungerCitation: Harvard School Commencement, 1986; Harvard 1995 (Psychology of Human Misjudgment)
models.mungerQuote: A year in which you don't destroy one of your best-loved ideas is a wasted year.

models.name: Skill-maintenance routine (simulator model)
aka: Knowledge-maintenance routine / aircraft-simulator model (preventing skill atrophy through disuse)
