models.definition: Rarely used knowledge atrophies through disuse, so schedule deliberate refreshers for the skills you need only in emergencies — pilots use simulators for exactly the maneuvers that never come up in normal flying. Reach for it for any competence whose need is infrequent but critical.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Socratic solitaire
models.definition: Interrogate yourself in writing — pose the hard questions, answer them, then attack the answers — as a solo substitute for a genuine adversary. Munger structured whole talks this way; use it when no one competent will argue with you.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Synthesis — making models interact
aka: Intradisciplinary and interdisciplinary synthesis (Faraday-experimenter vs Maxwell-synthesizer)
models.definition: The payoff of a multidisciplinary stock of models comes when you make them collide on one problem and see which combination explains the result; synthesis, not collection, is the skill. Munger's Faraday-vs-Maxwell contrast: the experimenter accumulates, the synthesizer unifies — and unification is where the leverage is.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics (UCSB) 2003; Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four), 1996

models.name: Take a simple, basic idea and take it very seriously
aka: Take a simple idea and take it seriously
models.definition: Munger's two-part rule for his own success: the edge comes not from esoteric ideas but from applying elementary ones with a consistency almost nobody sustains. Use it as a check on novelty-seeking — the failure is rarely that you lacked the concept, it's that you didn't act on the one you had.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Take-what-you-wish augmentation
models.definition: Actively raid other disciplines for whatever tools you need rather than waiting for their permission or their curriculum, then assimilate them into your own working repertoire. The practice half of the cross-disciplinary ethos — pair with full attribution, which is the ethical half.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: The fundamental algorithm of life: repeat what works
aka: Figure out what works and do it (Lee Kuan Yew's mantra)
models.definition: Munger's explicitly labelled 'fundamental algorithm of life': identify what demonstrably works, do more of it, and stop doing what doesn't — a pragmatic, evidence-first rule that overrides ideology and theory. Reach for it when a decision is being argued from doctrine rather than from a track record.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2010, Afternoon Session; Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2019, Morning Session; Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2021, Q43

models.name: Thought experiment
models.definition: Reason through a hypothetical constructed to isolate one variable, extracting conclusions no experiment could cheaply deliver. Munger explicitly names it as a reasoning tool and credits Einstein's use of it; reach for it when the real-world test is impossible, unethical, or too slow.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000

models.name: Trial and error (empirical iteration)
aka: Trial and error for product optimization
models.definition: Where theory cannot tell you the answer, run cheap experiments and let outcomes decide — Munger's Coca-Cola thought experiment optimizes flavor by iteration, not deduction. Reach for it when the variable space is large, the theory is weak, and each test is cheap.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four), July 20, 1996

models.name: Two-track analysis
models.definition: Run every decision down two tracks at once: (1) what do the rational factors governing the situation actually say, and (2) what subconscious influences are pushing my brain toward an automatic and possibly useless conclusion. The second track is the one people skip, and it is where most large errors live.
models.mungerCitation: USC 'A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom', 1994
models.mungerQuote: Personally, I've gotten so that I now use a kind of two-track analysis

models.name: Vicarious learning (standing on the shoulders of giants)
aka: Make friends among the eminent dead
Standing on the shoulders of giants
models.definition: Learn from other people's catastrophes rather than your own, and study the best predecessors in any field before attempting it — personal experience is the most expensive tutor available. Reading biography and post-mortems is a direct substitute for paying tuition in ruin.
models.mungerCitation: Harvard School Commencement (Talk One), June 13, 1986
models.mungerQuote: make friends among the eminent dead

models.name: Warner & Swasey rule (the tool you need but haven't bought)
aka: Warner & Swasey rule applied to thinking tools
models.definition: 'The man who needs a new machine tool and hasn't bought it is already paying for it' — applied to thinking, the mental model you lack is already costing you, invisibly, in worse decisions. The argument for acquiring models before you can name the problem they will solve.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000

models.name: Complexity limits order-seeking
models.definition: Max Planck abandoned economics because he could not stand the mess — some systems are too complex and reflexive to yield the clean order a physicist expects, and demanding that order produces false models. Use it to set expectations before modeling social systems: the right answer may be a range and a shrug.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: Consistently not stupid beats trying to be brilliant
aka: Avoid stupidity rather than seek brilliance
models.definition: Long-run advantage comes from systematically removing standard stupidities rather than from occasional genius, because the errors compound and the brilliance doesn't. Munger's stated explanation of Berkshire's record and a direct application of inversion to life strategy.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Keys to Our Success)

models.name: Cross-checking against an independent system
aka: Cross-checking one system against an independent one (tax law's common sense vs. GAAP)
models.definition: Verify a conclusion by testing it against a system built on entirely different assumptions — agreement is meaningful, divergence is a red flag. Munger checked GAAP treatments against tax law's cruder common sense, on the logic that two independent errors rarely coincide.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight), written summer 2000

models.name: Existence proof — 'I've seen it done'
models.definition: A single confirmed instance settles the question of possibility and shifts the debate from 'can it be done' to 'why isn't it'. Munger used his own life as the proof that a multidisciplinary self-education works; use it to dismantle claims of impossibility cheaply.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Face reality even when you don't like it
aka: Face reality especially when you don't like it
models.definition: Especially when the truth is unwelcome, look at it directly — the discomfort is precisely the signal that denial is operating and that the cost of continuing is compounding. Munger treats this as a prerequisite for every other thinking tool: no model works on facts you refuse to admit.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000

models.name: Hard work as precondition for insight
models.definition: The flashes of judgment that look like intuition are the residue of unglamorous preparation — sitting with the material long enough that the pattern becomes visible. Counterweight to the fantasy that good models substitute for work; they only make the work compound.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 2 (evaluation-process narrative)

models.name: Hypothesize-and-test with consistency to established principles
models.definition: The scientific method's working discipline: form a hypothesis, test it, and require that any accepted conclusion be consistent with the established results of the more fundamental disciplines. The consistency requirement is the part people skip — it is what rules out most bad theories for free.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Importance-weighted training allocation
aka: Train hardest where malfunction damage is greatest
models.definition: Train hardest on the areas where a malfunction would cause the most damage, not on the areas that are most fun or most frequently encountered. Reallocates learning effort by consequence rather than by frequency — the pilot drills engine failure, not cruise.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Paradox as a signal
aka: Paradox as a signal — either you've erred or you're fruitfully at the edge of your discipline
models.definition: When your reasoning yields a contradiction, one of two things is true: you have made an error, or you are standing productively at the edge of your discipline. Either way the paradox is information — treat it as a location marker, not an annoyance to be argued away.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: Probabilistic thinking
aka: Elementary math of permutations and combinations
models.definition: Reason in odds and expected values rather than in binaries, using the elementary math of permutations and combinations and decision trees as Fermat and Pascal set it out. Munger's claim is that anyone who cannot do this walks through life like a one-legged man in an ass-kicking contest.
models.mungerCitation: USC 'A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom', 1994
models.mungerQuote: one-legged man in an ass-kicking contest

models.name: Prospective hindsight (premortem)
aka: Prospective hindsight / premortem via fictional future history
models.definition: Assume the disaster has already happened and write its history — the imagined certainty of failure unlocks specific causes that a forward-looking risk survey never surfaces. Munger wrote an entire fictional future history of a financial scandal in 2000 and watched much of it arrive; use before committing to any irreversible plan.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight), written summer 2000

models.name: Push the insight to its next logical level
models.definition: Take a true principle and extend it to the extreme case, where its structure becomes visible and its limits become testable. A generator of new understanding — most people stop at the first useful implication, and the third one is usually where the value is.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000

models.name: Rules and filters
aka: Rules and filters (checklists and decision filters)
models.definition: Pre-commit to simple screens that reject the great majority of candidates before any detailed analysis, so that scarce judgment is spent only on survivors. Distinct from a checklist: a checklist ensures you consider everything, a filter ensures you consider almost nothing — Berkshire's 'too hard' pile and opportunity-cost screen are filters.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2022, Afternoon Session

models.name: Occam's razor
models.definition: Among competing explanations that fit the evidence equally well, prefer the one requiring the fewest assumptions — not because simple is true, but because each extra assumption is another way to be wrong. A tiebreaker, not a proof; a simpler theory that fits the data worse is not favored.
models.mungerCitation: Supporting usage only: simplicity dictums on record; no known naming of Occam
models.mungerQuote: take a simple idea and take it seriously

models.name: Vivification
models.definition: Making an idea vivid and concrete to lodge it in memory and drive action — the deliberate use of the same extra-vivid-evidence channel that Munger identified as a source of misjudgment. Double-edged: it is a persuasion tool precisely because it bypasses proportionate weighing.

models.name: Base rates / outside view
aka: Base Rates from History (the outside view)
models.definition: Before reasoning about the specifics of your case, ask how similar cases have historically turned out and anchor there — the inside view's rich detail is exactly what makes it overconfident. The single cheapest improvement available to most forecasts, and the one people resist hardest because their case feels different.
models.mungerCitation: The 'base rates' framing is Kahneman's; Munger's nearest named model is elementary Fermat/Pascal probability (USC 1994)

models.name: Black swan events
models.definition: Taleb's model of rare, high-impact events that lie outside the range of past observation and are rationalized as predictable only afterward. The practical content is not forecasting them but structuring exposure so that one cannot end you — Munger spoke of fat tails rather than adopting the term.

models.name: Connectionism (network model of learning)
aka: Connectionism / neural-network model of learning (Thorndike stimulus-response; brain as latticework)
models.definition: Learning as the strengthening of connections between units rather than the filing of discrete facts — the brain is itself a latticework, which is why isolated knowledge is not retrievable. Offers the mechanistic case for why Munger's structural metaphor is more than a metaphor.

models.name: Deduction, induction, abduction
models.definition: Three distinct inference modes with three distinct warrants: deduction guarantees a conclusion given the premises, induction generalizes from cases and can always be overturned, abduction picks the best available explanation and is the weakest of the three. Knowing which one you just performed tells you how much confidence you are entitled to.

models.name: Dysrationalia and mindware gaps
aka: Rationality / dysrationalia and mindware gaps (Keith Stanovich, David Perkins)
models.definition: Stanovich's construct: rationality is separable from intelligence, and smart people reason badly either from missing 'mindware' (they never learned the model) or from failing to deploy what they have. Explains why raw IQ is a poor predictor of good judgment — and why a latticework is a real intervention.

models.name: First principles thinking
models.definition: Decompose a problem to the assumptions you can verify as fundamentally true, then reason up from there rather than by analogy to what already exists. Expensive and slow — use it where convention is likely wrong or the analogy class is contaminated, not for routine decisions.

models.name: Fox vs. hedgehog
aka: Fox vs. hedgehog cognitive styles (Isaiah Berlin via Philip Tetlock)
models.definition: Berlin's distinction, made empirical by Tetlock: hedgehogs know one big thing and force everything through it; foxes know many small things and stitch. Foxes forecast measurably better and get invited on television measurably less — the model is a direct argument for the latticework over the single grand theory.

models.name: Goals (clear, achievable objectives)
models.definition: Specify what you are actually trying to achieve, in terms concrete enough that you could tell whether you got there and by when. Sounds trivial and is routinely skipped — most bad reasoning is well-executed analysis of an unstated or wrong objective.

models.name: Gray rhino events
models.definition: Wucker's counterpart to the black swan: the large, obvious, high-probability threat charging straight at you that everyone sees and nobody acts on. Reach for it when the failure is one of will and coordination rather than of knowledge.

models.name: Hanlon's razor
models.definition: Never attribute to malice what is adequately explained by incompetence, haste, or indifference — malice requires coordination and motive that most situations don't supply. Useful mainly because assuming malice triggers reciprocal escalation and closes off the cheap fix.

models.name: The map is not the territory
models.definition: Korzybski's principle: every model is a reduction of reality, and the reduction is where the danger lives — the map's clean edges are artifacts of the mapping, not features of the ground. Reach for it whenever a metric, model, or abstraction starts being defended against the reality it was built to describe.

models.name: Chesterton's fence
aka: Don't tear down a fence until you know why it's there
models.definition: Do not remove a fence until you know why it was put there — the apparent absurdity of an existing arrangement is usually evidence of your ignorance, not of its uselessness. The check on first-principles enthusiasm: reformers who skip this step reliably rediscover the original reason the hard way.

models.name: Decision journal
models.definition: Record the decision, the reasoning, the expected outcome and your confidence at the time — before you learn the result. It is the only defense against hindsight bias rewriting your memory of what you actually believed, and the only way to make decision-quality-over-outcomes auditable rather than merely aspirational.

models.name: Falsifiability (Popper)
models.definition: A claim is scientific only if it forbids something observable — if no possible evidence could refute it, it explains nothing however well it seems to fit. Use it as a first screen on any theory: ask what result would prove this wrong, and treat inability to answer as disqualifying.

models.name: Feynman technique
models.definition: Test understanding by explaining the thing in plain language to a novice; wherever you reach for jargon or hand-waving, that is exactly the gap. The operational test for whether you hold Planck knowledge or chauffeur knowledge.

models.name: Hamming questions
models.definition: Richard Hamming's pair: what are the important problems in my field, and why am I not working on them? The point is that effort flows to tractable problems by default, and importance has to be chosen deliberately or it never gets chosen at all.

models.name: Illusion of explanatory depth
models.definition: People believe they understand how things work — zippers, tax policy, markets — in far more detail than they do, and the illusion collapses the instant they try to produce the mechanism. Reach for it before trusting your own confidence: demand the causal chain out loud, not the conclusion.

models.name: Necessary vs. sufficient conditions
models.definition: A necessary condition must be present for the outcome; a sufficient one guarantees it; confusing the two produces both wasted effort and false confidence. Most success formulas are lists of necessary conditions being sold as sufficient ones.

models.name: Overton window
models.definition: At any moment only a bounded range of positions is sayable in public, and the boundary moves over time — which means the range of ideas you can hear being discussed is a poor proxy for the range that is true or possible. Useful as an epistemic correction: absence from the discourse is not evidence of absence.

models.name: Regret minimization framework
models.definition: Project yourself far forward and ask which choice you will regret least from there, which strips out the short-term social and status noise dominating the decision now. Best suited to large, irreversible, identity-level choices; useless for routine ones.

models.name: Via negativa
models.definition: Improvement by subtraction: removing the harmful, the fragile, and the stupid is more robust than adding the beneficial, because you know more about what fails than about what works. The general form of Munger's 'avoid stupidity' and of inversion applied to design.

models.name: Cicero's History Rule (ignorance of what came before you were born)
aka: Cicero's history rule, generalized to all disciplines
know the history of your discipline
models.definition: Cicero's dictum that to not know what happened before you were born is to remain forever a child; Munger generalized it into an obligation to master the founding history of every discipline you use, because a technique detached from the story of how and why it was invented is a technique you cannot judge the limits of. Reach for it when you are handed a formula, a regulation, or an institutional practice with no account of the problem it was built to solve.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Ten: USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, 2007

models.name: Confucian Family Ethic as Social Technology
models.definition: The view that an inherited ethical code — here Confucian obligations of family duty, thrift, and education — functions as real economic and social infrastructure, producing savings rates, low crime, and human-capital investment that formal institutions cannot legislate into existence. Reach for it when explaining persistent national or organizational performance gaps that policy variables alone fail to account for.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2019, Q26, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2019/03/03/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journal-annual-meeting-2019/

models.name: Franklin's Warning (when citizens can vote themselves money, the Republic nears its end)
aka: voting yourself money from the public purse
models.definition: The founding-era warning that once a majority discovers it can vote itself benefits from the treasury, the fiscal discipline holding a republic together erodes and collapse follows. Reach for it when evaluating the long-run stability of any system where the beneficiaries of spending also control the spending decision.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2022, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2022/02/28/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journals-2022-annual-meeting/

models.name: Imitation of Worthy Exemplars (and avoidance of unworthy ones)
aka: Imitation of worthy exemplars (Ben Franklin's model vs. Bernie Cornfeld's)
models.definition: Historical figures function as behavioral templates: deliberately choose whom to copy (Franklin) and whom to treat as a cautionary anti-model (Cornfeld), because social proof and role-modeling operate whether or not you select the model consciously. Reach for it when designing your own conduct or an institution's culture — the exemplars people actually have in their heads drive behavior more than the rules do.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Six: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998

models.name: Making Friends With the Eminent Dead (learning via biography)
aka: making friends among the eminent dead
models.definition: Acquire mentors and cognitive models by reading the lives of great thinkers rather than by meeting people, since biography lets you associate with the best minds in history and attach ideas to a human story that makes them stick in memory. Reach for it as the default cheap substitute for scarce access to living expertise, and as a memory technique for abstract ideas.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (The Importance of Reading)

models.name: Plato on Demagogues and the Decay of Democracy
models.definition: Plato's claim that democracies carry the seeds of their own decay: license and flattery of the crowd empower demagogues, who convert popular enthusiasm into tyranny. Reach for it when a political or organizational system is rewarding the loudest promiser over the most competent steward, as an ancient precedent that the pattern is structural rather than novel.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2022, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2022/02/28/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journals-2022-annual-meeting/

models.name: Failure Prompts Reform
aka: crisis as the precondition for repair
systems reform only after they break
models.definition: The historical regularity that societies and institutions rarely fix known defects until a visible failure forces the issue — reform is downstream of catastrophe, not of foresight. Reach for it both to predict that a diagnosed-but-tolerated problem will persist until it breaks, and to anticipate that the reform arriving after the break will be shaped by panic rather than analysis.
models.mungerCitation: Harvard-Westlake School talk, 2010, https://pdfcoffee.com/110302239-munger-talk-at-harvard-westlakepdf-pdf-free.html

models.name: Historical Analogy and Its Limits
models.definition: Reasoning from a past case to a present one ('this is another Munich', 'another 1929') imports the analogy's whole causal structure whether or not it fits, so the discipline is to state explicitly which features are shared and which are not before drawing the conclusion. Reach for it whenever a decision is being argued primarily by naming a historical episode — the analogy is doing hidden work that should be made explicit and tested.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2018, Q17, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2018/02/19/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journal-annual-meeting-2018/

models.name: Historical Pattern-Matching from Extreme Cases (the Japan precedent)
aka: Historical pattern-matching — Japan's 1990s post-false-accounting stagnation as precedent
Learning from extreme historical cases (Japan's stalled economy; wealth effects in reverse)
models.definition: When a system produces an extreme, hard-to-explain outcome — Japan's decade-plus stagnation after a credit and false-accounting boom, despite every conventional stimulus — treat it as a live precedent that bounds what is possible in your own system, including wealth effects running in reverse. Reach for it whenever someone argues an outcome cannot happen here; the extreme case is the highest-information data point history offers.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Seven: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000; Talk Eight: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (written 2000)

models.name: Loss-of-Dominance Rate Is ~100%
aka: the top companies of one era are gone the next
models.definition: The historical base rate that dominant firms (and by extension nations and institutions) almost never retain dominance across long horizons — run the list of leaders from a century ago and nearly all have lost position. Reach for it as a prior against extrapolating any current franchise's durability, and when pricing terminal-value assumptions or succession risk.
models.mungerCitation: 2003 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), section "Berkshire Without Buffett," p.180, https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf

models.name: Pax Nucleana (deterrence as the source of the long peace)
models.definition: The reading of post-1945 history in which the absence of great-power war is explained not by moral progress but by weapons so destructive that initiating war became irrational for all sides. Reach for it when a long run of good outcomes tempts you to credit improved character rather than an underlying incentive structure that could change.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2022, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2022/02/28/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journals-2022-annual-meeting/

models.name: Sodom and Gomorrah Pattern (moral decay precedes practical collapse)
aka: Sodom and Gomorrah pattern (moral decay in finance presages practical consequences)
models.definition: Widespread moral rot in a financial or institutional culture reliably precedes concrete practical consequences, even when the accounts still look fine and no law has visibly been broken; the ethical decay is the early signal and the crash is the lagging one. Reach for it when a system's numbers are healthy but its norms are visibly deteriorating — history's verdict is that the norms lead.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Seven: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000

models.name: South Sea Bubble Lesson (markets need not be casino-fast)
aka: Financial-history lesson: South Sea Bubble aftermath — markets need not be casino-fast
models.definition: After the South Sea Bubble, Britain banned public stock trading for roughly a century and still led the Industrial Revolution — proof that a society can restrain speculative liquidity without forfeiting economic progress. Reach for it against the claim that any friction on trading or speculation must harm capital formation; the historical record says the causal link is far weaker than assumed.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: The Method of Invention (Whitehead)
aka: invention of the method of invention
models.definition: Whitehead's observation that the decisive breakthrough of the 19th century was not any particular invention but the invention of the method of invention itself — a systematic, repeatable process for generating discoveries. Reach for it when assessing why progress accelerates in some eras and institutions and stalls in others: look for whether the underlying discovery process has been engineered, not just whether outputs appeared.
models.mungerCitation: 2007 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), opening remarks, p.276, https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf

models.name: "This Time Is Different" Fallacy
models.definition: The recurring conviction, at the top of every credit boom, that structural change has repealed the old rules — new technology, better regulation, a new economy — which Reinhart and Rogoff document as the single most reliable marker of an approaching crisis across eight centuries of financial history. Reach for it whenever a valuation or leverage level is defended by asserting that historical comparisons no longer apply.

models.name: Contingency vs. Determinism
models.definition: Some outcomes are overdetermined and would have happened regardless of small perturbations; others hang on accidents — a weather front, an illness, one messenger — and the skill is judging which regime you are in before forecasting. Reach for it when someone claims an outcome was inevitable: overdetermined systems are forecastable, contingent ones are not, and treating one as the other is a major source of confident error.

models.name: Counterfactual Reasoning
models.definition: Causal claims about the past only mean something against an explicit alternative — 'X caused Y' asserts that without X, Y would not have happened — so disciplined history requires constructing the nearest plausible world in which X was absent. Reach for it to test attributions of credit and blame, and to distinguish a decisive cause from a merely present one.

models.name: Cycles vs. Linear Progress
models.definition: History contains both recurring oscillations (credit cycles, political generations, boom-bust-reform sequences) and genuine one-way ratchets (technology, accumulated knowledge, life expectancy); most forecasting errors come from extrapolating a cycle as a trend or dismissing a real trend as 'just a cycle'. Reach for it when projecting anything forward — first classify whether the driver is mean-reverting or cumulative.

models.name: Great Man vs. Structural Forces
models.definition: The long-running dispute over whether outcomes are driven by exceptional individuals or by demographics, geography, technology and institutions that would have produced roughly the same result through someone else; the honest answer is that structure sets the distribution and individuals decide the draw, with individual leverage rising when systems are near a tipping point. Reach for it when attributing success or failure to a CEO, general or founder — ask what the counterfactual replacement would have achieved.

models.name: History Is Written by the Victors (source bias)
models.definition: The surviving account of any conflict or transition is produced by whoever held the pen afterwards, so motives, atrocities and alternatives are shaped to justify the winner's position — the evidence is not neutral, it is a party to the case. Reach for it whenever you rely on a narrative produced by an interested party, including internal company postmortems and any incumbent's account of how it got there.

models.name: Institutional Sclerosis (Olson's rise and decline of nations)
models.definition: Long periods of stability let distributional coalitions — guilds, cartels, unions, licensed professions, entrenched bureaus — accumulate faster than they dissolve, progressively slowing growth until war or upheaval clears them out; this is why devastated countries sometimes outgrow undisturbed ones. Reach for it to explain long-run stagnation in an economy or a mature company where no single actor is at fault but every process has a defender.

models.name: Lindy Effect
models.definition: For non-perishable things — ideas, books, technologies, institutions — expected remaining life is proportional to age already survived, so what has lasted 500 years is a better bet to last another 500 than this year's innovation is to last a decade. Reach for it when weighing an old practice against a new one absent decisive evidence; longevity is itself a track record of surviving unknown stresses.

models.name: Presentism
models.definition: The error of judging past actors by present knowledge, values and hindsight — condemning decisions for information nobody had, or assuming past people were simply stupider rather than differently constrained. Reach for it both when reading history and when reviewing your own organization's past decisions: the postmortem question is what was knowable then, not what is obvious now.

models.name: Revolution of Rising Expectations (Tocqueville Effect)
models.definition: Revolts erupt not at the depth of misery but when conditions have begun improving and expectations outrun the rate of improvement — the gap between the anticipated and the delivered is what mobilizes people. Reach for it when a reform, raise, or partial concession is followed by more unrest rather than less; the discontent is measuring the gap, not the level.

models.name: Santayana's Dictum (those who forget the past repeat it)
models.definition: Institutions and societies without living memory of a failure systematically recreate its conditions, because the people who paid the price have retired and the safeguards they built look like pointless cost to the successors. Reach for it when the last survivors of a crisis leave the room or a rule's rationale can no longer be stated by anyone enforcing it — that is the moment the repeat becomes likely.

models.name: Survivorship Bias in the Historical Record
models.definition: History is assembled from what survived — durable buildings, winners' archives, literate elites, firms that didn't fold — so the record systematically overstates success rates, durability and the wisdom of past strategies. Reach for it before drawing any base rate from history: ask what the missing denominator looked like and who never got written down.

models.name: Bet sizing on mispricing (bet seldom, bet big)
aka: Bet heavily when the odds are in your favor
Bet heavily when the odds are with you (odds discipline / infrequent big bets)
Betting only when the odds favor you (probability/odds discipline)
Variable 6: Determining how much to bet when you find a mispriced asset
models.definition: Once you have found a genuine mispricing, the question shifts from whether to act to how much — and the correct answer scales with the size of the edge, not with your enthusiasm; most of the time the right bet is zero. Reach for it whenever you are about to take a token position in something you believe strongly, or a large one in something you merely like.
models.mungerCitation: USC Business School, 'A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom', 1994 (pari-mutuel passage)
models.mungerQuote: the wise ones bet heavily when the world offers them that opportunity

models.name: Business quality over statistical cheapness
aka: Great business at a fair price
Pay up for quality (the See's Candy lesson)
Pay up for quality businesses (See's Candy lesson)
Variable 7: Determining whether the quality of a business should be considered
models.definition: Over long holding periods your return converges on the business's return on capital, not on your entry discount — so a superb business at a fair price beats a mediocre one at a bargain price. Reach for it when a thesis rests entirely on the gap between price and book value with no account of what the business earns on reinvested capital.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, 2005
models.mungerQuote: a great business at a fair price is superior to a fair business at a great price

models.name: Focus investing (concentration over diversification)
aka: Focus (concentrated) investing; critique of wide diversification
Variable 4: Determining how much of each security to buy
models.definition: Good ideas are rare, so a portfolio spread across dozens of positions guarantees you own your twentieth-best idea; concentration in a handful of thoroughly understood businesses is the price of an above-average result. Reach for it when portfolio construction is being driven by comfort rather than conviction — and note it is only safe in combination with circle of competence.
models.mungerCitation: USC Business School, 'A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom', 1994; Berkshire meeting remarks
models.mungerQuote: the wise ones bet heavily when the world offers them that opportunity

models.name: Frictional costs (fees, taxes, and turnover drag)
aka: Croupier / frictional costs of the investment industry (casino analogy)
Croupiers' take (frictional costs of investment management)
models.definition: Fees, spreads, and realized-gain taxes are subtracted from the compounding base every period, so their long-run cost is exponential rather than linear — a 2% annual drag can consume a third or more of a multi-decade result. Reach for it when comparing strategies with similar gross returns but different turnover, and to see why deferred taxes function as an interest-free loan.
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998

models.name: Intrinsic value (value to a private owner)
aka: Intrinsic value vs. market price (valuation set by interest rates and recent returns)
Price is what you pay, value is what you get
Value investing: price vs. value; intrinsic value anchor
Variable 1: Determining the appropriate intrinsic value of a business
models.definition: A business is worth the cash it can distribute over its remaining life, discounted back — an estimate, necessarily a range, arrived at by asking what a rational private owner would pay for the whole thing. Reach for it whenever a valuation argument leans on multiples or comparables, which price relative to other opinions rather than to cash.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire and Wesco meeting remarks; BBC interview, 2009
models.mungerQuote: value to a private owner

models.name: Mr. Market
aka: Make a bipolar market your servant, not your master
The market is there to serve you, not to instruct you
models.definition: Graham's parable of a manic-depressive business partner who quotes you a price every day: the market exists to serve you with prices, not to inform you of value. Invoke it whenever a falling quote is tempting you to treat the crowd's mood as evidence — the volatility is the source of the opportunity, not the risk.
models.mungerCitation: USC Business School, 'A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom', 1994
models.mungerQuote: A manic-depressive who comes by every day.

models.name: Rationality as a duty (be objective and dispassionate)
models.definition: Treat clear, unemotional thinking not as a talent but as a moral obligation you owe yourself and your partners — the edge comes from consistently avoiding stupidity rather than from occasional brilliance. Reach for it as a pre-commitment device before decisions where excitement, loyalty, or fear are running hot.
models.mungerCitation: USC Law commencement, 2007; recurring Berkshire annual meeting theme
models.mungerQuote: consistently not stupid

models.name: Stock as proportional ownership of a business
aka: Stock as fractional ownership of a business
Stock as fractional ownership of a business; intrinsic value via discounted future cash flow
View a stock as an ownership of the business
models.definition: A share is a fractional claim on a real operating business, not a ticker that wiggles; therefore the only sound way to value it is to value the whole enterprise and divide. Reach for this first whenever you catch yourself reasoning about a stock's price action instead of its earning power.
models.mungerCitation: Kiplinger interview, 2005; BBC interview, 2009
models.mungerQuote: The number one idea is to view a stock as an ownership of the business.

models.name: Stock market as a pari-mutuel system
aka: Pari-mutuel system as a model of markets
Parimutuel system as model of markets (odds vs. skill; beating the house)
models.definition: Prices are odds set by the crowd's betting, so the winner is not whoever picks the best horse but whoever finds the horse whose odds are wrong — a great company at a great price is no edge if everyone already agrees. Use it to reframe "is this a good business?" into "is this business better than its price implies?"
models.mungerCitation: USC Business School, 'A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom', 1994

models.name: The too-hard pile (three baskets: in, out, too tough)
models.definition: Sort every opportunity into three bins — yes, no, and too hard — and make the third bin large and guilt-free; declining to have an opinion is itself a decision with positive expected value. Reach for it when an idea is interesting but requires you to be right about several things you cannot verify.
models.mungerCitation: USC 1994 / Berkshire meeting remarks (three baskets: in, out, too tough)

models.name: When to sell (sit-on-your-ass investing)
aka: Patience and Inactivity ('sit on your ass investing')
Variable 5: Determining when to sell a security
models.definition: If the business is still compounding at a high rate inside your circle of competence, the correct holding period approaches forever; sell for thesis breakage, better opportunity, or overvaluation so extreme it dwarfs the tax and reinvestment cost — not for boredom or a price target. Reach for it when the urge to "take some off the table" is doing the reasoning.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire and Wesco meeting remarks (supporting usage)
models.mungerQuote: sit on your ass investing

models.name: Capital allocation
aka: Capital allocation skills (Right Stuff in a Business 1)
Rational capital allocation (buying back undervalued stock)
Rational capital allocation — aggressive repurchase of undervalued own stock
models.definition: A CEO's returns are determined less by operations than by where incremental dollars go — reinvestment, acquisitions, buybacks, dividends, debt paydown — yet most executives are promoted for operating skill and arrive at the job untrained in it. Reach for it when assessing management: track the actual historical allocation record, not the stated strategy.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight), written 2000

models.name: Buyback arithmetic
models.definition: Repurchases create value per share only when shares are bought below intrinsic value — above it they transfer wealth from continuing holders to sellers, regardless of the EPS optics. Reach for it whenever a buyback is announced as unconditionally shareholder-friendly, or is being used to offset dilution from stock compensation.

models.name: Cigar-butt investing (net-nets)
models.definition: Buying statistically cheap, mediocre businesses below liquidation value for one last puff of free profit — Graham's original method, which works on small sums and fails as capital grows, because the discount is realized once while a bad business consumes value continuously. Reach for it as the explicit contrast case that clarifies why quality dominates at scale.

models.name: Cloning (shameless copying of proven ideas)
models.definition: Originality is overrated as a source of returns; systematically studying and copying the disclosed positions and methods of demonstrably superior allocators is cheaper than invention and has a better hit rate — provided you clone the reasoning, not just the ticker. Reach for it when generating candidates, never as a substitute for your own valuation.

models.name: Dividend policy
models.definition: Retain a dollar only if the business can turn it into more than a dollar of market value; otherwise pay it out. Reach for it when a low-return business is retaining earnings to fund growth, or when a dividend is being defended on tradition and signaling rather than on the returns available inside the company.

models.name: Look-through earnings
models.definition: The economic return of a portfolio is your proportional share of the underlying businesses' earnings — including retained earnings you never receive as dividends — not the change in quoted market prices. Reach for it to score your own performance over short horizons, when prices are noise and business results are signal.

models.name: Reinvestment runway (compounding at high ROIC)
models.definition: A high return on capital only compounds if there is somewhere to redeploy the cash at that same rate; a great business with no runway becomes a cash cow, not a compounder. Reach for it to distinguish businesses that will multiply capital from those that merely return it — the difference is decades of return, not percentage points.

models.name: Risk of ruin (survival first)
aka: Never risk what you have for what you don't need
models.definition: Compounding is multiplicative, so a single zero erases every prior gain — which makes avoiding wipeout categorically different from, and prior to, maximizing expected return. Reach for it whenever leverage, illiquidity, or correlated bets could turn a bad outcome into a terminal one; the question is not "what is the expected value?" but "can I still play tomorrow?"

models.name: Time arbitrage (long horizon as a structural edge)
models.definition: Most market participants are constrained to quarterly or annual results by career and redemption risk, so the willingness to be wrong for three years is one of the few edges that cannot be arbitraged away by intelligence. Reach for it when an opportunity is obvious but the payoff timing is unknowable — that combination is precisely what makes it available.

models.name: Deliberate Unfairness to Individuals for Greater Average Fairness
aka: Tolerating deliberate unfairness to individuals for greater average fairness (Navy ship-aground rule)
models.definition: A harsh strict-liability rule that is knowingly unfair in individual cases — the old Navy rule that a captain whose ship runs aground is finished, blameless or not — can produce more total fairness and far fewer groundings than a system of case-by-case excuses. Use it when designing rules under weak monitoring: removing the excuse channel is often what makes the incentive bite.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: Trademark and Trade Dress Protection (and Genericide)
aka: Trademark / trade dress protection and avoidance of trademark loss (genericide)
models.definition: Law grants an owner exclusive rights in a distinctive mark or product get-up, but that right decays if the mark becomes the generic name for the product category itself (aspirin, escalator, cellophane) — so the owner must police usage to keep the asset alive. Reach for it when valuing a brand: the moat is a legal right with maintenance costs, not a permanent grant.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four, Poor Charlie's Almanack), July 20, 1996

models.name: Conduct Unbecoming an Officer (Duty of the Exemplar)
models.definition: A military-law standard holding that those in positions of authority are judged against a higher bar than mere legality or ordinary duty, because their behavior sets the norm others copy and defines what the institution tolerates. Reach for it when a leader's conduct is technically permissible but corrosive to the standard they are supposed to embody, or when diagnosing how misbehavior at the top propagates downward.
models.mungerCitation: 2005 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), section "Conduct Unbecoming an Officer," p.220, https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf

models.name: Fiduciary Standard / Punctilio of Honor
models.definition: A fiduciary owes the beneficiary not the morals of the marketplace but 'the punctilio of an honor the most sensitive' — undivided loyalty, no self-dealing, and disclosure beyond what arm's-length dealing requires. Apply it whenever someone handles another's money or discretion: the question is not 'was the deal legal?' but 'would it survive a loyalty test?'
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations (Talk Six, Poor Charlie's Almanack), 1998

models.name: Legal Regime and Culture as an Investment Factor
aka: Legal systems and culture as investment factor (Drucker: US shareholder-favorable regime)
models.definition: Long-run equity returns depend on whether the surrounding legal system and culture actually deliver value to outside shareholders — Drucker's point that the U.S. regime was unusually shareholder-favorable, and that this is a contingent institutional fact, not a law of nature. Reach for it before extrapolating one country's historical returns onto another jurisdiction: same business, different rule-set, different outcome.
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations (Talk Six, Poor Charlie's Almanack), 1998

models.name: Nip Evil in the Bud
aka: Nip evil in the bud (wealth-to-political-power feedback loop)
models.definition: Certain institutional harms — notably the feedback loop where wealth buys political power which buys more wealth — compound so fast that they must be stopped early and cheaply, because once entrenched they become unremovable at any price. Reach for it when a small abuse looks tolerable: ask what it costs to stop now versus after it has bought its own protection.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Asbestos and Tort Reform)

models.name: Regulatory Capture
models.definition: Asserts that a body created to police an industry tends over time to be co-opted by it, as the regulated party supplies the expertise, the personnel, and the career prospects that shape the regulator's judgment — usually without conscious corruption. Reach for it when assessing whether a supervisory institution can actually be relied on, or when an incentive structure quietly converts a watchdog into an advocate.
models.mungerCitation: Harvard-Westlake School talk, 2010, https://pdfcoffee.com/110302239-munger-talk-at-harvard-westlakepdf-pdf-free.html

models.name: Stay Far From the Legal Line
aka: Moral code: stay far from the legal line
models.definition: Asserts that treating the boundary of legality as the operating target is itself a failure mode: because the line's exact location is uncertain and judgment degrades under incentive pressure, the only reliable protection is a wide self-imposed margin of ethical clearance. Reach for it when evaluating conduct that is arguably legal but requires a lawyer to defend, or when an organization's compliance posture has become 'what can we get away with.'
models.mungerCitation: 2004 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), opening speech, section "Moral Code," p.195, https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf

models.name: Trade Secrecy in Lieu of Patent or Copyright
aka: Trade secrecy in lieu of unavailable patent/copyright protection
models.definition: When an invention cannot be patented or copyrighted (or when disclosure would teach competitors more than the monopoly is worth), the alternative protection is simply never to tell anyone — trading a time-limited legal monopoly for an unlimited but fragile factual one. Use it when deciding whether to disclose: patents expire and teach rivals; secrets last forever but die the moment they leak or are independently rediscovered.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four, Poor Charlie's Almanack), July 20, 1996

models.name: Zero-Pay, Significance-Based Service
aka: Zero-pay, significance-based service (English lay magistrates; Franklin's unpaid officials; shareholder-directors)
models.definition: Some roles are performed better by unpaid people who serve for honor and significance than by salaried professionals — England's lay magistrates, Franklin's unpaid officials, engaged shareholder-directors — because removing the paycheck removes the incentive to keep the job at the expense of doing it right. Reach for it when designing governance: pay can convert a judge into an employee whose first duty is to his own tenure.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: Burden of Proof
models.definition: The party asserting a claim must prove it; if the evidence is in equipoise, the asserter loses — so where the burden sits often determines the outcome before any evidence is heard. Reach for it in any argument or decision: ask who has to convince whom, and notice that quietly shifting the burden is the most powerful rhetorical move available.

models.name: Common Law
models.definition: A body of law built bottom-up from accumulated judicial decisions rather than from a comprehensive code, evolving case by case as judges apply and adapt prior rulings to new facts. Use it as a model of decentralized, error-correcting rule-making — an institution that learns from experience, at the cost of predictability and speed.

models.name: Due Process
models.definition: Before the state (or any authority) deprives someone of life, liberty, or property, it must follow fair procedure — notice, a hearing, an impartial decider. Reach for it whenever a decision is legitimate only if the process was: firing an employee, expelling a member, banning a user; process failures poison outcomes even when the outcome was right.

models.name: Duty of Care
models.definition: An obligation to exercise the caution a reasonable person would use toward those foreseeably affected by your conduct — the threshold element that must exist before carelessness can become legal fault. Use it to locate responsibility: the first question in any harm is not 'who screwed up?' but 'who owed a duty to whom?'

models.name: Good Faith
models.definition: An implied obligation to deal honestly and not to destroy the other party's benefit of the bargain, even where the contract's literal text would permit it. Reach for it when someone is technically compliant but obviously gaming the agreement — good faith is the law's admission that no contract can enumerate every way to cheat.

models.name: Negligence
models.definition: The completed tort of causing foreseeable harm by falling below the standard of reasonable care — requiring duty, breach, causation, and damages together, not merely a bad outcome. Use it to separate accident from fault: most bad outcomes are not negligence, and the discipline of checking all four elements kills a lot of outrage-driven blame.

models.name: Presumption of Innocence
models.definition: The accused is treated as innocent until proven guilty — a default state, not a probability estimate, chosen because the system deliberately prefers false acquittals to false convictions. Reach for it when judging anyone on incomplete information: it forces you to name what the default should be and who pays for being wrong.

models.name: Reasonable Doubt
models.definition: The evidentiary threshold a criminal case must clear — near-certainty rather than mere probability — reflecting an asymmetric cost function where convicting the innocent is treated as far worse than freeing the guilty. Use it as a general tool for calibrating confidence to stakes: different decisions deserve different bars, and civil law's 'preponderance' bar exists precisely because its errors cost less.

models.name: Adversarial System
models.definition: Truth is sought by having two self-interested advocates argue opposite sides before a neutral decider, on the theory that partisan zeal surfaces more evidence than a single investigator would find. Reach for it when designing any truth-finding process — but note its known failure mode: it rewards the better-resourced advocate, not the better case, which is why one-sided arguments should never be trusted as complete.

models.name: Bright-Line Rules vs Standards
models.definition: A rule fixes the answer in advance ('65 mph'), while a standard defers it to a decider at the time ('reasonable speed') — rules buy predictability and cheap enforcement at the cost of over- and under-inclusion, standards buy accuracy at the cost of discretion, cost, and gaming. Reach for it when writing any policy: choose rules where discretion will be abused or decisions are frequent, standards where cases are rare and heterogeneous.

models.name: Civil Law (Codified) Tradition
models.definition: A legal system built top-down from a comprehensive statutory code, where judges apply the code rather than make binding precedent — favoring predictability and accessibility over case-by-case adaptation. Use it as the contrast case to common law: the same dispute can resolve differently not because of facts or judges but because of how the system generates its rules.

models.name: Constitutional Pre-Commitment
models.definition: A polity binds its own future self with rules that are deliberately hard to change — supermajorities, entrenched rights, independent central banks — because it expects future majorities to be tempted into decisions it would regret. Reach for it when today's rational actor foresees tomorrow's weakness: the value of the constraint lies exactly in its inconvenience.

models.name: Contract / Enforceable Promise
models.definition: A legally binding exchange of promises that lets strangers cooperate across time by making defection costly — value comes precisely from the ability to bind your own future self. Reach for it whenever cooperation requires someone to move first: the enforceability of the promise, not the goodwill behind it, is what makes the deal possible.

models.name: Liability (Strict vs Fault-Based)
models.definition: Legal responsibility for harm can be assigned on proof of fault (negligence) or imposed regardless of care (strict liability, as with ultrahazardous activity or defective products) — the choice determines who bears the cost of unavoidable accidents. Use it as an incentive dial: strict liability makes the actor internalize all harm and is right when the actor is the cheapest cost-avoider and fault is hard to prove.

models.name: Limited Liability
models.definition: The legal fiction that a corporation is a person whose shareholders risk only their investment shields passive investors from unlimited downside, which is what makes large-scale pooled capital possible at all. Use it to see the tradeoff: it enables enormous enterprise while systematically letting firms externalize catastrophic tail risk onto creditors and the public.

models.name: Precedent / Stare Decisis
models.definition: Courts follow prior decisions on like facts, trading first-best accuracy in the instant case for predictability, equal treatment, and economy of decision-making. Use it to understand why organizations resist obviously correct one-off exceptions: the exception's real cost is the precedent it sets, and precedent binds far more cases than the one in front of you.

models.name: Property Rights
models.definition: A legally enforced bundle of rights — to use, exclude, and transfer — that converts physical control into a tradable, investable asset and internalizes the consequences of its use onto the owner. Use it to explain behavior that looks like carelessness: unowned or insecurely owned things get overused and under-maintained (the tragedy of the commons is a property-rights failure, not a character failure).

models.name: Property Rules vs Liability Rules
models.definition: Calabresi and Melamed's insight that an entitlement can be protected either by a property rule (nobody may take it without your consent, at your price) or a liability rule (they may take it and pay court-set damages) — and the choice hinges on whether bargaining is cheap enough to trust. Use it whenever asking 'injunction or damages?': property rules fit low-transaction-cost settings, liability rules fit holdout-prone ones like eminent domain and pollution.

ref.title: Property Rules, Liability Rules and Inalienability: One View of the Cathedral

models.name: Rule of Law
models.definition: Power is exercised through general, known, prospective rules applied equally to rulers and ruled, rather than through arbitrary discretion — the precondition for long-horizon investment, since no one builds what can be taken by fiat. Reach for it when comparing jurisdictions or organizations: ask whether the rules bind the people at the top, because that single fact predicts most of the rest.

models.name: Separation of Powers / Checks and Balances
models.definition: Splitting authority among rivalrous branches so that each has both the means and the motive to resist the others' overreach — ambition made to counteract ambition, rather than relying on the virtue of officeholders. Reach for it in any organizational design where a single actor could both make and judge the rules: the safeguard is structural rivalry, not good intentions.

models.name: Statute of Limitations
models.definition: Claims expire after a fixed period, cutting off valid grievances in order to preserve evidentiary reliability, allow people to plan, and force diligence on claimants. Reach for it as a general principle of institutional hygiene: some disputes should become unlitigable simply because they are old, and repose has value independent of justice in the individual case.

models.name: All human systems are gamed (game theory)
models.definition: Any rule, metric, or incentive scheme placed in front of intelligent self-interested agents will be optimized against, often in ways the designer never imagined. When designing or evaluating a system, assume adversarial play is the base case and ask what the cleverest cheater would do first.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: Be the house, not the punter
aka: House edge / take of the track
Position yourself on the side with the structural edge
models.definition: In any system with a mathematical take, returns accrue systematically to whoever holds the edge and are drained from whoever pays it; the durable strategy is to occupy the house's position rather than to try to out-bet it. Reach for it when deciding which side of a repeated wager, market, or fee structure to stand on.
models.mungerCitation: Acquired podcast, 2023, https://www.acquired.fm/episodes/charlie-munger

models.name: Black-Scholes as a 'know-nothing system'
models.definition: Option-pricing formulas give roughly right answers over short horizons for someone with no view on the underlying, and progressively sillier answers as the horizon lengthens or the user actually knows something. The general lesson: a model built to encode ignorance should not be used as an authority by people with information.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Valuing Options and the Black-Scholes Model)

models.name: Buried covariance
aka: Hidden correlation of risks that surfaces in bad conditions
models.definition: Risks that look independent in normal conditions turn out to be correlated precisely when conditions turn bad — the diversification you paid for evaporates at the moment you need it. Whenever a portfolio, supplier base, or hedge relies on independence, ask what common factor could switch all the correlations to one.
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998

models.name: Compound interest / compounding
aka: Compound growth arithmetic (long-horizon compounding to a target)
models.definition: Value that grows on its own accumulated growth rises geometrically, so small differences in rate, time, and drag (fees, taxes, interruptions) produce enormous differences in terminal outcome. Reach for it whenever a decision has a long horizon: it says protect the runway, minimize frictions, and never interrupt the machine unnecessarily.
models.mungerCitation: A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom, USC Marshall, 1994 (compound interest named first among the 'great useful models'); Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 2; Practical Thought About Practical Thought?, 1996
models.mungerQuote: understanding both the power of compound interest and the difficulty of getting it...

models.name: Decision tree theory
aka: Decision tree theory (also under Mathematics in the 25iq list)
models.definition: Lay a decision out as a branching tree of choices and chance nodes, attach probabilities and payoffs to the branches, and fold back to compare alternatives. It is high-school algebra applied to real life, and its main value is forcing you to enumerate outcomes you would otherwise leave unnamed.
models.mungerCitation: A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom, USC Marshall, 1994
models.mungerQuote: they'd taken high school algebra and applied it to real life problems

models.name: Fat tails and extreme events
aka: Fat tails / extreme events — bell-curve limitations, Taleb's Black Swans and 'Extremistan'
models.definition: In many real domains — markets, wars, epidemics, insurance losses — extreme outcomes are vastly more frequent than a normal distribution predicts, so the rare event dominates the total. Reach for it whenever a model's worst case was derived from sigma counts: price the tail explicitly instead, and remember Berkshire underwrites assuming tails fatter than Gaussian.
models.mungerCitation: Wesco/Berkshire remarks that Berkshire assumes fatter-than-Gaussian tails; Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults, UCSB, 2003

models.name: Fundamental principle of algebra — seek functional equivalents
models.definition: If A=B and B=C, then A=C: restate an intractable problem in an equivalent form you can actually solve. In practice this means hunting for the substitutable variable — the proxy, analogue, or reformulation — rather than assaulting the original question head-on.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: Gödel incompleteness / irremovable paradox
aka: Gödel incompleteness — irremovable paradox in any system
models.definition: Even pure mathematics contains true statements it cannot prove within itself; no sufficiently rich system is both complete and consistent. The practical lesson Munger draws is temperamental: since paradox cannot be engineered out of any serious system, learn to cheerfully endure it rather than demand a tidiness reality does not offer.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000; Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: Kelly criterion / optimal bet sizing
aka: Kelly criterion / Kelly optimization model (2p−1=x bet sizing; half-Kelly recommended)
models.definition: A formula (bet fraction ≈ 2p−1 for even-money odds) giving the stake that maximizes long-run geometric growth of capital; betting more than Kelly guarantees eventual ruin, betting less merely grows slower. Practitioners typically use half-Kelly because edge estimates are themselves uncertain, and Kelly is brutally punishing when your probability is wrong.
models.mungerCitation: Substance is Kelly-like in the USC 1994 pari-mutuel passage; Munger never invoked Kelly by name — the attribution enters the genre via Hagstrom (1999) and Poundstone

models.name: Mathematics of the chain letter / Ponzi scheme
aka: Pyramid growth requiring ever more entrants
models.definition: A structure that pays earlier participants out of the contributions of later entrants requires a geometrically expanding supply of new entrants and therefore must collapse; the arithmetic guarantees it regardless of the story told about the returns. Reach for it whenever reported gains depend on inflows rather than on cash thrown off by an underlying asset.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2000, Morning Session, https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2000/04/29/morning-session---2000-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html

models.name: Monte Carlo simulation
models.definition: When a system is too tangled for closed-form math, sample its inputs randomly thousands of times and read the distribution of outcomes rather than a point estimate. Its honesty is bounded by its assumptions: a simulation built on normal inputs and independent draws will reproduce those beliefs with impressive-looking precision.
models.mungerCitation: Harvard-Westlake School talk, 2010, https://pdfcoffee.com/110302239-munger-talk-at-harvard-westlakepdf-pdf-free.html

models.name: Normal (Gaussian) distribution and its abuse
models.definition: The bell curve describes outcomes generated by many small independent additive causes, and where it applies, mean and standard deviation summarize everything. Munger's emphasis is the second half: the curve is routinely bolted onto financial and social phenomena where the generating process is nothing like it, producing confident numbers about events the model says are impossible.
models.mungerCitation: A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom, USC Marshall, 1994 (Gaussian distribution / bell-curve passage); Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults, UCSB, 2003 (critique of misapplied Gaussian curves)

models.name: Numerical fluency / Galilean quantification
aka: Math as the language of scientific reality
Meaning (understanding vs knowing the name; translate to numbers and consequences)
Numbers and their meaning (innumeracy: compounding, time value, relative vs absolute numbers)
models.definition: Mathematics is the language in which physical and economic reality is written, so any judgment that resists being put in numbers is probably not yet understood. Practically: run the arithmetic, know the difference between relative and absolute figures, and distrust conclusions whose magnitudes you have never checked.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four, Poor Charlie's Almanack), July 20, 1996; A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom, USC Marshall, 1994

models.name: Permutations and combinations (elementary probability of Fermat and Pascal)
aka: Probabilities and number of possible outcomes
models.definition: The elementary counting math that says how many ways outcomes can be arranged, and therefore how likely each is — the mechanical foundation under any honest probability estimate. Munger treats it as non-optional literacy: without it you cannot enumerate the possible outcomes of a decision, and you will substitute a story for a number.
models.mungerCitation: A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom, USC Marshall, 1994; Poor Charlie's Almanack, Ch. 5
models.mungerQuote: the elementary math of permutations and combinations

models.name: Arithmetic of active management
aka: Market as zero-sum minus costs; half below median
models.definition: Investing against other investors is a zero-sum game before costs and a negative-sum game after them: by definition half of all dollars managed sit below the median, and fees, frictions, and taxes come out of everyone's share. Any pitch that implies most participants can beat the average is arithmetically impossible, not merely optimistic.
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998

models.name: Correlation versus causation
models.definition: Two things moving together can mean A causes B, B causes A, a third factor causes both, or nothing at all — association alone never fixes the arrow. Reach for it whenever a claim rests on observed co-movement: ask for the mechanism, the confounder, and what would have happened without the supposed cause.
models.mungerCitation: Supporting usage: influence-from-mere-association tendency (The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard, 1995) covers mistaking association for causation

models.name: Expected value
aka: Quantification (translate judgments into numbers, expected value)
models.definition: The probability-weighted average of all outcomes — the single number that tells you whether a gamble is worth taking independent of how any one instance turns out. It is the discipline that separates a good decision from a good result, and it is what makes low-probability/high-payoff and high-probability/low-payoff bets comparable on one scale.
models.mungerCitation: Substance present in the USC 1994 pari-mutuel and Fermat/Pascal passages; the formal EV label is the compilers' framing

models.name: Limits of geometric (exponential) progressions on a finite base
aka: Limits of geometric progressions on a finite earth
Scale limits to compounding (diminishing returns on large capital)
Size as an anchor on returns
models.definition: No exponential can run forever inside a finite system — a growth rate that outpaces its host must eventually break, no matter how long the trend has held. Apply it to any projection of perpetual growth in earnings, population, debt, or resource use: the interesting question is not whether it stops but what stops it.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight, Poor Charlie's Almanack; written summer 2000)

models.name: Multiply by zero
models.definition: In a multiplicative chain, one zero annihilates every other term no matter how large — brilliance, effort, and prior returns all vanish against a single ruinous outcome. It is the arithmetic case for avoiding catastrophic risk first and optimizing second, and for treating any nonzero chance of ruin as disqualifying under repetition.
models.mungerCitation: Matches Munger's ruin-avoidance theme; the canonical 'long list of numbers multiplied by zero' formulation is Buffett's

models.name: Nonstationarity of data
aka: Nonstationarity of data (Bradford Cornell — averaging across different populations misleads)
models.definition: Statistics computed across periods or populations governed by different regimes describe an average of things that never existed, and the resulting parameters have no forecasting content. Before using a historical mean, volatility, or correlation, ask whether the process generating the data was the same throughout the window.
models.mungerCitation: 2003 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), 'Berkshire's Advantages in Super Cat Insurance Underwriting,' p.180, https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf

models.name: Power laws
models.definition: In power-law distributions a few observations account for most of the total (the 80/20 or Pareto pattern), the average is not representative, and the biggest event yet seen is usually not the biggest possible. Reach for it in city sizes, book sales, venture returns, and network effects — domains where 'typical' is a meaningless summary and effort should concentrate on the head.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2004, Morning Session, https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2004/05/01/morning-session---2004-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html; 2004 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), 'Book Recommendations,' p.215, https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf

models.name: Simple math beats fancy math in business (distrust of false precision)
aka: Higher mathematics is not needed in business
models.definition: The mathematics that actually pays in business is elementary — arithmetic, compound interest, and basic probability — while elaborate higher mathematics mostly manufactures false precision and confidence about things that cannot be known. Reach for it when a decision is being justified by the sophistication of a model rather than by the robustness of the underlying facts.
models.mungerCitation: A Conversation with Charlie Munger, University of Michigan Ross, 2017, https://worldlypartners.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/2017-a-conversation-with-charlie-munger-at-university-of-michigan-ross.pdf

models.name: Standard deviation / the sigma test of luck versus skill
aka: Standard deviations / Gaussian-luck critique (six-sigma explanation of Berkshire)
models.definition: Express an observed result as a number of standard deviations from what chance alone would produce, then ask whether the luck explanation survives — Munger uses it to show that attributing Berkshire's record to good fortune requires a preposterous sigma count. Useful whenever someone dismisses (or credits) a track record without checking how big the chance hypothesis would have to be.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Cost-benefit analysis
models.definition: Enumerate and quantify the full costs and benefits of an option — including opportunity costs and second-order effects — and compare like with like before deciding. Its discipline is less the arithmetic than the enumeration: most bad decisions omit a cost rather than mis-add one.
models.mungerCitation: Generic decision tool consistent with Munger's engineering and quality-control remarks; not a named Munger model

models.name: Law of large numbers
models.definition: As the number of independent trials grows, the observed average converges on the true expected value — which is why insurers can price a book they cannot price a policy, and why small samples say almost nothing. The trap is applying it to correlated or non-repeating events, where the numbers never get large in the sense the theorem requires.
models.mungerCitation: Supporting usage in Berkshire insurance-underwriting discussions; not named by Munger as one of his models

models.name: Randomness (mistaking noise for signal)
models.definition: Random processes generate streaks, patterns, and apparent trends that the mind reads as meaningful causes; much of what looks like skill, momentum, or a turning point is variance. Reach for it before explaining any short-run result — the first hypothesis for a pattern in noisy data should be that it is noise.
models.mungerCitation: USC 1994 names the Gaussian distribution and casino probability as core models; 'randomness' per se is the compiler's label

models.name: Regression to the mean
models.definition: Where an outcome mixes skill and luck, extreme results are disproportionately produced by extreme luck, so the next observation drifts back toward the average without anything causal happening.
