It is the standard explanation for fading star performers, mean-reverting margins, and the illusion that punishment works and praise backfires.
models.mungerCitation: Reversion-to-mean reasoning pervades Munger/Buffett commentary on returns and ability, but no talk where Munger names it as one of his models

models.name: Scenario analysis (best/worst case)
models.definition: Construct a small number of coherent, internally consistent futures — not just an upside and downside twiddle of one variable — and check whether the decision survives each. It is the cheap substitute for a full decision tree when probabilities cannot be estimated, and its main product is discovering the scenario you cannot live through.
models.mungerCitation: Functionally adjacent to Munger's endorsed Fermat/Pascal decision-tree thinking (USC 1994); the named technique comes from the decision-analysis literature

models.name: Survivorship bias
models.definition: Analyzing only the entities that made it to the present systematically overstates success rates and mislabels the causes of success, because the failures that shared the same traits are invisible. Whenever you study winners — funds, founders, strategies — ask where the graveyard is and whether the data set was built from it.
models.mungerCitation: From the statistics literature via Bevelin; nearest neighbor in the record is Buffett's 'Superinvestors' survivorship argument

models.name: Agent-based models
models.definition: Simulate a population of individual agents following simple rules and watch what aggregate patterns emerge, rather than assuming the aggregate and solving for equilibrium. Useful when heterogeneity and interaction are the whole story — segregation, traffic, panics — and closed-form math cannot represent them.

models.name: Bayes' theorem / Bayesian updating
aka: Bayes's theorem (prior beliefs + new data = improved belief)
models.definition: Correct belief revision multiplies your prior probability by the likelihood of the new evidence: strong evidence against a very unlikely hypothesis still leaves it unlikely. Use it whenever a test, signal, or anecdote arrives — the decisive question is not 'how convincing is this evidence' but 'how likely was the claim before it, and how often does this evidence appear when the claim is false'.
models.mungerCitation: Munger cited Fermat/Pascal, not Bayes; Bayesian updating is a latticework-genre addition

models.name: Central limit theorem
models.definition: Averages of many independent, finite-variance random variables converge to a normal distribution regardless of the underlying shape — which is what licenses the bell curve where it legitimately applies. Its preconditions are also the checklist for when it fails: dependence, feedback, or infinite variance, and normality never arrives.

models.name: Coincidences and miracles (law of truly large numbers)
aka: Coincidences and miracles (misjudging randomness and improbable events)
models.definition: With enough trials, events of tiny individual probability become near-certain somewhere in the sample — so astonishing coincidences require no explanation beyond the size of the population that could have produced them. Use it whenever a striking one-in-a-million story is offered as evidence: with millions of chances, the miracle is expected.
models.mungerCitation: Bevelin's import from the probability literature (Littlewood's Law; Diaconis & Mosteller); no known instance of Munger naming it

models.name: Global and local maxima
models.definition: Hill-climbing by always taking the locally improving step lands you on the nearest peak, which is usually not the highest one; reaching a global maximum often requires first going downhill. Reach for it when incremental optimization has stalled — the question becomes whether you are stuck on a small hill and need a discontinuous move.

models.name: Mean, median, and mode
models.definition: Three different answers to 'what is typical', which diverge exactly when the distribution is skewed — and the choice among them is where most honest-looking statistics mislead. Whenever an average is quoted for incomes, outcomes, or durations, ask which measure it is and what the shape of the distribution does to it.

models.name: Optionality / asymmetric payoffs
models.definition: Positions with capped downside and open-ended upside gain value from volatility and uncertainty rather than losing it, so you can profit without forecasting. Reach for it when you can pay a small known cost for a large unknown payoff — and invert it to spot the reverse asymmetry, where small steady gains hide a catastrophic tail.
models.mungerCitation: Taleb-genre label; Munger's nearest formulation is 'bet heavily when the odds are with you'

models.name: Regression analysis
models.definition: Fitting a line or surface to data to estimate how much of one variable's movement is associated with another's, holding named factors constant. Its power is quantifying relationships; its standing danger is that a good fit says nothing about causality, and omitted variables silently load onto whatever you did measure.

models.name: Reliability of case evidence (anecdote vs. systematic evidence)
models.definition: A vivid individual case carries more psychological weight than a table of frequencies while carrying almost no informational weight, because it is an uncontrolled sample of one. Before letting a story move a decision, ask what a systematic count of similar cases shows and why this one was the one you heard about.
models.mungerCitation: Drawn from the evidence-based-medicine and statistics literature; adjacent to Munger's vividness warnings but framed by the compiler

models.name: Sampling and sample size
models.definition: A sample supports inference about a population only if it is large enough and drawn without bias; small samples produce wildly variable estimates and are the engine behind most spurious 'findings'. Before believing a rate, ask how many observations produced it, how they were selected, and what the margin of error is.

models.name: Sensitivity analysis
models.definition: Vary each input across its plausible range to see which ones actually drive the output, and how far they can move before the conclusion flips. It converts a single fragile number into a map of where the real uncertainty lives, and usually reveals that two or three assumptions carry the whole model.

models.name: Variation vs. central tendency ('the trend of the system vs. trends in the system')
aka: Variation vs. central tendency (Stephen Jay Gould, Full House)
models.definition: Gould's point that a change in an average can be produced entirely by a change in the spread rather than by movement of the whole — the disappearance of .400 hitters reflects shrinking variance among players, not decline. Use it when a headline average moves: ask whether the distribution shifted or merely tightened against a wall.

models.name: Bayesian vs. frequentist inference
models.definition: Two meanings of 'probability': a long-run frequency in repeated trials, or a degree of belief updated by evidence. Knowing which one a number refers to determines what you may do with it — frequentist tools are silent about one-off events, which is exactly where most consequential decisions live.

models.name: Benford's law
models.definition: In many naturally occurring sets of numbers spanning orders of magnitude, leading digits are not uniform — about 30% of values start with 1 and only ~4.6% with 9. Fabricated figures rarely obey it, which makes the law a cheap first screen for cooked books, invented survey data, and manipulated results.

models.name: Conditional probability and independence
models.definition: P(A|B) is generally not P(B|A), and probabilities multiply only when events are genuinely independent — two errors that produce most everyday probability disasters, from the prosecutor's fallacy to compounded system-failure estimates. Whenever chances are combined or a test result is interpreted, check the direction of the conditioning and whether independence was assumed or established.

models.name: Ergodicity (time average vs. ensemble average)
models.definition: The average outcome across many people playing once can differ completely from the outcome of one person playing repeatedly, because ruin removes you from the game. Expected value is the right guide only for ensembles you can average over; for a single life or a single balance sheet, the survival-weighted path is what matters.

models.name: Fermi estimation (order-of-magnitude reasoning)
models.definition: Decompose an unknowable quantity into factors you can each guess within a factor of ten; the independent errors partly cancel and the product lands surprisingly close. Use it as a bullshit detector before any detailed model — most bad plans die on a napkin, and the ones that survive tell you which factor to actually go measure.

models.name: Jensen's inequality / nonlinearity of averages
models.definition: For a nonlinear function, the average of the outputs is not the output of the average — so planning around the mean input systematically misprices any convex or concave payoff. This is why 'the average customer', 'the expected delay', and 'the base-case scenario' mislead whenever the response curve bends.

models.name: Random walk
models.definition: A process whose next step is independent of its history produces trends, runs, and chart patterns that look meaningful but carry no predictive information. Where a random walk is a decent approximation — short-run price moves being the classic case — pattern-finding is worse than useless because it manufactures false confidence.

models.name: Selection bias
models.definition: When the process that put observations into your data set is related to the outcome you are measuring, the sample answers a different question than the one you asked. It is broader than survivorship: self-selected respondents, referred patients, and inbound leads all carry it, and no amount of sample size fixes it.

models.name: Simpson's paradox
models.definition: A relationship that holds in every subgroup can reverse when the groups are pooled, because group sizes and base rates differ — the aggregate literally tells the opposite story from the parts. Whenever a combined statistic contradicts what practitioners see on the ground, disaggregate before believing either.

models.name: Statistical significance, p-values, and multiple comparisons
models.definition: A p-value states how surprising the data would be if nothing were going on — not the probability that a claim is true — and running enough comparisons guarantees 'significant' results from pure noise. Ask how many hypotheses were tested, whether the analysis was pre-specified, and how large the effect is, before treating significance as evidence.

models.name: Volatility drag (geometric vs. arithmetic mean)
models.definition: Compounded returns follow the geometric mean, which is always below the arithmetic mean and falls further the more results swing — a +50% then −50% pair averages zero but leaves you down 25%. It explains why reducing variance can raise terminal wealth without raising average returns, and why volatile track records overstate what investors actually earned.

models.name: Asymmetric Warfare
models.definition: When two sides have grossly unequal strength, the weaker side wins not by matching the stronger on its chosen terms but by shifting the contest to a dimension where the strong side's advantages are irrelevant or become liabilities. Use it to explain why incumbents with overwhelming resources routinely lose to opponents who refuse to fight the war the incumbent is equipped for.

models.name: Counterinsurgency
models.definition: The strong side's answer to guerrilla asymmetry: since the insurgent hides inside a population, victory comes from winning that population's cooperation rather than from destroying enemy formations — and heavy-handed force is counterproductive because it manufactures new insurgents. Use it whenever suppressing a decentralized opponent by force would itself create the conditions that sustain the opponent.

models.name: Mutually Assured Destruction
models.definition: When both sides can annihilate each other even after absorbing a first strike, the very completeness of each side's power makes using it suicidal, and a stable peace emerges from maximal threat. Reach for it wherever escalation is credible but self-destructive — nuclear standoffs, patent-portfolio détentes, price wars between duopolists — and note the paradox that vulnerability, not defense, is what stabilizes the system.

models.name: Seeing the Front
models.definition: Commanders who personally inspect the front line get unfiltered information, because every layer of reporting between the decision-maker and reality has an incentive to sanitize bad news. Reach for it whenever you are deciding from summaries, dashboards, or direct reports rather than from the thing itself.

models.name: Two-Front War
models.definition: Fighting on two fronts at once divides finite force, attention, and logistics, so a combatant strong enough to beat either opponent separately can lose to both together. Reach for it as a warning model whenever you are tempted to open a second conflict — a second product line, a second lawsuit, a second reorganization — before the first is settled.

models.name: Arms Race
models.definition: When rivals each invest to stay ahead of the other, escalating spending leaves relative position unchanged while absolute cost climbs for everyone — a competitive treadmill that consumes resources without producing advantage. Use it to recognize contests where the rational individual move (match the rival) produces a collectively ruinous outcome, and to look for the exit: a treaty, a differentiated position, or refusal to run.

models.name: Attrition vs. Maneuver Warfare
models.definition: Two opposed theories of victory: attrition grinds the enemy down by trading resources you have more of, while maneuver dislocates him — hitting the joints, rear, and command structure so his forces become useless without being destroyed. Use it to force an explicit choice about which war you are fighting, since attrition rewards the side with deeper reserves and maneuver rewards the side with better speed and judgment.

models.name: Center of Gravity
models.definition: Clausewitz's hub of all power and movement: the one source — an army, an alliance, a capital, a leader's will — on which an opponent's whole capacity depends, and against which effort should be directed rather than dissipated across secondary targets. Reach for it to move from "where can we attack" to "what single thing, if removed, collapses the rest."

models.name: Defender's Advantage
models.definition: The defender fights on prepared ground with shorter supply lines and needs only to not lose, so the attacker historically requires a large local superiority — roughly three to one by rule of thumb — to succeed. Reach for it when assessing an assault on an entrenched incumbent, and note its inversion: the same terrain that protects you also fixes you in place.

models.name: Deterrence
models.definition: You can prevent an action without ever taking one, provided the opponent believes you are both able and willing to impose an unacceptable cost — which makes credibility, not capability, the binding constraint. Reach for it when the goal is that nothing happens; the corollary is that a threat you would never actually execute deters nobody once it is tested.

models.name: Escalation Ladder
models.definition: Conflict proceeds by discrete rungs of intensity, each of which invites a matching or higher response, so the relevant question is never just "should I take this step" but "where does the ladder end if we both keep climbing." Reach for it before the first retaliation, since the rungs are far easier to climb than to descend and each one narrows the space for a negotiated exit.

models.name: Fog of War
models.definition: Clausewitz's observation that in conflict most information is missing, late, or wrong, and the commander must act anyway on a picture he knows to be partly false. Reach for it to resist plans that assume clean information, and to judge decisions by what was knowable at the time rather than by what was revealed afterward.

models.name: Force Concentration
models.definition: Massing strength at one decisive point beats spreading it evenly, because combat power scales superlinearly with local superiority — so a smaller army that concentrates can defeat a larger one that disperses, piece by piece. Use it against the instinct to defend everything or fund everything a little; the price of concentration is accepting weakness everywhere else, and that price is usually worth paying.

models.name: Friction
aka: Everything in war is simple but the simplest thing is difficult
models.definition: Clausewitz's companion to fog: everything in war is simple, but the simplest thing is difficult — orders are misheard, weather intervenes, units arrive late, and countless trivial frustrations compound until the plan bears no resemblance to execution. Use it to budget for the gap between a plan on paper and a plan in the world, and to prefer plans that are robust to degradation over plans that are optimal when everything works.

models.name: Golden Bridge
aka: Build Your Enemy a Golden Bridge
models.definition: Sun Tzu's rule to always leave a surrounded enemy an escape route, because a cornered opponent with nothing to lose fights with a desperation that is expensive to overcome. Use it in negotiation, litigation, and competition: total victory that strips the loser of any acceptable exit converts a solvable conflict into a fight to the death.

models.name: Guerrilla Warfare
models.definition: The weak side's operational doctrine: refuse decisive battle, disperse into the population or terrain, strike only where locally superior, and win by making the conflict cost more than the prize is worth to the stronger side. Use it when you cannot win an attrition contest and must convert the fight into a test of the opponent's patience and cost tolerance rather than of raw strength.

models.name: Logistics
aka: Amateurs talk tactics, professionals talk logistics
models.definition: Amateurs talk tactics, professionals talk logistics: campaigns are limited less by courage or cleverness than by the unglamorous capacity to move fuel, food, ammunition, and replacements to where they are needed. Reach for it whenever a strategy is exciting on the map but silent about the supply line that would have to sustain it.

models.name: OODA Loop
models.definition: John Boyd's model of conflict as competing cycles of Observe, Orient, Decide, Act: the side that completes the cycle faster forces the slower side to keep responding to a situation that has already changed, until its decisions become irrelevant. Use it when speed of adaptation matters more than quality of any single decision, and note that Orient — the mental model you fit observations into — is the leverage point, not Decide.

models.name: Pyrrhic Victory
aka: Another such victory and we are undone
models.definition: A win whose cost exceeds its value, leaving the victor worse off than before and unable to survive another such success. Use it to score outcomes on net position rather than on who held the field — bidding wars, litigation, and price wars routinely produce winners who would have been better off losing early.

models.name: Scorched Earth
models.definition: Destroying the resources an advancing enemy would capture and live off — denying him the prize rather than defending it — trades your own assets for his exhaustion. Reach for it as the extreme case of value-denial strategy, and note that it works only when you can bear the self-inflicted damage longer than the opponent can bear the deprivation.

models.name: Aristotle on deserved inequality and envy
aka: Aristotle: men resent unjust inequality, not inequality
Perceived justice as the antidote to envy
models.definition: Aristotle's observation that people do not resent inequality as such — they resent inequality they judge undeserved; envy and social resentment track perceived injustice, not the size of the gap. Reach for it when diagnosing anger at rewards (executive pay, windfalls, inherited advantage) to ask whether the grievance is about magnitude or about desert.
models.mungerCitation: Harvard-Westlake School talk, 2010, https://pdfcoffee.com/110302239-munger-talk-at-harvard-westlakepdf-pdf-free.html; Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2016, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2016/03/07/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journal-annual-meeting-2016/

models.name: Assiduity
aka: Sit down and stay with it until done
models.definition: Munger's favorite word for the compound of sitting down and staying with the problem until it is done — steady application rather than bursts of brilliance. It matters because most durable advantages come from doing an unglamorous thing consistently for a very long time, a strategy almost nobody is willing to copy.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Ten, USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007

models.name: Avoid sloth and unreliability
aka: Do the job you have well
models.definition: Munger's negative rule: whatever else you do, do not be lazy and do not be undependable, because these two failures are sufficient on their own to sink an otherwise able life. Its power is as an avoidance checklist — most careers are lost to these rather than to any deficit of talent, and both are fully within personal control.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Ten, USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007

models.name: Deserve what you want
models.definition: The reciprocity rule that the surest way to get a thing — a good job, a good spouse, a good reputation — is to make yourself the kind of person who deserves it, rather than to pursue the thing directly. Use it to redirect effort from persuasion and positioning toward becoming genuinely worthy, which is both more reliable and harder to compete away.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Ten, USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007; Poor Charlie's Almanack, 'Mungerisms' (How to Find a Good Spouse)

models.name: Destroy your best-loved ideas
aka: You're not entitled to an opinion until you can destroy your own
models.definition: The epistemic duty to actively seek evidence that would demolish your most cherished conclusions, and to count a destroyed favorite idea as an achievement rather than a loss. Reach for it when a conviction has become identity-laden and the search for evidence has quietly turned confirmatory.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2021, Q41, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2021/02/26/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journal-annual-meeting-2021/

models.name: Filial piety and intergenerational value transfer
models.definition: The Confucian insight that the durable transmission of values happens through families across generations, not through institutions or exhortation, and that honoring one's forebears is the mechanism by which a civilization keeps what it learned. Apply it when asking how a culture — corporate or national — actually propagates: the answer is usually who raised whom, not what the mission statement says.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Ten, USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007

models.name: Honesty as the best policy
aka: Tell the truth and you won't have to remember your lies
models.definition: Vincenti's rule — tell the truth and you won't have to remember your lies — treats honesty as a cognitive and operational efficiency, not merely a virtue: deception imposes a permanent bookkeeping burden and a permanent tail risk. Use it when a small convenient misstatement looks costless; the cost is the state you now have to maintain forever.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Keys to Our Success); 'Charlie on Honesty: 2004 Wesco Meeting'

models.name: Moral duty to be underpaid
aka: Take less than you could extract
models.definition: The prescription that people in positions of trust and leverage should deliberately take less than they could extract — being visibly underpaid buys legitimacy and defuses the politics of envy that grasping compensation provokes. Reach for it when setting one's own pay, fees, or terms where the maximum extractable is well above the defensible.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2008, Afternoon Session, https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2008/05/03/afternoon-session---2008-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html

models.name: Persevere without hope
aka: It is not necessary to hope in order to persevere
models.definition: William the Silent's maxim that it is not necessary to hope in order to persevere, nor to succeed in order to persevere — duty and disposition can carry action after optimism has run out. Use it when a correct course of action offers no visible prospect of reward, where waiting for motivation guarantees abandonment.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Ten, USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007

models.name: Philosophy of responsibility
aka: Bear the consequences of what you create
models.definition: The ethical rule that whoever originates a risk, product, or obligation must retain exposure to its consequences — responsibility should not be severable from the act of creating the hazard. Reach for it when a system lets originators sell the downside onward (loan origination, securitization, advice without exposure).
models.mungerCitation: Harvard-Westlake School talk, 2010, https://pdfcoffee.com/110302239-munger-talk-at-harvard-westlakepdf-pdf-free.html

models.name: Principle of economy
models.definition: Munger's rule that an explanation should be pushed down to the most fundamental level available and should not multiply entities beyond necessity — prefer the account that rests on the hardest underlying discipline. Use it to rank competing explanations: the one that bottoms out in physics, biology, or arithmetic beats the one that stops at a fashionable abstraction.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Prudence: underspend your income
aka: Save as servant of duty (Judge Munger)
models.definition: The rule, inherited from Munger's grandfather, that you should live below your means and treat accumulated savings as a reserve held in trust for duties yet to arrive — not as deferred consumption. Its value is optionality: the person with a cash cushion can act on opportunity, refuse bad offers, and rescue family, while the fully-committed cannot.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Ten, USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007

models.name: Rationality as a moral duty
aka: Rationality as an obligation, not a technique
Rationality is a high moral duty
models.definition: Munger's claim that thinking clearly is not merely an instrumental skill but an ethical obligation — you owe it to yourself and to those who depend on you to not be stupid, and irrationality is a moral failing rather than bad luck. Reach for it when framing why cognitive discipline is owed to others, not just useful to you.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2017, Q38, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2017/02/26/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journal-annual-meeting-2017/; Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2015, Afternoon Session; A Conversation with Charlie Munger, University of Michigan Ross, 2017; 2006 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), "Rationality is a High Moral Duty," p.247

models.name: Reliability ethos as economic infrastructure
aka: Religion-instilled guilt as driver of economic trust
models.definition: The claim that a culture's internalized guilt and duty — historically instilled by religion, and persisting after belief fades — is what makes high-trust commerce cheap, and that societies lacking it pay for every transaction in lawyers and verification. Reach for it when comparing institutions across cultures, or when assessing what a firm actually loses when it teaches its people that rules are for suckers.
models.mungerCitation: Herb Kay Lecture, 'Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults', UCSB, 2003

models.name: Samuel Johnson's ethical rule on removable ignorance
models.definition: If ignorance is easily removable and you hold a position of responsibility, remaining ignorant is not innocence but treachery — a breach of duty, not a gap in luck. Apply it to yourself before any decision where the missing knowledge is a phone call or an afternoon of reading away, and to institutions that plead surprise at foreseeable disasters.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998; Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000; Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB, 2003

models.name: Stoic response to adversity
aka: Stoic response to adversity (Epictetus: every mischance an opportunity)
models.definition: Epictetus's rule that you cannot choose your circumstances but you can choose your response, so every mischance should be converted into an occasion to behave well and learn something. Reach for it when a setback tempts you into self-pity or blame, which add a second, self-inflicted loss on top of the first.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Ten, USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007; earlier used in Talk One, Harvard School Commencement, June 13, 1986

models.name: The Robinson Crusoe method
aka: Work it out yourself from what you have
models.definition: Attack a hard problem the way a castaway must — reason it out yourself from first principles with whatever materials are already at hand, rather than waiting on experts, permission, or received opinion. Reach for it when a problem is being deferred because the 'proper' resources or authorities are unavailable.
models.mungerCitation: CNBC, "Charlie Munger: A Life of Wit and Wisdom," 2023, https://www.cnbc.com/2023/11/30/full-transcript-from-cnbcs-charlie-munger-a-life-of-wit-and-wisdom-.html

models.name: The iron rule of life
aka: Only 20% of people can be in the top fifth
models.definition: The arithmetic constraint that relative-standing goods are zero-sum by construction — only a fifth of people can be in the top fifth — so a life plan built on out-ranking others is a plan that most adopters must fail at. Reach for it when expectations, envy, or ambition are pinned to rank rather than to absolute attainment.
models.mungerCitation: CNBC, "Charlie Munger: A Life of Wit and Wisdom," 2023, https://www.cnbc.com/2023/11/30/full-transcript-from-cnbcs-charlie-munger-a-life-of-wit-and-wisdom-.html

models.name: Tortoise-and-hare strategy
aka: Assimilate proven insights, avoid standard calamities
models.definition: Aesop's frame applied to competition: the plodder who reliably assimilates the best proven insights of his betters and avoids the standard calamities will pass faster rivals who make one big unforced error. It is the argument for a checklist-and-avoidance approach over brilliance — you do not need to be smart if you are consistently not stupid.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Keys to Our Success)

models.name: Tragic-flaw structure
aka: Greek tragedy / morality play as analytic frame
models.definition: The Greek-tragedy frame in which catastrophe is not random misfortune but the lawful working-out of a flaw already present in the protagonist's virtues — hamartia — often visible to the audience long before the character. Use it to analyze corporate and career blowups: ask what strength, overextended, produced the collapse, rather than hunting for an external villain.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Eight, 'The Great Financial Scandal of 2003', written summer 2000

models.name: Virtue effects and vice effects in economics
models.definition: Munger's insistence that moral qualities have first-order economic consequences: honesty, reliability, and trust lower transaction costs and raise output, while cheating and sloth impose real losses that standard economics leaves out of the model. Use it when a purely quantitative analysis of a business or a system produces a number that ignores whether the people in it can be trusted.
models.mungerCitation: Herb Kay Lecture, 'Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults', UCSB, 2003

models.name: Wisdom acquisition as a moral duty
models.definition: The position that learning is not a hobby or a career tactic but an obligation — you owe it to those who depend on you to become less wrong over time, and to go to bed wiser than you woke. Deploy it against the plateau that follows early competence, where people stop reading and start coasting on a fixed stock of models.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Ten, USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007

models.name: Anticipating trouble
aka: Train for ill and not for good
models.definition: Housman's counsel to train for ill and not for good — rehearse the disaster before it arrives so that when it comes you meet it already prepared rather than improvising in shock. Use it as the temperament behind pre-mortems, reserves, and redundancy: the cost of preparation is small and certain, the cost of surprise is large and random.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Ten, USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007

models.name: Eventual exposure
aka: The social system punishes massive cheating with disgrace
models.definition: Large-scale cheating is discovered — the social system eventually punishes it with disgrace, and the interval between the act and the exposure feels like safety but is only delay. Use it to correct the base rate intuition of anyone tempted by a fraud whose current concealment appears stable: the relevant question is not whether it will surface but what it costs when it does.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Eight, 'The Great Financial Scandal of 2003', written summer 2000

models.name: Franklin's empty sack
aka: It is hard for an empty sack to stand upright
models.definition: Franklin's maxim that it is hard for an empty sack to stand upright: financial precarity corrodes moral conduct, because a person or firm under existential money pressure will rationalize what a solvent one would refuse. Reach for it when designing incentives or judging character — solvency is a precondition of ethics, not merely a result of it.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Eight, 'The Great Financial Scandal of 2003', written summer 2000

models.name: Graded culpability
aka: Betrayal by trusted rule-makers is the worst sin
models.definition: Dante's moral architecture, in which the lowest circle is reserved not for the violent or the greedy but for traitors — those who betrayed a trust they voluntarily accepted. It supplies a ranking rule: the same act is far worse when committed by someone secure, knowing, trusted, and empowered to make the rules than by someone desperate or duped.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Eight, 'The Great Financial Scandal of 2003', written summer 2000

models.name: Internal compass: an ethical margin inside the legal line
aka: Stay well inside the legal line
models.definition: Conduct yourself well inside what the law permits, because the legal boundary is a lagging, gameable, and externally-defined edge — anyone who steers by it will eventually cross it by accident or by the drift of clever counsel. Reach for it whenever the argument for an action is that it is technically permissible.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms'; 2004 Wesco meeting remarks

models.name: Killing the goose that lays the golden eggs
models.definition: Aesop's warning that a renewable source of advantage will be destroyed by anyone who tries to extract its whole value at once — restraint is what preserves the exploitable resource. Apply it wherever a system is quietly generating value: reputations, customer trust, tax bases, fisheries, and the credulity of a market can all be harvested to death.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Eight, 'The Great Financial Scandal of 2003', written summer 2000

models.name: Ounce of prevention
aka: An ounce of prevention is worth a pound of cure
models.definition: Franklin's maxim that cheap early intervention dominates expensive late remediation, because the cost of a problem compounds while the cost of preventing it does not. Reach for it when a small, unglamorous fix now is competing against the option to wait — distinct from 'Anticipating trouble' (expect ill), this one is the cost-benefit case for acting on the expectation.
models.mungerCitation: 2001 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), "Asbestos litigation," p.155, https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf

models.name: Reduce your wants
aka: Lowered expectations as a happiness strategy
The best way to get happiness is to lower your expectations
models.definition: Happiness is a ratio of attainment to expectation, so the reliable lever is the denominator: deliberately want less rather than chase more. Reach for it when someone is trying to solve a satisfaction problem by increasing income, status, or acquisition.
models.mungerCitation: 2008 Wesco annual meeting notes (Peter Boodell), opening remarks, p.297, https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf

models.name: Treat those two impostors just the same
models.definition: Kipling's injunction to meet triumph and disaster with the same composure, treating both as unreliable verdicts on your judgment rather than as truths about your worth. Reach for it to guard against elation after wins as much as despair after losses — distinct from the Stoic 'adversity as opportunity' frame, since the target here is success as well as misfortune.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2013, notes of Charlie Munger's remarks, https://worldlypartners.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/2013-daily-journal-corp-annual-meeting-notes-of-charlie-mungers-remarks.pdf

models.name: Attitudes as tools
aka: Attitudes (patience, discipline, rationality, honesty, lowered expectations)
models.definition: Bevelin's packaging of the dispositional prerequisites for good judgment — patience, discipline, rationality, honesty, and deliberately lowered expectations — as themselves a mental model, on the view that temperament gates whether any other model gets used correctly. Reach for it when someone with excellent analysis keeps producing bad decisions; the defect is usually in disposition, not in the toolkit.
models.mungerCitation: Assembled from on-record Munger/Buffett life-advice quotes (e.g. on lowered expectations and rationality as a moral duty); 'attitudes' as a tool is Bevelin's framing

models.name: Critical and analytical reading
aka: Adler's How to Read a Book
models.definition: Mortimer Adler's four-level scheme — inspectional, systematic skimming, analytical, and syntopical reading — which treats reading as an active skill with distinct gears rather than one uniform activity. Use it to allocate effort: most material deserves a skim, a few books deserve to be argued with line by line, and the highest level, syntopical, means reading many authors against each other on one question.
models.mungerCitation: Munger's on-record advocacy of constant reading (Poor Charlie's Almanack, 2005); the Adler framework is Hagstrom's addition
models.mungerQuote: In my whole life, I have known no wise people... who didn't read all the time

models.name: Pragmatism
aka: Pragmatism (Peirce, William James, Dewey — truth defined by practical consequences)
models.definition: The Peirce–James–Dewey doctrine that the meaning and truth of an idea lie in its practical consequences — if two beliefs imply no different expectations, the dispute between them is empty. Use it to dissolve unresolvable arguments by asking what would actually differ in the world, and as a licence to keep whatever works while discarding ideological loyalty to a method.

models.name: Simile
models.definition: An explicit comparison marked as a comparison — X is *like* Y — which imports the analogy's explanatory power while flagging that the mapping is partial. Prefer it to metaphor when you want the listener to keep the difference in view rather than absorb the identification unexamined.

models.name: Meaning as use; description precedes explanation
aka: Failure to explain is caused by failure to describe (via Mandelbrot)
models.definition: Wittgenstein's later view that a word's meaning is its use in a language game rather than a fixed referent, paired with Mandelbrot's corollary that failure to explain is caused by failure to describe. Use it when a debate has become terminological, and as a working method: when explanation stalls, go back and describe the phenomenon more carefully before theorizing again.
models.mungerQuote: failure to explain is caused by failure to describe

models.name: Mental models theory of cognition
models.definition: Kenneth Craik's thesis that the mind works by building small-scale internal models of external reality and running them forward to anticipate events — which is why the quality of your models, not raw processing power, determines the quality of your predictions. It is the theoretical foundation of the latticework project itself: improve the models and you improve everything downstream.

models.name: Ontological vs. epistemological limits of knowledge
models.definition: The distinction between what cannot be known because the world is genuinely indeterminate (ontological) and what cannot be known because our instruments, models, or minds fall short (epistemological), sitting inside the broader metaphysics/epistemology/ethics map of what is, what we can know, and what we should do. Use it to classify an uncertainty before attacking it — more effort helps only against the second kind.

models.name: Paradigm shifts
models.definition: Kuhn's account in which a field does not update smoothly but accumulates anomalies inside a reigning paradigm until the framework breaks and is replaced wholesale — and where practitioners defend the old paradigm long past its usefulness because their status is built on it. Apply it to any discipline whose explanations require ever more epicycles, as economics did after 2008.

models.name: Realism
models.definition: The stance that there is a way the world is, independent of what anyone believes or would prefer, and that the job of thinking is to track it rather than to construct a comfortable account. It functions as a discipline against motivated reasoning: the world does not negotiate, and describing it accurately is prior to deciding what to do about it.

models.name: The detective method
aka: Habits of mind of Dupin, Holmes, Father Brown
models.definition: The composite habits of mind of Dupin, Sherlock Holmes, and Father Brown: skepticism toward the obvious account, exhaustive investigation, attention to the small anomalous detail, open-mindedness, explicit generation of alternative explanations, and reasoning about the psychology of the actors. Use it as a procedure for any inquiry where the first plausible story arrives too easily.

models.name: Allegory
models.definition: A narrative whose surface events systematically stand for a second, abstract structure — Plato's cave, Animal Farm — letting an argument be carried past defenses that would reject it stated plainly. Use it to teach something the audience is motivated not to hear; recognize it when a story is doing argumentative work while claiming to be mere description.

models.name: Categorical imperative
models.definition: Kant's rule that you may act only on a maxim you could will to become a universal law, and must treat persons always as ends and never merely as means. Its practical use is as a universalizability test on rationalized exceptions: the question 'what if everyone did this?' exposes the free-riding logic in most convenient ethical shortcuts.

models.name: Dichotomy of control
aka: What is up to us and what is not
models.definition: Epictetus's opening move in the Enchiridion: sort every element of a situation into what is up to you (your judgments, choices, effort) and what is not (outcomes, other people, the past), and invest emotion only in the first column. It is distinct from Stoic resilience — that is how to respond after the blow lands; this is the prior triage that determines what can wound you at all.

models.name: Hero's journey
models.definition: Campbell's monomyth — call, refusal, threshold, ordeal, transformation, return with the boon — the narrative skeleton audiences find satisfying across cultures. Its use is double-edged: it is a tool for structuring persuasion and for understanding why certain stories move people, and a warning that a real situation shaped to fit this arc is being distorted to be tellable.

models.name: Hubris and nemesis
models.definition: The tragic arc in which success breeds overreach, overreach invites the reckoning, and the reckoning is proportionate to the arrogance — the tragedy of ambition, distinct from the tragic flaw in that the flaw here is created by the winning. Use it to time skepticism: the most dangerous moment for a person or firm is shortly after a great and public triumph.

models.name: Irony
models.definition: The gap between what is said and what is meant, or between what an actor believes and what the audience knows (dramatic irony), or between intention and result (situational irony). As an analytic tool it directs attention to the gap itself: the most informative fact about a system is often the distance between its stated purpose and its actual effect.

models.name: Is–ought problem
models.definition: Hume's observation that no description of how things are entails a conclusion about how they ought to be — a normative premise must be smuggled in somewhere. Reach for it whenever data is offered as though it settled a values question ('this is natural/efficient/traditional, therefore right'); the argument's real premise is the hidden one.

models.name: Ship of Theseus
models.definition: If every plank of a ship is replaced one at a time, is it the same ship? — the puzzle of identity under gradual total replacement. Use it on companies, teams, brands, and institutions that have kept the name while turning over every person, product, and premise: the question of whether continuity is real or nominal has practical consequences for what you can infer from track record.

models.name: Socratic method
models.definition: Progress by interrogation rather than assertion: elicit a claim, press it with questions until its hidden contradictions surface, and let the interlocutor discover the flaw himself. Use it on your own positions as a discipline (what would have to be true for this to be false?) and on others where telling them directly would only trigger defense.

models.name: The examined life
aka: The unexamined life is not worth living
models.definition: Socrates's claim that the unexamined life is not worth living — that a life run on inherited assumptions, never audited, is being lived by someone else's defaults. It is the standing prompt to periodically ask why you hold your central beliefs and pursue your current goals, a review almost nobody schedules and everyone needs.

models.name: Utilitarianism
aka: Greatest good for the greatest number
models.definition: The consequentialist doctrine that the right act is the one producing the greatest aggregate welfare, which makes ethics a matter of measurement and trade-off rather than rule-following. Reach for it when rules produce absurd outcomes at scale — and hold it against its known failure modes: it will happily sacrifice an individual for a large enough sum, and the sums are usually guessed.

models.name: Veil of ignorance
models.definition: Rawls's device: choose the rules of a system without knowing which position in it you will occupy, which strips self-interest out of the design and forces attention to the worst-off seat. Reach for it when designing any policy, compensation scheme, or split where you are also a beneficiary — ask what you would choose if you were assigned your role at random afterward.

models.name: Virtue ethics
models.definition: Aristotle's alternative to rules and calculations: ask not 'what act is right?' but 'what would a person of good character do?', on the view that virtues are habits built by practice and that each sits as a mean between two vices (courage between cowardice and recklessness). Use it where rules run out and consequences are unforecastable — which is most real decisions.

models.name: Autocatalysis (self-accelerating reaction)
models.definition: A reaction whose own product catalyzes further reaction, so the process feeds itself and accelerates until a reagent runs out — the chemical template for reinforcing feedback in business (advertising that raises sales that fund more advertising, network effects, brand-building). Reach for it when small early advantages look like they could compound into runaway self-reinforcement rather than settle back down.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four, Poor Charlie's Almanack), July 20, 1996; also Stanford Law School 1996, 'A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom, Revisited' (Disney home-video example)

models.name: Breakpoints / phase transitions
models.definition: Systems often change state abruptly at a threshold rather than gradually — water is liquid at 33°F and ice at 31°F, and nothing in the smooth approach announces the flip. Reach for it when a small further change in a variable (price, dose, leverage, temperature, headcount) may not add a little but instead move the whole system into a qualitatively different regime.
models.mungerCitation: A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom (USC Business School), 1994; Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (UCSB), 2003
models.mungerQuote: the engineering idea of breakpoints — that's a very powerful model too
listedBy: Farnam Street (implied via Critical Mass citation)

models.name: Critical mass
aka: Critical mass (physics metaphor for accumulated expertise)
Critical mass / breakpoint — nonlinear results from added success factors
models.definition: Below a threshold of accumulated mass/energy a system merely fizzles; above it, the reaction becomes self-sustaining and results jump nonlinearly. Use it to judge whether an accumulation — of capital, customers, talent, distribution, or expertise — has crossed the point where it sustains itself, and to recognize that effort below critical mass yields nothing at all.
models.mungerCitation: A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom (USC Business School), 1994; The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight, written summer 2000); Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (UCSB), 2003
models.mungerQuote: the notion of a critical mass — that comes out of physics — is a very powerful model

models.name: Feynman's first principle — do not fool yourself
aka: Feynman's first principle: do not fool yourself (physicists' systematic self-criticism)
models.definition: The physicist's habit of systematic self-criticism: the easiest person to deceive is yourself, so you must actively hunt for the ways your own favored conclusion could be wrong. Reach for it before acting on any belief you would find costly to abandon — it is the discipline that makes every other model in the latticework trustworthy.
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998

models.name: Reductionism — explain in the most fundamental terms available
aka: Reductionism — explain in the most fundamental way possible (Boltzmann's constant)
models.definition: Physics discipline demands that an explanation be pushed down to the most basic principles that will carry it, rather than resting on a higher-level restatement of the phenomenon (Munger's example: Boltzmann's constant, and his complaint that economics too often names an effect instead of explaining it). Reach for it when an explanation feels satisfying but is really just a label — ask what underlying mechanism would have to be true.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: Scaling laws / square–cube law
aka: Scale and limits (mistakes from failing to consider that systems change with size)
Surface area (scaling of surfaces vs. volumes)
models.definition: Quantities scale with different powers of size — area with the square, volume and mass with the cube — so a design that works at one scale fails at ten times the scale for purely geometric reasons. Reach for it whenever someone proposes to 'just do the same thing bigger': the ratios that made it work do not survive the change of size.
models.mungerCitation: A Lesson on Elementary, Worldly Wisdom, USC Marshall, 1994 (scale effects are a centerpiece of the talk)

models.name: Complex-systems humility (limits of reductionism)
aka: Complex-systems humility (everything interacting; avoiding one-formula reductionism)
models.definition: The counterweight to reductionism: in systems where everything interacts with everything, no single formula or variable governs outcomes, and one-factor models mislead precisely where the stakes are highest. Use it to resist the pull of a tidy single explanation for messy multi-causal phenomena, and to insist on a checklist of interacting forces instead.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 2

models.name: Physics as a hard constraint / filter
aka: Laws of physics as a limit on what is possible
models.definition: Before evaluating a business, technology, or forecast on its economics, check it against what the laws of physics actually permit — physical limits are non-negotiable and rule out whole classes of proposals regardless of how attractive the projections look. Reach for it when a pitch depends on energy, materials, or scale performance that would have to violate or strain fundamental physical constraints.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2021, Q61, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2021/02/26/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journal-annual-meeting-2021/

models.name: Thermodynamics
aka: Thermodynamics (double-listed by Griffin under Chemistry and Physics; counted once)
models.definition: The accounting rules for energy in any real system: energy is conserved, it flows only from hot to cold without work being done, and every conversion exacts a tax. Use it as the general reminder that there are no free lunches — every output requires an input, and useful work must be paid for somewhere.
models.mungerCitation: 2002 Wesco annual meeting notes (Whitney Tilson), section "Importance of science," p.171, https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf

models.name: Equilibrium
aka: Equilibrium (law of supply and demand; Alfred Marshall's equilibrium economics applied to markets)
models.definition: A state in which opposing forces balance and the system stops changing on net — the physics idea economics borrowed for supply and demand. Use it to locate resting points and to ask what forces would have to shift for the balance to move; use it warily, since Munger flagged economists' 'physics envy' — social systems rarely sit still long enough for the equilibrium to be real.
models.mungerCitation: Discussed supply/demand and elasticity (USC 1994) and criticized equilibrium-style physics envy in Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (UCSB), Oct 3, 2003; he never listed equilibrium among his own models

models.name: Friction & efficiency losses
models.definition: No real process converts all input into useful output; some fraction is always dissipated resisting motion. Reach for it to account for the drag that theoretical models ignore — transaction costs, handoffs, approvals, taxes, and the human effort of overcoming inertia — and to notice that reducing friction is often cheaper than adding force.
models.mungerCitation: Munger's on-record 'frictional costs' concern intermediation costs (croupiers, 'febezzlement'; 1998 foundation talk), not physics friction; the physics framing is a compiler's

models.name: Leverage
models.definition: A lever multiplies an applied force in proportion to its arm — and multiplies it in both directions, amplifying error as readily as effort. Use it to locate the point where modest input produces outsized output (capital, code, media, a key relationship), while remembering that every lever also magnifies whatever goes wrong.
models.mungerCitation: Munger discussed financial leverage constantly but did not present 'leverage' as a physics mental model

models.name: Bohr model
models.definition: A known-to-be-wrong picture of the atom that nevertheless predicts hydrogen's spectrum and remains the working image most people carry. Use it as the canonical case of a useful approximation: models earn their keep by being wrong at a scale that does not matter for the question at hand — and by being retired when it does.

models.name: Econophysics
aka: Econophysics (classical mechanics → relativity/Keynes-Minsky → quantum/complexity progression in Hagstrom's 2013 telling)
models.definition: The program of importing successive physics paradigms into economics — classical mechanics, then relativity and Keynes–Minsky instability, then quantum and complexity views. Its value in the lattice is historical rather than predictive: it shows how each generation borrows the physics of its day as a metaphor and mistakes the metaphor for a mechanism.

models.name: Electromagnetism
models.definition: Attraction and repulsion mediated by fields rather than contact — action at a distance, with like repelling like and opposites attracting, and electricity and magnetism revealed as one phenomenon seen from two angles. Its lattice use is as the model of invisible field effects: influence that acts across a gap and unification of what looked like two separate forces.

models.name: Inertia
models.definition: A body at rest stays at rest and a body in motion stays in motion unless acted on by an outside force — mass resists any change in state. Reach for it to explain why organizations, habits and markets persist long past their justification, and to budget for the force required to start something as well as the force required to stop it.

models.name: Kinetics (reaction rates)
models.definition: Whether a reaction can happen (thermodynamics) is a separate question from how fast it happens (kinetics) — diamonds are unstable relative to graphite yet last forever because the rate is effectively zero. Reach for it whenever an outcome is inevitable in principle but the timing is what actually matters: markets, technologies and institutions can be doomed for decades before anything visible occurs.

models.name: Mechanistic (Newtonian) worldview and physics envy
aka: Newtonian mechanics / mechanistic-reductionist worldview (Newton, Descartes) as the template of classical economics
models.definition: The Newton–Descartes template — a clockwork universe of deterministic parts — that classical economics adopted wholesale, importing precise-looking equations into a domain that does not obey them. Reach for it to spot false rigor: mathematics borrowed from physics can lend an argument credibility it has not earned.

models.name: Momentum
models.definition: Mass times velocity: a moving system carries stored effort that keeps it going and makes it costly to redirect. Use it to see why a big organization already in motion can beat a better idea starting from rest, and why the right moment to change direction is before the momentum is built.

models.name: Newton's laws of motion
models.definition: Three rules that govern nearly all everyday mechanics: inertia, force equals mass times acceleration, and every action provokes an equal and opposite reaction. The third is the most transferable — expect any forceful intervention in a human system to generate a counter-force of comparable size.

models.name: Nonlinearity
models.definition: Output is not proportional to input: doubling the cause may produce ten times, or one-tenth, the effect, and small perturbations can dominate outcomes. Use it as a standing warning against straight-line extrapolation and against averaging — in a nonlinear world the average of the inputs does not give the response at the average.

models.name: Quantum mechanics
models.definition: At small scales, reality is discrete, probabilistic and observer-entangled rather than continuous and determinate. Its transferable lesson is epistemic humility about scale: rules that hold reliably at one level of a system may have no counterpart at another, so do not assume the macro intuition survives the descent.

models.name: Relativity
models.definition: Measurements of time, length and simultaneity depend on the observer's frame; there is no privileged vantage point from which to read off the absolute values. Use it to remember that your view of a situation is a frame-dependent measurement — and that people who disagree may be reading the same reality from a different frame rather than lying.

models.name: Resilience vs. fragility
models.definition: Systems differ in how they respond to shock: fragile ones break, robust ones absorb, and a few gain from disorder — and the difference is mostly structural (slack, optionality, independence of parts), not predictive skill. Reach for it when you cannot forecast the shock and must instead design to survive it.

models.name: Shannon's law (channel capacity)
models.definition: Any communication channel has a hard maximum rate of reliable information transfer, set by its bandwidth and its signal-to-noise ratio — beyond it, errors are unavoidable no matter how clever the coding. Use it to reason about the limits of any pipe carrying information, including meetings, reporting lines and dashboards: adding volume past capacity adds noise, not signal.

models.name: Uncertainty principle
models.definition: Certain paired quantities (position and momentum) cannot both be known precisely — the precision of one is bought with the imprecision of the other, and measurement itself disturbs the system. Reach for it whenever measuring changes what you measure: metrics that are watched get gamed, and demanding precision on one dimension often destroys it on another.

models.name: Viscosity
models.definition: A fluid's internal resistance to flow — honey and water obey the same laws but at wildly different rates. Reach for it to describe how thick a medium is: capital, information and talent move through some markets and organizations freely and through others barely at all, and the viscosity often matters more than the pressure applied.

models.name: Activation energy
models.definition: Even a reaction that releases energy will not start until an initial barrier is cleared — the match must be struck. Reach for it when a change is obviously worthwhile yet nothing happens: the problem is usually the up-front hump, not the payoff, and the fix is to lower the barrier or supply a burst of energy to clear it.

models.name: Catalysis
models.definition: A catalyst lowers the activation energy of a reaction and speeds it enormously without being consumed — it changes the rate, never the equilibrium. Use it to find the small, reusable intervention that unlocks a process already wanting to happen (a person, a tool, a deadline), and to remember that a catalyst cannot make a thermodynamically impossible reaction go.

models.name: Conservation laws
models.definition: Certain quantities — energy, momentum, mass, charge — are neither created nor destroyed, only moved or transformed, which makes them the accountant's tool of physics: if it left here it went somewhere. Reach for it to force closure on any accounting: costs that vanish from one budget reappear in another, risk that is 'eliminated' has actually been transferred to someone.

models.name: Diffusion
models.definition: Random motion of particles reliably carries them from high to low concentration, producing directed, predictable spread with no directing agent — and slowly, since diffusion time grows with the square of distance. Use it as the default model for how things spread without a plan (ideas, people, contamination), and as a reminder that undirected spread is dependable over short ranges and hopeless over long ones.

models.name: Entropy and the second law
models.definition: Isolated systems drift toward disorder, and reversing that drift always costs energy imported from outside. Reach for it whenever something orderly is deteriorating on its own — codebases, organizations, relationships, gardens — and recognize that maintenance is not optional overhead but the price of not decaying.

models.name: Escape velocity
models.definition: Below a threshold speed a projectile falls back no matter how high it climbs; at or above it, it leaves the gravity well for good. Reach for it when partial effort is genuinely worthless — some ventures, habits and reforms must clear a threshold or fail entirely, which makes half-funding them strictly worse than not starting.

models.name: Half-life / exponential decay
models.definition: Quantities that decay in proportion to their current size lose a fixed fraction per interval, so the same period always halves what remains and the tail never quite reaches zero. Reach for it to reason about how fast things fade — skills, knowledge, customer cohorts, drug concentrations, outrage — and to distinguish 'it's declining' from 'it will be gone'.

models.name: Hysteresis
models.definition: The state of a system depends on its history, not just current conditions — magnetize iron and it stays partly magnetized after the field is removed, so the path down is not the path up. Reach for it when reversing a cause fails to reverse the effect: reputations, prices, unemployment and trust do not simply retrace when the pressure is lifted.

models.name: Laminar vs. turbulent flow
models.definition: Below a critical velocity a fluid flows in smooth predictable layers; above it, the same fluid in the same pipe breaks into chaotic eddies where prediction collapses and resistance jumps. Use it as the model of regime change in predictability itself — the identical system can be forecastable at one intensity and genuinely unforecastable at a slightly higher one.

models.name: Le Chatelier's principle
models.definition: A system at equilibrium responds to an imposed change by shifting so as to partially offset it — press on it and it pushes back. Reach for it to predict the counter-adaptation to any policy, price change or incentive: the system will absorb part of your intervention, so the naive first-order forecast will overstate the effect.

models.name: Potential vs. kinetic energy
models.definition: Energy stored by position or configuration (a raised weight, a compressed spring, a stressed fault line) is invisible until it converts into motion, at which point it is released all at once. Reach for it to see accumulated, non-obvious stored force — deferred maintenance, unspoken grievance, built-up leverage — and to ask what would trigger the conversion.

models.name: Resonance
models.definition: Small, repeated inputs at a system's natural frequency accumulate into enormous amplitude, while the same energy applied off-frequency does almost nothing. Use it to explain why timing and matching beat force: the message, the product or the push that lands in phase with an audience's existing rhythm gets a response wildly out of proportion to its energy — and why unchecked resonance destroys bridges.

models.name: 'Dollop-by-Dollop' Gradualism
models.definition: Because perception registers only contrast, a change delivered in increments small enough to escape notice can accumulate to an enormous total that would have been rejected outright if proposed at once — the boiling-frog mechanism weaponized in accounting fraud. Use it to audit any trend that has moved a long way through steps each of which seemed unremarkable.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight, Poor Charlie's Almanack; written summer 2000)

models.name: 'Niederhoffering' a System (gaming a human institution through its incentives)
aka: Gaming a human system by exploiting its incentives
models.definition: Any system with measurable rewards will be reverse-engineered and gamed by its cleverest participants, who satisfy the metric while defeating the purpose (Niederhoffer taking only the softest courses to top the Harvard rankings). Reach for it before you install any metric, quota, or scoring rule on smart people.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: 'Rational Man' vs. 'Foolish Man' Models
models.definition: You need both models of human behavior and must know which regime you are in: the rational-actor model works for market-wide, incentive-driven aggregates, while the foolish-man model explains most individual and institutional behavior. Reach for it before importing an economics model into a human situation.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000 (Talk Seven)

models.name: 'Rub-Off' Effects (reputational contagion by association)
models.definition: Disgrace spreads by association to innocent neighbors — one fraudulent firm taints its whole industry, auditor, and analyst coverage, because the mind cannot quarantine the stain. Use it when assessing reputational risk from partners, clients, or peers you do not control.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight, Poor Charlie's Almanack; written summer 2000)

models.name: 'Wisely Restrained Falsehood'
aka: Plausibility constraint — fraud calibrated to not strain credulity
models.definition: Sophisticated deceit is calibrated to sit just below detection and credulity thresholds — the fraud that succeeds is the modest one that never strains belief, not the spectacular one. Reach for it when a set of numbers is suspiciously, consistently just-good-enough.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight, Poor Charlie's Almanack; written summer 2000)

models.name: Admiration-Based Love (including the eminent dead)
aka: Make friends among the eminent dead
models.definition: We become what we admire, and admiration is the one form of love that reliably improves the admirer — so choose heroes deliberately, and the eminent dead make superb ones because they are patient, available, and never disappoint. Reach for it when selecting mentors, bosses, and reading.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Talk Ten: USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007

models.name: Appeal to Interest, Not to Reason (Franklin's rule)
models.definition: Franklin's persuasion maxim: if you would convince, appeal to interest rather than to reason — arguments that threaten a listener's self-interest will lose to arguments that serve it, no matter the logic. Reach for it when you must change a mind that has money or status riding on the current answer.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003; Talk Ten, USC Gould School of Law, 2007

models.name: Authority-Misinfluence Tendency
models.definition: Humans follow leaders automatically, and will obey a badly-reasoned or misheard instruction from an authority figure to the point of catastrophe (the co-pilot who lets the captain crash the plane). Reach for it when designing systems that must survive a wrong order, and pick carefully whom you work under — you will absorb them.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #22; Talk Ten, USC Gould, 2007 ('work only under people you admire')

models.name: Availability-Misweighing Tendency
aka: Over-influence by vivid or the most recent information
models.definition: The mind weighs what is easy to recall — vivid, recent, or emotionally charged — far above what is merely true, and treats what it cannot easily retrieve as though it did not exist. The remedy is deliberate checklists and forced attention to the absent, undramatic evidence.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 ('over-influence by extra-vivid evidence') / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #18
models.mungerQuote: An idea or a fact is not worth more merely because it is easily available.

models.name: Conditioned Reflexes in Brand Building
aka: Brand business as stimulus-response creation and maintenance
models.definition: A great consumer brand is a manufactured stimulus-response reflex — trademark, taste, imagery and ubiquity fused so that the cue triggers purchase without deliberation, and the reflex must be continually maintained by advertising. Reach for it when valuing a consumer franchise or judging whether a moat is psychological rather than economic.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four, Poor Charlie's Almanack), July 20, 1996

models.name: Contrast-Misreaction Tendency
aka: Contrast-caused distortions of sensation, perception and cognition
models.definition: The mind registers differences, not absolutes, so judgment is set by whatever it was just compared against — the $1,000 option looks cheap after the $65,000 car, and the mediocre spouse looks fine next to a terrible one. Reach for it whenever a choice is presented in a sequence someone else controls.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #16

models.name: Curiosity Tendency
models.definition: Humans have an innate drive to know, and — uniquely among the tendencies — it is a benign one that great educational cultures amplify into lifelong learning and compounding advantage. Reach for it when choosing what to cultivate in yourself or select for in others.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #6

models.name: Deferred Gratification
aka: Deferred gratification plus a weird mix of patience and aggression
Delayed gratification as an engine of compounding
models.definition: The disposition to forgo present consumption and present action in exchange for a much larger later payoff — the psychological precondition for compounding in money, skill, and reputation. Reach for it when assessing whether an individual or institution can actually hold a position long enough for the arithmetic of compounding to work.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2017, opening remarks, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2017/02/26/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journal-annual-meeting-2017/; Charlie Munger at Caltech, 2020, https://assets.empirefinancialresearch.com/uploads/2020/12/Munger-Caltech-Interview-transcript-12-18-20-Empire-Financial-Research.pdf

models.name: Deprival-Superreaction Tendency
models.definition: Losing something — or merely almost getting it and then not — hurts far more than the equivalent gain pleases, so people fight harder to avoid removals than to secure additions. Reach for it to explain labor disputes over small takeaways, gambling behavior after near misses, and why cutting a long-enjoyed benefit costs more political capital than never granting it.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #14; Talk Four (1996); Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998

models.name: Disliking/Hating Tendency
aka: Tendency to distort due to disliking/hating
models.definition: The mirror of liking: we ignore virtues in people we dislike, dislike everything associated with them, and distort facts to maintain the hatred — the engine of feuds, litigation, and tribal war. Reach for it when you notice yourself unable to concede a single point to an opponent.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #3

models.name: Doubt-Avoidance Tendency
models.definition: The brain is built to banish doubt by reaching a decision fast — a survival adaptation for prey animals that becomes a defect for judges, jurors, and investors. It is triggered by puzzlement and stress, which is exactly why salesmen manufacture both; the remedy is to deliberately defer conclusions on important questions.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #4

models.name: Drug-Misinfluence Tendency
models.definition: Chemical dependency destroys judgment while simultaneously destroying the ability to perceive that judgment is destroyed, and it operates through denial — the decline is always downhill and rarely reversed. Munger's rule is avoidance of the whole class of substances, because you cannot manage what disables your own detection.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #20

models.name: Easy Money Causes Regrettable Behavior ('Munger's rule')
aka: Large amounts of easy money cause bad human behavior
models.definition: Munger's rule that when very large sums become available easily and quickly, human behavior reliably degrades — fraud, promotion, and self-delusion follow the money rather than the people. Reach for it when a sector, product, or compensation scheme suddenly makes enormous money available with little friction, as a prior on coming misconduct regardless of who is involved.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2016, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2016/03/07/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journal-annual-meeting-2016/

models.name: Envy/Jealousy Tendency
aka: Envy as driver of spending (Mandeville's Fable of the Bees)
Envy as the one sin with no upside
Envy avoidance through plainly deserved success
models.definition: People are driven less by absolute desire than by the intolerable fact that someone nearby is doing better — envy explains compensation fights, luxury spending, and institutional herding into whatever peers own. It is the one sin with no upside: nobody enjoys it, so the remedy is simply to refuse to play, ignoring relative performance entirely.
models.mungerCitation: Talk One, Harvard School Commencement, 1986; The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / PCA 2005 — Tendency #8; Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003; Talk Ten, USC Gould, 2007
models.mungerQuote: The world is not driven by greed, it's driven by envy.

models.name: Excessive Self-Regard Tendency
models.definition: People systematically appraise themselves — and their own possessions, decisions, and hires — far above any external assessment (90% of Swedish drivers rate themselves above average). Reach for it before trusting your own self-evaluation, and expect it to make people overvalue what they already own and whoever they personally recruited.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #12; Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998; Talk Five Revisited (2006)

models.name: Extinction of Conditioned Reflexes
aka: Blocking competitors' habit formation via universal availability
