models.definition: Conditioned responses decay when the reinforcement stops — and a competitor's reflex can be pre-emptively blocked by making your own product universally available so the rival's cue is never paired with reward. Use it offensively (deny a competitor the chance to form habits) and defensively (never let your own reinforcement lapse).
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four, Poor Charlie's Almanack), July 20, 1996

models.name: First-Conclusion Bias
models.definition: The mind is like the human egg: once one conclusion gets in, a shutter closes and all rival ideas are locked out. Use it to justify forcing a genuine second and third hypothesis onto the table before the first one hardens.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995; Poor Charlie's Almanack (label 'first-conclusion bias' popularized by Farnam Street)
models.mungerQuote: The mind of man is a lot like the human egg.

models.name: Getting Rid of Old Ideas (Keynes)
aka: Keynes: removing wrong ideas is the hard part
models.definition: The difficulty is not getting new ideas in but getting old ones out — accumulated wrong beliefs are the binding constraint on thinking, and they must be actively destroyed rather than passively outgrown. Reach for it as a periodic maintenance ritual on your own convictions.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (The ethos of not fooling yourself)

models.name: Groupthink (Janis)
aka: Suppression of dissent in cohesive groups
models.definition: A cohesive decision-making group converges on a course of action by suppressing dissent and reality-testing in order to preserve unanimity and cohesion — distinct from social proof, which is copying strangers, in that groupthink is an in-group actively silencing its own doubters. Reach for it when a board, committee, or investment team reaches suspicious unanimity fast.
models.mungerCitation: A Conversation with Charlie Munger, University of Michigan Ross, 2017, https://worldlypartners.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/01/2017-a-conversation-with-charlie-munger-at-university-of-michigan-ross.pdf

models.name: Ideology as Mind-Fetter
models.definition: Intense ideology cabbages the mind — once a belief becomes identity, every incoming fact is processed to defend it, and objectivity is permanently gone. Munger's remedy: state the opposing case better than its advocates can before you are entitled to hold your own opinion, and avoid heavy ideology altogether.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998; Talk Ten, USC Gould School of Law, May 13, 2007

models.name: Incentive-Caused Bias
aka: Underestimating bias from own self-interest and incentives
models.definition: Distinct from mere behavioral response to incentives: self-interest silently corrupts *cognition*, so professionals sincerely believe whatever serves them — the salesman, consultant, or auditor is not lying, he has genuinely convinced himself. Apply extreme skepticism to advice from anyone whose pay depends on the recommendation.
models.mungerCitation: Named verbatim in The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995; Talk Four (1996); Talk Five (1998); Talk Six (1998); Talk Eight (2000); Talk Ten (2007)
models.mungerQuote: Whose bread I eat, his song I sing.

models.name: Inconsistency-Avoidance Tendency (commitment and consistency)
models.definition: The mind resists change, so conclusions once reached — and identities once adopted — get defended rather than re-examined; this is why a small public commitment reliably produces a large later one, and why auditors who blessed last year's books bless this year's. Reach for it when someone must reverse a prior position, and never let a first commitment be extracted from you cheaply.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #5; applied in Talk Eight (2000)

models.name: Influence-from-Mere-Association Tendency
aka: Classical / Pavlovian conditioning — power of mere association
models.definition: Judgment is warped by pure association with no causal link — shiny models beside cars, the messenger blamed for bad news, past success in an unrelated context taken as competence. Reach for it whenever a conclusion rests on something merely adjacent to the evidence rather than the evidence itself.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 ('bias from mere association') / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #10; Talk Four (1996); Talk Eight (2000)

models.name: Intense Interest as Prerequisite for Excellence
models.definition: No one performs at the top of a field they are not intensely interested in; passion beats raw IQ as a predictor of mastery because it supplies the volume of practice. Use it when choosing a career, a partner, or an operator to back.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Talk Ten: USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007

models.name: Investor Temperament (patience plus decisiveness; the 'right stuff')
aka: Extreme patience combined with extreme decisiveness

The Right Stuff attributes (Griffin's 13: patient, disciplined, calm/courageous/decisive, intelligent but not misled by high IQ, honest, confident and nonideological, long-term oriented, passionate, studious, collegial, sound temperament, frugal, risk averse)
models.definition: Success in judgment-heavy fields is a temperament question more than an IQ question: the winning profile is patient, disciplined, calm but courageous when acting, honest, nonideological, long-term oriented, studious, frugal, and not misled by its own high IQ. Reach for it when selecting people or auditing yourself — the trait list predicts better than the résumé.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 2 (Charlie Sums It Up) & Ch. 3 (Importance of Temperament)

models.name: Kantian Fairness Tendency
models.definition: People spontaneously extend and expect fair, reciprocal treatment (drivers merging by turns), and react with disproportionate fury when the norm is violated — even at cost to themselves. Reach for it when designing rules that must feel legitimate, and to predict blowups over perceived unfairness that look economically irrational.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #7

models.name: Liking/Loving Tendency
aka: Bias from over-influence by liking tendency
Tendency to distort due to liking/loving
models.definition: We distort judgment toward those we like: ignoring their faults, favoring their people and products, and warping facts to keep the affection intact. Use it to check why a deal, a hire, or an argument feels right — you may be responding to the person, not the merits.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #2

models.name: Lollapalooza Effect
aka: Bias from the combined effect of many psychological tendencies operating together
Confluence of biases acting in concert
Lollapalooza effect (confluence of forces acting in one direction)
Lollapalooza effects — extreme outcomes from large combinations of factors
Lollapalooza results (confluence of factors producing extreme outcomes)
models.definition: When several psychological tendencies act in the same direction at once, their combined force is not additive but multiplicative, producing extreme outcomes (Tupperware parties, cults, bubbles, open-outcry auctions). Use it to explain results that look far too large for any single cause, and to spot situations engineered to stack biases.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #25, 'Lollapalooza Tendency'

models.name: Low Expectations as the First Rule of a Happy Life
aka: First rule of a happy life is low expectations
Unrealistic expectations as a source of misery
models.definition: Satisfaction is a function of outcome relative to expectation, so the cheapest available lever on well-being is lowering the expectation rather than raising the outcome; unrealistic expectations are self-inflicted misery. Reach for it when diagnosing chronic disappointment in a person, a workforce, or an investor base whose absolute circumstances are objectively good.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2021, Q67, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2021/02/26/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journal-annual-meeting-2021/; CNBC, "Charlie Munger: A Life of Wit and Wisdom," 2023, https://www.cnbc.com/2023/11/30/full-transcript-from-cnbcs-charlie-munger-a-life-of-wit-and-wisdom-.html

models.name: Man-with-a-Hammer Tendency
models.definition: To the man with only a hammer, every problem looks like a nail — specialists force every question into the one model they own, which is the core argument for holding a latticework of models from many disciplines. Reach for it whenever an expert's diagnosis happens to require exactly the expert's specialty.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four), 1996; The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995; USC 1994; Talk Five (1998); Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Operant (Skinnerian) Conditioning
models.definition: Behavior is shaped by its consequences: reward it and it repeats, and variable-ratio reward schedules produce the most persistent behavior of all. Distinct from mere association — use it when designing training, compensation, or any system meant to produce a durable habit.
models.mungerCitation: Practical Thought About Practical Thought? (Talk Four, Poor Charlie's Almanack), July 20, 1996

models.name: Overconfidence (including procedure-induced overconfidence)
models.definition: Confidence in a judgment rises faster than its accuracy, and elaborate procedure — models, committees, consultants, diligence checklists — inflates confidence without improving the estimate. Reach for it whenever a process makes you feel more certain; ask whether the certainty came from new information or just from ritual.
models.mungerCitation: Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998 (Talk Six)

models.name: Overoptimism Tendency
aka: Believing what you want to believe
models.definition: People believe what they wish were true, independent of evidence — Demosthenes' rule, visible in every forecast, business plan, and pension-return assumption. The remedy is not cheerfulness but trained use of probability math applied against your own hopes.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #13; Investment Practices of Leading Charitable Foundations, 1998
models.mungerQuote: What a man wishes, that also will he believe.

models.name: Overweighing What Can Be Counted
aka: Calculate too much, think too little
models.definition: The measurable crowds out the important: factors with clean numbers get full weight while decisive but unquantifiable factors get zero, producing precise wrong answers. Reach for it whenever a model's inputs happen to be exactly the things that were easy to obtain.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003; Poor Charlie's Almanack Ch. 3 (Common (and uncommon) Sense)

models.name: Psychology of Human Misjudgment (the standard-causes-of-error checklist)
aka: Psychology of misjudgment / predictable patterns of irrationality
Standard causes of human error checklist
models.definition: The umbrella model: human misjudgment is not random but follows a finite, enumerable set of subconscious tendencies that can be listed, memorized, and run as a checklist against any decision. Reach for it whenever you need a systematic sweep for error rather than an ad-hoc gut check.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard Law School, 1995; expanded to 25 tendencies in Poor Charlie's Almanack, 2005; foreshadowed in USC 1994 ('roughly 20 little principles')

models.name: Reason-Respecting Tendency
models.definition: People comply far better when given a reason — and, per Langer's copy-machine study, they comply almost as well when the 'reason' is meaningless, which makes the tendency both a teaching tool and an exploit. Use it constructively by always telling people why, and defensively by asking whether the reason offered actually explains anything.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #24 (Langer copy-machine study)

models.name: Reciprocation Tendency
models.definition: The automatic drive to repay favors, concessions, and hostilities in kind — powerful enough that a trivial unsolicited gift buys a disproportionate return, which is why it is the workhorse of sales and lobbying. Use it constructively to build cooperation, and defensively by refusing small gifts from anyone who will later ask you for something.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #9; Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003

models.name: Resentment and Revenge Tendency (and the Disraeli compromise)
aka: Revenge as a disastrous mode of thought
models.definition: Distinct from envy: resentment is grievance at having been wronged, and it corrodes the holder far more than the target, driving revenge-seeking that destroys value on both sides. Munger's practical remedy is the 'Disraeli compromise' — forgive and move on, but keep a private list of enemies and let time do the work.
models.mungerCitation: Talk One, Harvard School Commencement Speech, Harvard School, Los Angeles, June 13, 1986; Talk Ten, USC Gould, 2007

models.name: Reward and Punishment Superresponse Tendency
aka: Perverse institutional incentives / incentives can be changed (power of incentives)
Underestimating the power of rewards and punishment
models.definition: People respond to incentives far more strongly than almost anyone forecasts — incentives are a superpower, and behavior will bend to whatever the system actually pays for, not what it says it wants. When you cannot understand why someone is behaving badly or bizarrely, look first at what they are being rewarded for.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 (Tendency #1); Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005; Academic Economics (UCSB), 2003
models.mungerQuote: Never, ever, think about something else when you should be thinking about the power of incentives

models.name: Self-Pity
aka: Self-pity as a disastrous mode of thought
models.definition: Self-pity is always counterproductive and is the near-universal gateway to paranoia — it converts misfortune into identity and paralyzes response. Reach for it as a personal tripwire: the moment you feel it, treat it as evidence of malfunction, not information about the world.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Talk Ten: USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007

models.name: Self-Serving Bias
models.definition: We attribute our successes to skill and our failures to circumstance, and we rationalize whatever profits us as also being right — evil rewarded dies hard, because the reward blinds the actor to the evil. Munger's rule: fight it in yourself, and expect and allow for it in everyone else, including good people.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Talk Ten: USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007; Talk Eight Revisited (postscript)

models.name: Senescence-Misinfluence Tendency
models.definition: Cognitive decline with age is real, arrives unevenly, and is largely invisible to the person declining — though continuous practice of well-learned skills slows it. Reach for it in succession planning, in evaluating aging decision-makers, and in setting your own exit rules while you can still make them.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #21

models.name: Serpico Effect (everyone-does-it rationalization)
models.definition: Once corruption is universal inside a group, social proof plus incentive-caused bias makes participation feel normal and refusal feel like betrayal — the honest cop becomes the deviant. Use it to predict that isolated integrity inside a rotten system will not survive, and that the system, not the individual, must be fixed.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Earnings Manipulation and Accounting Shenanigans)

models.name: Simple, Pain-Avoiding Psychological Denial
models.definition: When reality is too painful to bear, the mind simply refuses to register it — the mother who insists her dead son is alive, the executive who cannot see the failing division. Reach for it when someone's blindness is too total to be explained by ignorance or incentive.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #11

models.name: Social-Proof Tendency
aka: Crowd Folly / Avoiding the Madness of Crowds
models.definition: When uncertain, people take their cue from what others do — and uncertainty plus stress makes the effect overwhelming, producing lemming-like herding in crowds, boards, and entire industries. Reach for it to explain bubbles, corporate conventionality, and why 'everyone else is doing it' feels like evidence when it is only echo.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #15; Talk One (1986); Talk Four (1996); Talk Seven, Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000; Talk Eight (2000)
models.mungerQuote: crowd folly

models.name: Stress-Influence Tendency
models.definition: Light stress sharpens performance; heavy stress produces sudden, non-linear breakdown and, as Pavlov's flooded dogs showed, makes people radically reprogrammable — which is exactly why every persuasion system manufactures stress first. Reach for it when judging decisions made under pressure, and never let an important commitment be extracted from you while stressed.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #17

models.name: The Wooden Effect (concentrate the playing time on your best players)
models.definition: Munger's name for John Wooden's practice of giving nearly all the playing time to his seven best players — output rises when effort and opportunity are concentrated on the most able rather than spread evenly. Reach for it when allocating scarce capital, attention, or authority across people or projects of unequal quality.
models.mungerCitation: CNBC, "Charlie Munger: A Life of Wit and Wisdom," 2023, https://www.cnbc.com/2023/11/30/full-transcript-from-cnbcs-charlie-munger-a-life-of-wit-and-wisdom-.html

models.name: Twaddle Tendency
models.definition: People compulsively produce talk — chattering away with no signal — and the twaddle of the useless drags down the productivity of the useful, which is why serious organizations must be structured to keep prattlers away from work. Reach for it in meeting design, hiring, and in noticing when you are speaking to fill silence rather than to say something.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 ('say-something syndrome') / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #23

models.name: Upton Sinclair's Rule (a salary blocks understanding)
aka: It's difficult to get a man to understand something when his salary depends on not understanding it
models.definition: Sinclair's maxim that a man cannot be made to understand a thing when his paycheck depends on his not understanding it — cognition itself is bought, not merely conclusions. Reach for it when evaluating the sincerity of an expert, auditor, analyst, or executive whose livelihood is contingent on the answer.
models.mungerCitation: Harvard-Westlake School talk, 2010, https://pdfcoffee.com/110302239-munger-talk-at-harvard-westlakepdf-pdf-free.html; also 2010 Wesco annual meeting notes, Part I, p.322, https://www.valueplays.net/wp-content/uploads/The-Best-of-Charlie-Munger-1994-2011.pdf

models.name: Use-It-or-Lose-It Tendency
models.definition: Skills and models decay without practice, and the ones you use rarely are exactly the ones you will fumble when they matter — which is why Munger insisted on practicing all his models, not just the convenient ones. Reach for it to justify deliberate rehearsal of rarely used but high-stakes competence.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 / Poor Charlie's Almanack 2005 — Tendency #19

models.name: Chains of Habit
models.definition: The chains of habit are too light to be felt until they are too heavy to be broken — habits, good and bad, compound invisibly and then become identity. Reach for it when a small recurring behavior looks costless: the decision is not about today's instance but about the chain.
models.mungerCitation: Talk One, Harvard School Commencement Speech, Harvard School, Los Angeles, June 13, 1986

models.name: Chunked Basic Knowledge (pattern recognition through fluency)
aka: Skilled intuition as pattern recognition / chunking (Herbert Simon)
models.definition: Mastery comes from having the fundamentals so thoroughly chunked that whole configurations are perceived at once — the blindfold chess player sees positions, not pieces. Reach for it when deciding whether to learn the basics cold or lean on lookup: only chunked knowledge composes into judgment.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Common (and uncommon) Sense)

models.name: Cult Conversion Methods (extreme persuasion)
models.definition: Cults reliably convert ordinary people by stacking social proof, authority, reciprocation, commitment, isolation, and stress into a single lollapalooza — proof that persuasion is engineering, not character. Reach for it to recognize the same toolkit at work in sales organizations, ideological movements, and hard-charging corporate cultures.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: Decision Fatigue (and running ordinary life on autopilot)
aka: Routinizing trivial decisions to preserve judgment
models.definition: Deliberate judgment is a depletable resource, so trivial recurring choices should be routinized or eliminated to reserve capacity for the few decisions that matter. Reach for it when designing a working life or a decision process that must stay sharp for rare, high-stakes calls.
models.mungerCitation: Berkshire Hathaway Annual Meeting 2013, Morning Session, https://buffett.cnbc.com/video/2013/05/04/morning-session---2013-berkshire-hathaway-annual-meeting.html

models.name: Extrapolating the Recent Past
aka: Beware simple extrapolation of past trends
models.definition: Forecasters project whatever just happened indefinitely forward — the mechanism behind pension funds assuming bull-market returns forever and behind most cyclical ruin. Reach for it whenever a projection's slope matches the last few years' slope with no independent reason.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (The Scandal of American Pension Fund Accounting)

models.name: Face-Saving (the cost of being right out loud)
models.definition: People resent being shown to be wrong more than they value being corrected, so demonstrating superior insight often costs you more than the insight earns — Munger's advice is to keep the brilliance to yourself and let the decision speak. Reach for it before winning an argument you need the other person to implement.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack, Talk Ten: USC Gould School of Law Commencement Address, May 13, 2007

models.name: Hedonic Adaptation
aka: Fancy houses don't make you happier
models.definition: People rapidly re-baseline to improved material circumstances, so the anticipated happiness from a bigger house, a better car, or more money largely evaporates while the cost and obligation persist. Reach for it when forecasting the utility of an acquisition — personal or corporate — that is being justified by how good it will feel.
models.mungerCitation: CNBC, "Charlie Munger: A Life of Wit and Wisdom," 2023, https://www.cnbc.com/2023/11/30/full-transcript-from-cnbcs-charlie-munger-a-life-of-wit-and-wisdom-.html

models.name: Memory Limitations
aka: Normal limitations of sensation, memory and cognition
models.definition: Memory is reconstructive and small — it does not store, it re-creates, and it edits to fit current beliefs — so eyewitness accounts, recalled reasons, and remembered forecasts are all systematically unreliable. Reach for it by writing decisions and predictions down at the time, in advance.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment, Harvard 1995 — 'normal limitations of sensation, memory, cognition and knowledge'

models.name: Narrative Instinct
models.definition: Humans understand and remember the world as stories with causes, agents, and morals — so a good narrative will beat a true statistic, and any set of random facts will be spun into a coherent tale. Reach for it both as a communication tool and as a warning: if your explanation is a great story, that is not evidence it is right.
models.mungerCitation: Daily Journal Annual Meeting 2018, Q14, https://latticeworkinvesting.com/2018/02/19/charlie-munger-full-transcript-of-daily-journal-annual-meeting-2018/

models.name: Near-Miss Psychology
models.definition: An almost-win triggers the deprival reaction and drives *more* effort than a clean loss — slot machines are engineered to manufacture near misses precisely because they intensify play. Use it to spot systems deliberately built to exploit persistence, and to check whether your own 'so close' feeling is data or manipulation.
models.mungerCitation: Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults (Herb Kay Lecture, UCSB), 2003

models.name: Situational Determinism ('maelstrom of regrettable forces')
models.definition: Actors inside a corrupt system genuinely feel swept along by forces they did not create — a real psychological mechanism and simultaneously the standard excuse. Hold both halves: use it to fix systems rather than only punish individuals, and to notice when you are being offered it as an alibi.
models.mungerCitation: The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight, Poor Charlie's Almanack; written summer 2000)
models.mungerQuote: Maelstrom of regrettable forces.

models.name: Slippery Slope of Small Ethical Compromises
models.definition: Ethical collapse is never one large decision; it is a sequence of small concessions, each justified by the last, until the profession's standards are gone. Reach for it when deciding whether to make the first small exception — that is the only decision you actually get to make.
models.mungerCitation: Poor Charlie's Almanack 3rd ed., Ch. 3 'Mungerisms' (Demise of Ethics Among the Major Accounting Firms)

models.name: Status Quo Bias
aka: Status quo satisfaction and fear of change
models.definition: People are satisfied with the current state and fear change, so defaults win, inertia beats improvement, and 'do nothing' is chosen even when it is plainly dominated. Reach for it when setting defaults (they will stick) and when diagnosing an organization that cannot move despite agreeing it should.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998 (status quo satisfaction and fear of change)

models.name: Truth Opposed by Interest (Samuel Johnson's maxim)
aka: Truth is not merely unknown but hated
models.definition: A vested interest is not merely an obstacle to accepting a truth — it makes the truth genuinely invisible to the person holding it; the ignorance is manufactured and defended. Use it to predict which institutions will never reform themselves and which facts will stay unacknowledged regardless of evidence.
models.mungerCitation: Talk Five, Harvard Law School Class of 1948 50th Reunion, 1998

models.name: Anchoring
models.definition: The first number on the table drags every subsequent estimate toward it, even when it is arbitrary and known to be arbitrary — asking prices, prior valuations, and last year's budget all work this way. Reach for it by forming your own estimate before hearing anyone else's, and by controlling who anchors first in a negotiation.

models.name: Believing First and Doubting Later (Spinozan belief)
models.definition: Gilbert's finding: comprehension and belief are the same act — you must first accept a proposition to understand it, and only then can you (effortfully, and often not) reject it. Distinct from doubt-avoidance; reach for it to explain why exposure to a lie leaves residue even after it is debunked, and why distraction makes people credulous.

models.name: Confirmation Bias
models.definition: We seek, notice, and credit evidence that supports what we already believe, and discount the rest — the default operating mode of the mind, and the reason plausible theories survive contact with contrary data. Reach for it as the standing suspicion whenever your research keeps confirming you.

models.name: Do-Something Tendency (action bias)
models.definition: The opposite failure to status quo bias: people intervene to demonstrate value, discharge anxiety, or justify their role — the goalkeeper who dives because standing still looks passive. Reach for it whenever activity is being mistaken for contribution, especially in investing, management, and medicine.

models.name: Emotional Arousal
models.definition: Strong emotion — fear, excitement, anger, arousal — narrows attention and switches judgment into a different, hotter mode in which prior intentions do not bind ('hot-cold empathy gap'). Reach for it by making rules in the cold state that constrain you in the hot one.

models.name: Law of Small Numbers (overgeneralizing from small samples)
aka: Tendency to overgeneralize from small samples
models.definition: People treat small samples as though they carried the statistical authority of large ones, seeing patterns and 'hot hands' in what is just noise. Reach for it when a manager's three good years, a drug's small trial, or a startup's first cohort is being read as signal.

models.name: Omission and Abstract Blindness
models.definition: We reason from the evidence in front of us and are nearly blind to the evidence that was never generated — the dog that didn't bark, the customers who never complained, the trials never published. Reach for it by explicitly asking 'what data would exist if I were wrong, and would I ever see it?'

models.name: Prospect Theory (Kahneman & Tversky)
models.definition: The formal model behind loss aversion: people evaluate outcomes as gains and losses from a reference point rather than in absolute terms, weigh losses roughly 2–2.5x gains, and turn risk-seeking when facing certain loss. Distinct from Munger's Deprival-Superreaction in that it supplies the reference-point machinery and the value-function curvature.

models.name: Representativeness Heuristic
models.definition: People judge probability by resemblance to a mental prototype rather than by evidence and base rates — the quiet man 'looks like' a librarian, so he is judged one despite there being far more farmers. Reach for it whenever a judgment rests on how much something resembles a category.

models.name: Sunk-Cost Fallacy
models.definition: Past, unrecoverable investment is allowed to justify further investment — the project is continued because of what has already been spent rather than what remains to be gained. Reach for it every time 'we've come too far to stop' appears; only future costs and benefits are decision-relevant.

models.name: 'Walter Mitty Effect' (risk tolerance shifting with conditions)
models.definition: Stated risk tolerance is a fantasy measured in calm markets: people imagine themselves brave, then discover their true tolerance only in the drawdown. Reach for it when anyone — including you — reports how much loss they could stomach.

models.name: Ambiguity Aversion
models.definition: People prefer known risks to unknown ones and will pay a premium to avoid unquantified uncertainty (Ellsberg's urns), even when the ambiguous option is objectively better. Reach for it to explain why merely-unfamiliar assets, markets, and candidates trade at a discount — and where that discount becomes an opportunity.

models.name: Base-Rate Neglect
aka: Failure to account for base rates
models.definition: People discard the prior probability of a class in favor of vivid case-specific detail — the reason a positive test for a rare disease is usually a false positive, and the reason your startup's plan feels different from the 90% that fail. Reach for it by always asking 'what happened to everyone else who tried this?' before assessing your specific case.

models.name: Conjunction Fallacy
models.definition: A detailed, coherent story is judged more probable than a simpler one it logically contains ('Linda is a bank teller and a feminist' beating 'Linda is a bank teller') — plausibility is mistaken for probability. Reach for it whenever a compelling scenario has many specific conditions: each detail added makes it less likely, not more.

models.name: Dual-Process Theory (System 1 / System 2)
aka: System 1 / System 2 thinking (Kahneman; Frederick's Cognitive Reflection Test)
models.definition: Cognition runs on two systems: a fast, automatic, associative one that produces almost all judgments, and a slow, effortful, sequential one that is lazy and mostly ratifies the first. Reach for it as the architecture underneath every bias on this list — the fix is never 'try harder to be rational' but structuring the situation so System 2 actually engages.

models.name: Framing
models.definition: Logically identical choices produce opposite decisions depending on presentation — '90% survival' versus '10% mortality' — because framing sets the reference point that gains and losses are measured against. Reach for it by restating any important decision in at least two frames before choosing.

models.name: Fundamental Attribution Error
aka: Tendency to overestimate consistency of behavior
models.definition: We explain others' behavior by their character and our own by our circumstances, systematically overestimating how consistent and dispositional anyone's conduct really is. Reach for it before concluding someone is lazy, hostile, or brilliant — and before assuming a person who behaved well in one system will behave well in another.

models.name: Hindsight Bias
models.definition: Once you know the outcome, it feels as though it was always predictable, and your memory silently rewrites what you actually believed beforehand — destroying your ability to learn from the decision as opposed to the result. Reach for it by keeping a written decision journal and by judging process, not outcome.

models.name: Hyperbolic Discounting
models.definition: The value of a future reward drops steeply at first and then flattens, so preferences reverse as the moment approaches — you plan to save and then spend, and every quarterly-earnings decision is this in institutional form. Reach for it to explain self-defeating short-termism and to justify precommitment devices.

models.name: Language Instinct
models.definition: Language is innate rather than merely learned, which means the categories your language makes easy shape which thoughts come cheaply — and rhetorical framing therefore has cognitive, not just persuasive, force. Reach for it when word choice appears to be doing argumentative work.

models.name: Mental Accounting
models.definition: People sort money into non-fungible mental buckets — 'house money', the vacation fund, the tax refund — and treat identical dollars differently depending on the label, violating fungibility and producing systematic error. Reach for it when someone takes risks with 'winnings' they would never take with 'savings'.

models.name: Myopic Loss Aversion
models.definition: The more frequently you evaluate a volatile portfolio, the more losses you see and the riskier the same asset feels — evaluation frequency, not the asset, drives perceived risk and premature selling. Reach for it to justify not checking prices, and to explain the equity premium.

models.name: Noise (Fischer Black)
models.definition: Many participants trade on noise as though it were information, which is what makes markets liquid and simultaneously keeps prices only loosely tethered to value. Reach for it to remember that the crowd on the other side of your trade is frequently not informed at all — and to ask whether you are the noise.

models.name: Overreaction Bias
models.definition: De Bondt and Thaler's finding that people overweight recent and dramatic news relative to prior evidence, driving prices too far in both directions and setting up mean reversion. Reach for it when a single data point has moved a consensus a long way.

models.name: Regret Aversion
models.definition: People choose to minimize anticipated regret rather than to maximize expected value, which biases them toward inaction, conventionality, and whatever will be easiest to defend afterward. Reach for it when a decision looks defensively motivated — 'nobody was ever fired for buying IBM' is regret aversion, not analysis.

models.name: Stereotyping
models.definition: The mind compresses groups into a single prototype and then applies it to individuals, gaining speed at the cost of accuracy on any specific case — useful as a base rate, disastrous as a verdict. Reach for it to distinguish legitimate reference-class reasoning from lazy category substitution.

models.name: Bounded Rationality and Satisficing
models.definition: Herbert Simon's model: real decision-makers have limited information, time, and computation, so they do not optimize — they search until they find an option that is good enough and stop. Reach for it to predict actual behavior in organizations, and to recognize when satisficing is the correct policy rather than a failure.

models.name: Bystander Effect (diffusion of responsibility)
models.definition: The more people who could act, the less likely any one of them does — responsibility divides until it vanishes, and everyone reads everyone else's inaction as evidence nothing is wrong. Reach for it by naming a specific owner for anything that must actually happen.

models.name: Cognitive Dissonance
models.definition: Festinger's model: holding a belief and a contradicting action creates discomfort that is resolved by changing the belief, not the action — which is why people come to love what they suffered for and to justify what they cannot undo. Distinct from inconsistency-avoidance in specifying the mechanism: effort and irrevocability generate conviction.

models.name: Dunning-Kruger Effect
models.definition: The skills needed to perform well are the same skills needed to recognize good performance, so the least competent are the least able to perceive their incompetence — and the genuinely expert tend to underrate themselves. Reach for it when confidence and demonstrated ability are inversely correlated in a room.

models.name: Endowment Effect
models.definition: Merely owning something raises your valuation of it — sellers demand roughly twice what buyers will pay for the identical mug — so possession, not utility, is doing the pricing. Distinct from loss aversion in that it applies to static holdings; reach for it when your portfolio, your headcount, or your legacy product looks more valuable than it would if you had to buy it today.

models.name: Halo Effect
models.definition: One salient positive trait — attractiveness, a good quarter, a famous logo — leaks into judgments of every unrelated trait, so a successful company's culture gets praised and the same culture gets condemned after a bad year. Reach for it whenever ratings of independent attributes turn out to be highly correlated.

models.name: Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic Motivation (overjustification effect)
models.definition: Paying people for something they already enjoy converts play into work and destroys the intrinsic drive, which does not return when the payment stops. The critical counterweight to reward-superresponse: incentives are a superpower, but applying them to intrinsically motivated behavior actively subtracts.

models.name: Learned Helplessness
models.definition: Organisms subjected to uncontrollable outcomes stop trying, and keep not trying after control is restored — the failure generalizes into a belief about the self rather than the situation. Reach for it to explain apathy in badly-run organizations and why removing the cause is not enough to restore initiative.

models.name: Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs
models.definition: Human motives are roughly ordered — physiological, safety, belonging, esteem, self-actualization — with lower unmet needs dominating attention until satisfied. Empirically loose but useful as a first pass on why an appeal to purpose fails on someone worried about rent, or why status motives dominate once security is solved.

models.name: Peak-End Rule
models.definition: People judge an experience almost entirely by its most intense moment and its ending, nearly ignoring duration — so a longer, mostly-worse experience with a gentle ending is remembered as better. Reach for it when designing anything people will remember rather than merely undergo: negotiations, onboarding, service recovery, layoffs.

models.name: Planning Fallacy
models.definition: Plans are built from a best-case inside view of this specific project and therefore run systematically late and over budget, even by planners who know their last project ran late. Reach for it by replacing the inside view with the reference class: what did comparable projects actually cost and take?

models.name: Theory of Mind
models.definition: The capacity to model other people as having beliefs, intentions, and knowledge different from your own — and the systematic failure to do so, producing the curse of knowledge and most communication breakdowns. Reach for it when explaining something to someone who lacks your context: you cannot un-know what you know, so you must model their state explicitly.

models.name: Five W's Rule in Communication
models.definition: Every instruction should state who is to do what, where, when, and above all why — because people comply far better and adapt far more intelligently when they understand the reason behind an order. Reach for it when writing any directive: omitting the why converts a thinking subordinate into a literal one.
models.mungerCitation: The Psychology of Human Misjudgment (Philanthropy Round Table, 2005 revision), Reason-Respecting Tendency

models.name: Seamless Web of Deserved Trust
aka: Deserved trust as an operating system
Management already in place with integrity (Right Stuff in a Business 4)
Management with Integrity (Seamless Web of Deserved Trust)
models.definition: An organization can substitute earned trust among a small number of reliable people for elaborate rules, controls, and process — and where trust is genuinely deserved, that web outperforms any procedural system on both speed and integrity. Reach for it when deciding whether to solve a failure by adding a control or by changing who is in the room; the model says the second is often cheaper and the first often corrosive.
models.mungerCitation: USC Law School commencement, 2007
models.mungerQuote: a seamless web of deserved trust

models.name: Management Incentives & Compensation Design
aka: Compensation systems that create alignment with shareholders (Right Stuff in a Business 2)
models.definition: Compensation schemes are the sharpest lever on organizational behavior and the one most reliably mis-set, because the people designing the scheme are usually its beneficiaries or their social peers. Use it whenever you want to predict what an organization will actually do: read the pay plan, not the mission statement.
models.mungerCitation: Extensive on-record commentary on compensation-committee incentives; an application of his incentives super-power rather than a separately named model
models.mungerQuote: never think about something else when you should be thinking about the power of incentives

models.name: Bureaucratic Inertia & Empire Building
models.definition: As organizations scale, internal actors optimize for headcount, budget, and territory rather than the enterprise's purpose, and layers accumulate that resist any change threatening them. Reach for it when a large organization cannot do an obviously correct thing — the obstacle is usually someone's protected fiefdom, not disagreement about the facts.
models.mungerCitation: Largely duplicates the disadvantages-of-scale and bureaucracy discussion in the USC Business School talk, 1994; the organizational relabeling is the compiler's

models.name: Corporate Governance
models.definition: The structure of boards, owners, auditors, and managers determines whose interests actually get served, and formal governance frequently fails because directors are socially captured by the CEO who selected them. Reach for it when the stated safeguard (a board, a committee, an independent reviewer) is being cited as a reason to trust an outcome — ask who chose the safeguard and what it costs them to object.
models.mungerCitation: Extensive on-record commentary at Berkshire and Wesco meetings and in board-behavior remarks within The Psychology of Human Misjudgment; a topic he addressed rather than a model he named

models.name: Culture as a Control System
models.definition: Shared norms and expectations regulate behavior more cheaply and more pervasively than written policy, because they operate where no supervisor is watching and cover cases no rule anticipated. Use it when diagnosing why compliant organizations still misbehave — the formal system was never the real control system.
models.mungerCitation: Substance in the 'seamless web of deserved trust' remarks (USC Law, 2007) and trust-over-process commentary; the label is the compiler's

models.name: Information Suppression / Shooting the Messenger
models.definition: When bad news is punished, subordinates stop delivering it, and the leader ends up sealed inside a filtered picture of reality that grows more flattering as the situation gets worse. Use it as a design constraint: the only reliable fix is to make bad news travel fast and cost the messenger nothing.
models.mungerCitation: Substance in the USC Business School talk, 1994 (William Paley's 'cocoon of unreality' at CBS) and in Philanthropy Round Table remarks on wanting bad news early; the label is the compiler's
models.mungerQuote: cocoon of unreality

models.name: Talent, Trust, and Delegation
models.definition: Concentrate authority in a small number of exceptionally able people and then genuinely leave them alone, rather than diffusing decisions across a supervisory apparatus. Reach for it when choosing between hiring better people and building more oversight — this model says the first dominates, but only if you actually delegate afterward.
models.mungerCitation: Substance in the 'seamless web of deserved trust' remarks (USC Law, 2007) and Berkshire's few-great-people delegation philosophy; the bundled label is the compiler's

models.name: Information Cascades
models.definition: Once enough people act on a signal, later actors rationally ignore their own private information and imitate the observed behavior, so a chain of individually sensible decisions locks in an outcome the underlying evidence never supported. Use it to explain why crowds flip suddenly and why early movers exert wildly disproportionate influence over final consensus.
models.mungerCitation: The formal cascade model is the compiler's addition; Munger's named 'Social-Proof Tendency' (Harvard, 1995) covers the same behavioral terrain

models.name: Parkinson's Law
models.definition: Work expands to fill the time available for its completion, and bureaucracies grow at a rate largely independent of the work to be done, because officials multiply subordinates rather than rivals. Reach for it when setting deadlines or reading a headcount plan — absent a binding constraint, both slack and staff will be consumed.

models.name: Self-Organization
models.definition: Order can arise spontaneously from local interactions and feedback without any designer, plan, or central authority. Reach for it before assuming that structure implies intent, and when deciding whether to impose coordination or to set conditions and let it form.

models.name: Self-Organized Criticality
models.definition: Some systems naturally evolve toward a critical state in which the size distribution of disturbances follows a power law, so an identical small trigger may cause nothing or an avalanche — and the trigger's size tells you nothing about the outcome's size. Use it when people demand a proportionate cause for a large event: in a critical system, the cause and the crisis were never going to match.

models.name: Wisdom of Crowds
aka: Collective accuracy under diversity and independence
models.definition: An aggregate of independent, diverse, decentralized judgments can be more accurate than almost any individual within it — but only while those conditions hold; remove independence or diversity and the crowd collapses into a mob. Reach for it when designing estimation or forecasting processes: the value is in the aggregation, so protect it by collecting views before anyone hears anyone else's.

models.name: Bureaucracy (Weber)
models.definition: Hierarchy, written rules, fixed jurisdictions, and impersonal application of standards make large-scale coordination possible at all — bureaucracy is a technology for reliability, not merely a pathology. Use it to see the tradeoff honestly: the same features that make an organization predictable and fair make it slow and blind to cases the rules did not anticipate.

models.name: Conway's Law
models.definition: Organizations produce designs that mirror their own communication structures — the architecture of the artifact ends up matching the org chart of the people who built it. Reach for it when a system's seams keep appearing in the wrong places: you cannot get a design the organization is not shaped to produce, so change the teams or accept the architecture.

models.name: Diffusion of Innovations
models.definition: New ideas spread through a population in a predictable sequence — innovators, early adopters, early and late majority, laggards — with adoption driven by observability, trialability, relative advantage, compatibility, and simplicity rather than by merit alone. Reach for it when a demonstrably better thing is not being adopted: the model tells you which of those five attributes to fix.

models.name: Dunbar's Number
models.definition: Cognitive limits cap the number of stable social relationships a person can maintain at roughly 150, so groups beyond that size require formal structure, rules, and hierarchy to hold together where reputation alone previously sufficed. Use it to predict when a growing organization must change kind rather than just degree — the informal coordination that worked at 50 will not survive at 300.

models.name: Groupthink
models.definition: Cohesive groups under pressure suppress dissent to preserve unanimity, producing confident decisions with no adversarial testing behind them and a shared illusion that everyone agrees. Use it whenever a consequential decision is reached quickly and pleasantly — assign a genuine devil's advocate, or collect views privately before the meeting.

models.name: Institutional Imperative
models.definition: Institutions resist changes of direction, absorb available funds into whatever projects are proposed, and mindlessly imitate the behavior of peer institutions regardless of whether it makes sense. Reach for it when a company explains a decision by pointing to what competitors are doing — that is the imperative talking, not analysis.

models.name: Iron Law of Oligarchy
models.definition: Every organization, however democratic in intent, tends to develop a self-perpetuating leadership class, because coordination requires delegation and delegates accumulate information, relationships, and incentives to stay. Use it to temper expectations of any governance reform that relies on the governed policing the governors.

models.name: Norms and Taboos
models.definition: Unwritten rules govern behavior more powerfully than written ones, and taboos in particular mark the questions a group will not permit itself to ask — the violation is punished socially, not formally. Use it to locate an organization's real constraints: find what nobody says out loud, and you have found the boundary of what it can think.

models.name: Peter Principle
models.definition: In a hierarchy that promotes on current performance, people rise until they reach a role they are not competent in — and there they stay, because the promotion criterion measured the previous job, not the next one. Use it when designing promotion tracks: competence at level N is weak evidence about level N+1, and the model argues for separate ladders rather than treating management as a reward.

models.name: Social Capital
models.definition: Networks of relationships, norms of reciprocity, and trust are productive assets: they lower the cost of transacting, enforce agreements no contract could, and accumulate or decay like any other capital stock. Reach for it when explaining why two groups with identical resources perform very differently, or why an organization survives a shock that should have broken it.

models.name: Tribalism / In-Group Out-Group Dynamics
models.definition: People sort into groups on remarkably thin pretexts and immediately favor insiders, distrust outsiders, and reason in service of the tribe rather than the truth — belonging beats accuracy. Use it when a factual disagreement will not resolve under evidence: the dispute is probably about membership, and evidence is the wrong instrument.

models.name: Complex Adaptive Systems
aka: Dispersed interaction / no global controller / continual adaptation / out-of-equilibrium dynamics
models.definition: Systems whose agents learn and change strategy in response to each other, so the system's rules shift as you study or intervene in it — markets, ecosystems, cultures. Distinguishes 'complex' from merely 'complicated': a jet engine is complicated but stationary; an adaptive system fights back, so solutions decay.
models.mungerCitation: Santa Fe Institute strand of the genre (via Hagstrom), not Munger

models.name: Feedback Loops
aka: Positive feedback loops (spending and stock prices driving each other)
Self-reinforcing feedback loops / cascades (in prices and in public passion)
models.definition: System outputs loop back as inputs: balancing (negative) loops resist change and stabilize toward a goal, while reinforcing (positive) loops amplify deviation and produce exponential runaway or collapse. Reach for it whenever behavior over time looks stubbornly self-correcting or inexplicably explosive — the loop structure, not the event, is doing the work.
models.mungerCitation: Breakfast Meeting of the Philanthropy Roundtable, 2000; The Great Financial Scandal of 2003 (Talk Eight, written summer 2000); supporting usage at Harvard 1995 (open-outcry auction, Tupperware party)

models.name: Systems Thinking (Consequences of Consequences)
models.definition: Interventions in a system propagate through interactions and produce second-, third-, and higher-order effects that frequently swamp or reverse the intended first-order effect. Munger's discipline of always asking 'and then what?' — reach for it whenever a proposed fix is evaluated only on its immediate, visible result.
models.mungerCitation: UCSB 2003, 'Academic Economics: Strengths and Faults After Considering Interdisciplinary Needs'

models.name: Bottlenecks (Theory of Constraints)
models.definition: Throughput of any chain is set entirely by its single tightest constraint, so improvements anywhere else produce no gain and often just pile up inventory in front of the real limit. Reach for it before optimizing anything: find the constraint first, because effort spent off the bottleneck is definitionally wasted.

models.name: Churn
models.definition: Every stock of customers, employees, or members leaks at some rate, so a system must replace losses before any growth registers — high gross additions can mask a shrinking base. Reach for it when a growth number is quoted without its attrition counterpart.

models.name: Emergence
aka: the whole is greater than the sum of its parts
models.definition: Higher levels of a system exhibit properties that none of the components possess and that cannot be read off from them — the behavior arises from interactions, not parts. Warns against the reductionist move of studying pieces in isolation and expecting the whole to follow.

models.name: Irreducibility
models.definition: Some things have a floor that no amount of added resources can push below — a minimum time, complexity, or number of steps that cannot be parallelized away. Nine women cannot make a baby in one month; use it to resist the assumption that throwing more people or money at a task compresses it proportionally.

models.name: Antifragility
models.definition: Beyond merely resisting shocks, some systems actually improve from volatility, stressors, and disorder up to a point — muscles, immune systems, evolutionary populations. Distinguish three regimes rather than two: fragile (harmed by variance), robust (indifferent), antifragile (helped), and ask which one you are building.

models.name: Cascading Failure
models.definition: In tightly coupled systems, one component's failure shifts its load onto neighbors, overloading them in turn, so a local fault propagates into system-wide collapse far larger than the initial fault. Reach for it when assessing blackouts, bank runs, and dependency chains — the danger lives in the coupling, not in any single node's reliability.

models.name: Chaos and Sensitivity to Initial Conditions
models.definition: In some deterministic systems, arbitrarily small differences in starting state diverge exponentially, making long-range prediction impossible even with perfect knowledge of the rules. The lesson is epistemic humility about forecast horizons: the failure is structural, not a matter of needing a better model or more data.

models.name: Law of Requisite Variety
models.definition: Only variety can absorb variety: a controller must have at least as many distinct responses as the system it regulates has distinct states, or it will inevitably lose control in the uncovered cases. Ashby's law is the formal argument for why a simple rulebook cannot govern a rich environment — and, incidentally, for why a latticework beats one model.

models.name: Leverage Points
aka: places to intervene in a system
models.definition: Places in a system where a small, well-placed intervention produces disproportionate change — and they are ordered, with parameters and numbers weakest and goals, rules, and paradigms strongest. Reach for it when effort feels wasted: most people push hardest on the lowest-leverage knobs precisely because those are the visible ones.

models.name: Network Topology
models.definition: The shape of a network — hubs, clusters, degree distribution, path lengths — determines how fast things spread through it and how it fails, largely independent of the nodes' own properties. Scale-free networks are robust to random node loss but acutely vulnerable to targeted hub removal; ask about structure before asking about components.

models.name: Path Dependence
models.definition: The set of outcomes available now is constrained by the sequence of steps that got here, so early accidents get locked in and persist long after the conditions that produced them are gone. Explains why demonstrably inferior standards, codebases, and institutions survive — and warns that reversible-looking early choices often aren't.

models.name: Queueing and Utilization
models.definition: Wait times do not rise linearly with load — as utilization approaches 100%, queues explode toward infinity, and variability in arrivals or service times makes it worse. Distinct from bottlenecks (which set throughput): this sets delay, and it is why running any system 'fully utilized' guarantees it feels broken.

models.name: Resilience vs. Efficiency
models.definition: Optimizing a system toward maximum efficiency strips out the slack, buffers, and duplication that let it absorb shocks — the two properties trade directly against each other. Reach for it whenever an efficiency program is proposed: ask what shock the removed slack was silently absorbing.

models.name: Stock and Flow
models.definition: Stocks are accumulations (water in a tub, debt, trust, talent); flows are the rates filling and draining them. Confusing the two is one of the most common reasoning errors in public life — a falling rate of increase still raises the stock, and stocks are what actually cause behavior.

models.name: Time Delays and Lags
models.definition: When feedback arrives long after the action that caused it, controllers overcorrect, and the delay itself generates oscillation, boom-bust cycles, and blame misattributed to the wrong cause. Reach for it whenever a system swings wildly despite competent operators — the lag, not the operator, may be producing the instability.

models.name: The Man in the Arena
models.definition: The critic and the actor are not epistemic equals: the critic bears no cost for being wrong, while the actor pays for every error in public. Reach for it when weighting feedback — discount commentary from anyone who has no exposure to the outcome, and when deciding whether to act, price in that failure while trying ranks above the safety of never having tried.

models.name: The Strenuous Life
models.definition: Capacity is built by voluntary difficulty and atrophies under ease; comfort is not a neutral resting state but a slow subtraction. Use it when choosing between an easy path and a hard one of similar payoff — the hardship itself is part of the return.

models.name: Speak Softly and Carry a Big Stick
models.definition: Real capability lets you lower your voice; loud rhetoric is usually a substitute for the thing itself, and threats you must shout are threats you cannot back. Reach for it in any negotiation: build the reserve first, then let restraint — not volume — do the signaling.

models.name: Good Trusts vs. Bad Trusts
models.definition: Concentration of power is not itself the offense — behavior is; the test is whether scale is used to serve or to extract. Use it when tempted to regulate, break up, or condemn something by its magnitude alone: ask what it does, not how big it is.

models.name: Conservation as Intergenerational Stewardship
models.definition: Treat a shared resource as capital rather than income: consume only the yield, never the stock, because parties who cannot yet bargain — the unborn — hold a real claim on it. Reach for it whenever a decision's benefits land now and its depletion lands on someone with no seat at the table.

models.name: The Bully Pulpit
models.definition: A position confers an audience, and that audience is a separate lever from any formal authority the position grants — you can set the agenda without the power to compel. Use it when your official levers are weak: attention itself moves outcomes.

models.name: Do What You Can, With What You Have
models.definition: Begin from your actual position and inventory rather than waiting for the resources or conditions the ideal plan requires — the wait usually costs more than the shortfall. Reach for it against planning paralysis, when the gap between what you have and what you 'need' is being used as a reason not to start.

models.name: The Square Deal
models.definition: Legitimacy comes from an impartial referee guaranteeing every party fair terms of play, not from engineering who wins; the arbiter must be beholden to none of the contestants. Use it when designing rules between unequal parties — fix the process and let outcomes fall where they may.

models.name: The View From Above
models.definition: Deliberately imagine your situation from progressively higher altitude — the room, the city, the continent, the span of history — until its true proportion appears. Reach for it when a setback or a slight feels total and you need to restore scale before deciding.

models.name: Memento Mori
models.definition: Hold your finitude in view as a live constraint rather than an abstraction; mortality is the scarce resource that prices everything else. Use it to expose which commitments, grudges, and ambitions survive contact with a fixed and unknown deadline.

models.name: The Obstacle Is the Way
aka: The Impediment to Action Advances Action
models.definition: What blocks a given course of action becomes the material for a different and often better one — the impediment is not an interruption of the work but the current form of it. Distinct from merely enduring hardship: it asks what the obstacle makes possible that the open road did not.

models.name: Premeditatio Malorum
models.definition: Rehearse the day's likely frustrations and losses in advance — the ingrates you'll meet, the plans that will fail — so their arrival is recognition rather than shock. Aimed at emotional inoculation and lowered expectations, not at project risk analysis.

models.name: The Discipline of Assent
models.definition: An impression arrives unbidden, but the verdict you staple onto it is a separate, voluntary act you can withhold. Reach for it in the gap between provocation and reaction: drop the added judgment — 'I have been wronged' — and the harm often goes with it.

models.name: Objective Description
models.definition: Describe a thing in its bare physical particulars, stripped of the story, status, and honorifics that have accreted onto it — the feast is dead animals, the purple robe is wool dyed with shellfish. Use it to deflate anything whose grip on you comes from its packaging rather than its substance.

models.name: Sympatheia
aka: What Injures the Hive Injures the Bee
models.definition: You are a part of a whole, and your good is not separable from the whole's — damage done to the system you belong to routes back to you. Use it when a locally rational move quietly degrades the commons you depend on.

models.name: Judgements, Not Events, Disturb Us
aka: It's Not the Thing, It's Your Opinion of It
models.definition: Distress arrives not from what happened but from the verdict you silently passed on what happened — the event and the suffering are separated by an interpretation you authored. Reach for it when you feel a situation is doing something to you, and want to find the editable clause between the fact and the pain.

models.name: Role Ethics (Play Your Part Well)
models.definition: You are an actor in a play whose length and part are chosen by someone else; your only job is to play whatever role you're handed well, since the casting was never yours to negotiate. Use it when you're spending energy resenting your position instead of excelling within it.

models.name: Every Mischance Is an Opportunity
aka: What Resource Do I Have for This?
models.definition: On every setback, ask not 'why me' but 'what capacity do I have that this specific difficulty calls for' — rudeness summons patience, loss summons detachment, each trouble arriving matched to a strength it exists to exercise. Reach for it the instant something goes wrong, before the complaint forms.

models.name: Discipline of Assent
aka: Wait, Impression — Let Me See What You Are
models.definition: Impressions arrive uninvited, but agreement is a separate act you perform — so insert a pause between the appearance and your endorsement of it, and interrogate it before signing. Use it whenever a first reading of a situation arrives pre-loaded with an emotion you're about to act on.

models.name: Everything Has Two Handles
models.definition: Every situation can be grasped by a handle that bears weight and one that doesn't — a brother's wrong can be held by 'he wronged me' (which won't carry) or 'he is my brother' (which will). Use it when your framing of a problem is true but leaves you unable to move.

models.name: Embody, Don't Expound
models.definition: Sheep don't bring up grass to show the shepherd how much they ate — they digest it and produce wool; likewise, don't recite your principles, just let people encounter the results. Reach for it when tempted to announce a framework, philosophy, or diet rather than simply operate by it.

models.name: The School Is a Hospital, Not a Lecture Hall
models.definition: You enter a school of thought injured, so a good session should hurt rather than please — and the test of learning is changed conduct under pressure, not doctrine retained or admiration for the teacher. Use it to audit any training, book, or course you enjoyed but that altered nothing.

models.name: Nothing Is Lost, Only Returned
models.definition: Never say 'I lost it' but 'I gave it back' — everything you hold (property, position, people) was lent on terms you never set, and its recall is the lender's business, not a theft. Reach for it in grief and in reversals, to separate the fact of an ending from the sense of having been robbed.

models.name: The Impartial Spectator
models.definition: Smith's account of conscience: we judge our own conduct by imagining how a well-informed, disinterested observer would view it. Reach for it when self-interest is loud and you need a check that is neither your own flattery nor the crowd's applause.

models.name: The Man of System
models.definition: The reformer 'so enamoured with the supposed beauty of his own ideal plan' arranges people like chess pieces, forgetting that every piece has a principle of motion of its own. Reach for it whenever a design assumes people will do what the org chart, policy, or model says.

models.name: The Extent of the Market Limits Specialization
aka: Division of Labour Is Limited by the Extent of the Market
models.definition: Specialization can only go as deep as the market is wide — a village supports a general store, a city supports a shop that sells only buttons. Reach for it when deciding how narrow to niche a role, product, or career, or when forecasting what a larger market will unbundle next.

models.name: Sympathy as Imaginative Projection
models.definition: We do not feel others' feelings directly; we imagine ourselves in their situation and approve of their reactions insofar as they match what we would feel there. Reach for it to explain why understanding fades with distance, why we misjudge people whose circumstances we cannot picture, and why proximity drives compassion more than magnitude does.

models.name: Self-Love, Not Benevolence
aka: The Butcher, the Brewer, and the Baker
models.definition: Durable cooperation comes from addressing what the other party wants, not from appealing to their generosity: we get dinner not from the butcher's benevolence but from his regard to his own interest. Reach for it when a plan depends on someone being good rather than being served.

models.name: The Real Price Is Toil and Trouble
aka: Labour as the Real Measure of Value
models.definition: The true cost of anything is the labour and life it takes to acquire it — money price is only a nominal shadow of that. Reach for it to convert purchases, obligations, and projects into hours of your finite life and see what they actually cost.

models.name: Diamond–Water Paradox
aka: Value in Use vs Value in Exchange
models.definition: Water is essential and nearly free; diamonds are useless and dear — so usefulness does not set price. Reach for it whenever someone argues that something is valuable because it is important, or confuses what a thing is worth with what it fetches.

models.name: Consumption Is the Sole End of Production
models.definition: The purpose of any productive system is the consumer's benefit, yet producers organize, lobby, and capture the rules while consumers stay diffuse and silent. Reach for it when evaluating a policy, tariff, regulation, or internal process to ask who it actually serves.

models.name: Ricardo's Law of Rent
models.definition: Rent on any resource equals the surplus its productivity yields over the least productive unit still in use — the marginal one, which earns nothing. Reach for it whenever a scarce fixed input (land, spectrum, a prime shelf slot, a senior engineer) quietly captures the profits a business thinks it is earning.

models.name: Rent Follows Price, Not the Reverse
models.definition: Ricardo's causal reversal: corn is not expensive because rent is paid; rent is paid because corn is expensive. Price is set at the margin by the highest-cost producer needed to meet demand, and any surplus is then capitalized into the scarce input. Use it to check which way causation actually runs when someone blames a cost for a price.

models.name: Ricardian Equivalence
models.definition: Financing spending by borrowing rather than taxing changes little if people foresee the future taxes and save against them — debt is deferred taxation. Reach for it to see through any financing scheme that shifts the timing of a burden while pretending to remove it.

models.name: Iron Law of Wages
models.definition: Wages tend back toward subsistence because a wage above it expands the supply of labor until the gain is competed away. The general model: any gain available to a freely expanding population of entrants gets arbitraged back to the survival minimum — useful for spotting why an obviously lucrative niche stops being lucrative.

models.name: Labor Theory of Value (Embodied Cost)
models.definition: Ricardo held that the relative prices of reproducible goods track the relative quantity of labor required to produce them, scarcity aside. Wrong as a general theory of value, it remains a sharp model of the long-run floor: anything freely reproducible drifts toward its cost of production, and only what cannot be reproduced escapes.

models.name: The Machinery Question
models.definition: Ricardo publicly reversed himself to conclude that substituting machines for labor can genuinely injure workers, not merely relocate them — output and aggregate wealth can rise while the demand for labor falls. Reach for it when a productivity gain is defended by asserting that displaced people must land somewhere.

models.name: Ricardian Vice
models.definition: Schumpeter's name for Ricardo's habit: build a drastically simplified model, freeze everything inconvenient as a given, then pile concrete policy conclusions on its narrow shoulders. Use it as an alarm whenever a clean abstraction is carrying more real-world weight than its assumptions can bear.

models.name: Man muss immer verallgemeinern (Always Generalize)
models.definition: Jacobi's companion maxim to "invert, always invert": the broader problem is often easier than the stubborn special case, because generality strips away accidental detail and exposes the structure actually doing the work. Reach for it when a specific instance won't yield — ask what class it belongs to and solve that instead.

models.name: The Jacobian (Local Linearization)
models.definition: Any smooth transformation, viewed closely enough, is a linear one; the Jacobian is the table of local exchange rates between every input and every output. Reach for it when a nonlinear system defies global solution — the sensitivities around your current operating point carry most of the decision-relevant information.

models.name: Change of Variables (The Distortion Factor)
models.definition: Re-express a problem in new coordinates and quantities do not carry over untouched — you must multiply by how much the map stretches or compresses the space. Reach for it whenever comparing figures measured in different frames, units, or transformed scales: the conversion is never free, and forgetting the factor silently corrupts the answer.

models.name: Hamilton–Jacobi: Transform Until It's Trivial
models.definition: Rather than grinding through a system's equations, search for the change of variables in which its dynamics collapse into constants — the labor shifts from solving the problem to choosing the frame you solve it in. Reach for it when brute force is grinding: the right representation can do the work that effort cannot.

models.name: Iterative Relaxation (Jacobi Method)
models.definition: Instead of solving a coupled system exactly, guess, attack the largest remaining error, and repeat until the residual stops mattering. Reach for it when the system is too large or tangled for a closed form — reliable convergence beats an exact answer you cannot reach.

models.name: Inversion Reveals Hidden Symmetry
models.definition: When an object resists analysis, its inverse may be the well-behaved one: Jacobi inverted the intractable elliptic integral and found a function with a double periodicity wholly invisible in the original. Distinct from inverting a goal — this says the structure of a hard thing may live in the reverse direction, so flip the formulation before abandoning the problem.

models.name: The Honor of the Human Spirit
models.definition: Jacobi's answer to Fourier's demand that science serve public utility: inquiry answers to the honor of the human spirit, and work chosen for its depth rather than its application tends to yield the applications nobody could have commissioned. Reach for it when allocating effort under pressure to justify everything by immediate payoff.

models.name: Prior Probability (The Prior)
models.definition: Every judgment starts from a belief held before the new evidence arrives, and that starting point does as much work as the evidence itself. Reach for it whenever a striking new fact tempts you to reason as if you knew nothing beforehand.

models.name: Likelihood Ratio (Bayes Factor)
models.definition: Evidence is worth only the ratio of how expected it is under one hypothesis versus its rival; a fact consistent with everything moves nothing. Use it to price a piece of evidence before letting it move you.

models.name: Today's Posterior Is Tomorrow's Prior
models.definition: A conclusion is never final — it is the input to the next round of evidence, so belief accumulates rather than resets. Reach for it when you are tempted to treat a current view as a verdict instead of a running balance.

models.name: Confusion of the Inverse
models.definition: The chance of the evidence given the hypothesis is not the chance of the hypothesis given the evidence, yet the two are routinely swapped. Reach for it whenever someone argues from how unlikely a clue is to how certain a cause must be.

models.name: Probability as Degree of Belief
models.definition: A probability can describe how confident you are about a one-off claim, not just the long-run frequency of a repeatable trial. Use it when the question happens only once and frequencies do not exist to count.

models.name: Cromwell's Rule (Never Zero, Never One)
models.definition: Assigning a probability of exactly 0 or 1 makes a belief mathematically immune to all future evidence, however overwhelming. Reach for it when certainty is doing the arguing.

models.name: False Positives in Rare-Event Testing
