models.definition: When the thing you are screening for is rare, even an accurate test produces mostly false alarms, because the many healthy cases outnumber the few real ones. Use it before acting on any positive result — medical, security, or fraud detection.

models.name: Universal Gravitation (One Law for Heaven and Earth)
models.definition: Newton showed the force pulling an apple to the ground is the same force holding the moon in its orbit — the celestial and the mundane obey one rule, not two. Reach for it whenever a field is split into 'special' cases and 'ordinary' cases, and ask whether a single law already covers both.

models.name: Hypotheses Non Fingo (I Frame No Hypotheses)
models.definition: Newton gave gravity's law to extraordinary precision while flatly refusing to speculate about what causes it — a measured regularity is real knowledge even with no mechanism attached. Use it when the demand for a story outruns the evidence: state what you can show, and leave the rest explicitly unknown.

models.name: The Calculus of Change (Rates and Accumulation)
models.definition: Newton's calculus treats every quantity as having an instantaneous rate of change, with accumulation as the exact inverse of that rate. Reach for it when the level looks fine but the derivative — which way and how fast it is moving — is the actual story.

models.name: Newton's Method (Iterative Approximation)
models.definition: Start from a guess, use the local slope to project where the true answer lies, jump there, and repeat — near a good starting point each step roughly squares your accuracy. It is the model for converging fast on a hard answer, and a warning that a bad first guess can throw you further away than you began.

models.name: The Three-Body Problem
models.definition: Two bodies under gravity have an exact closed-form solution; add a third and no general solution exists — the system turns analytically intractable and long-run unpredictable. Use it as a warning that adding one more interacting party can shove a solved problem permanently past the edge of solvability.

models.name: Same Effects, Same Causes (Newton's Rules of Reasoning)
models.definition: Newton's rule of reasoning: assign the same causes to the same natural effects wherever you can — respiration in a man and in a beast, a stone falling in Europe and in America. Reach for it to resist inventing a bespoke explanation for something that already has a working one somewhere else.

models.name: Experimentum Crucis (The Decisive Experiment)
models.definition: Instead of piling up evidence consistent with your theory, design the one experiment whose rival explanations predict different outcomes — Newton's prism test forced a verdict between competing theories of color. Reach for it when a dispute is producing arguments instead of a test.

models.name: Galileo's Ship (Galilean Relativity)
models.definition: Below decks on a smoothly sailing ship, no experiment reveals that you are moving — uniform motion is undetectable from inside the system, and only relative motion is observable. Reach for it when you are trying to judge a trend you are embedded in, or when someone insists there is no movement because nothing around them appears to change.

models.name: Refutation from Within
models.definition: Galileo destroyed Aristotle's claim that heavier bodies fall faster without dropping anything: tie a heavy and a light body together, and the theory predicts the pair falls both faster (greater weight) and slower (the light one drags). A theory that contradicts itself needs no data to die — when attacking a position, first check whether its own premises already collide.

ref.title: Galileo's Leaning Tower of Pisa experiment

models.name: Dilute the Phenomenon (Inclined Plane)
models.definition: Free fall was too fast for Galileo to time, so he rolled balls down a gentle ramp — the same law of acceleration, slowed until his instruments could catch it. When a process is too fast, too rare, or too large to observe, don't abandon it: find a weakened version that obeys the same law and measure that instead.

models.name: Idealization (Reasoning in a Vacuum)
models.definition: Galileo found the laws of motion by reasoning about a vacuum and frictionless planes — conditions no one had ever seen — treating air resistance and friction as accidents concealing the underlying rule. Strip the mess away to find the law, then add the mess back as a correction; the error is mistaking the stripped-down model for the world rather than for the skeleton under it.

models.name: Instruments Precede Discovery
models.definition: Galileo saw Jupiter's moons not by thinking harder than his contemporaries but by grinding better lenses — the new facts arrived with the new instrument. When a field is stuck, ask what it cannot currently measure rather than what it has not yet argued, and expect the next round of discoveries to belong to whoever improves the apparatus.

models.name: Refusing to Look Through the Telescope
models.definition: Galileo's opponents largely did not dispute what the telescope showed — several simply declined to look through it. When evidence threatens a commitment, the common defense is not counter-argument but avoiding exposure to the evidence at all; watch for it in yourself as unopened reports, undone analyses, and questions you keep not asking.

models.name: Primary vs. Secondary Qualities
models.definition: Galileo argued that shape, number, and motion belong to the object itself, while color, taste, smell, and heat exist only in the creature perceiving it — remove the sensing body and they vanish. Use it to sort which parts of a description are properties of the thing and which are artifacts of the observer's apparatus, appetites, or vantage point.

models.name: Second Incompleteness: No Self-Certification
aka: No system proves its own consistency
models.definition: Gödel proved that any consistent formal system strong enough to describe arithmetic cannot prove its own consistency from within — a system that certifies itself is thereby suspect, not reassured. Reach for it whenever a body claims to have audited itself: the assurance must come from a vantage point outside the system, and that vantage point then needs its own.

models.name: Gödel Numbering (Arithmetization of Syntax)
models.definition: Gödel assigned every symbol, formula, and proof a unique number, so that statements about the system became statements within it — the trick that made incompleteness constructible and that prefigured stored-program computing. Use it when you want a system to reason about itself: find an encoding that turns the rules into data the rules can operate on.

models.name: The Diagonal Lemma: Forced Self-Reference
models.definition: Gödel showed that any system expressive enough to encode itself necessarily contains sentences that assert things about themselves — self-reference is not a defect you can design out, it comes free with sufficient expressive power. Reach for it when someone proposes to ban paradox by fiat: if the language can describe the language, the fixed points already exist.

models.name: Gödel's Completeness Theorem
models.definition: Gödel's first great result (1929) is the positive counterpart to incompleteness: in first-order logic, every statement true in all models is provable, so syntax fully captures semantics. Use it as the boundary marker — within a modest enough scope, formal derivation misses nothing; the gaps only open once the system gets rich enough to describe itself.

models.name: Expressiveness Buys Incompleteness
models.definition: Weak systems are complete and decidable; the moment a system becomes powerful enough to model itself, it loses that guarantee — a tradeoff, not an accident. Reach for it when designing languages, rules, or contracts: every increment of expressive power you add buys capability at the price of questions the system can no longer settle about itself.

models.name: Independence: Some Questions Are Choices
models.definition: Gödel proved the Axiom of Choice and the Continuum Hypothesis consistent with set theory — neither refutable from the axioms — so certain questions have no answer waiting to be discovered; both answers are livable, and adopting one is a decision. Use it when a debate resists all evidence: check whether it is a fact to find or an axiom to pick.

models.name: Gödel Speed-Up
aka: Climb a level to shorten the proof
models.definition: Gödel showed that adding a stronger axiom can collapse a proof from astronomically long to a few lines — the same conclusion, reachable cheaply only from a higher level. Reach for it when a derivation, argument, or search is grinding: the fix is often not more effort at the current level but a stronger assumption one level up.

models.name: Nyquist–Shannon Sampling Theorem
models.definition: A continuous signal can be reconstructed perfectly from discrete samples only if you sample at least twice as fast as its highest frequency; sample slower and fast variation aliases into false slow patterns you cannot detect after the fact. Reach for it whenever you set a measurement or review cadence: the interval you choose silently determines what you are capable of seeing.

models.name: Information Is Not Meaning
aka: Semantics Are Irrelevant to the Engineering Problem
models.definition: Shannon deliberately severed the engineering problem of transmitting symbols from the semantic question of what they mean — a channel can carry a message flawlessly while the message says nothing. Use it to separate the questions of whether something got through from whether it was worth sending, which are solved by different people with different tools.

models.name: Shannon–Weaver Transmission Model
models.definition: Every act of communication decomposes into source, encoder, channel, noise, decoder, and destination — and failure can be located in any one of them, not just in the speaker. Reach for it when a message misfires: ask which stage broke before blaming the sender or the audience.

models.name: Mutual Information
models.definition: The amount that learning X reduces your uncertainty about Y — a measure of dependence that catches any relationship, not just the straight-line ones correlation sees. Use it to ask of any proposed signal, metric, or test: how many bits about the thing I care about does this actually buy me?

models.name: Rate–Distortion Tradeoff
models.definition: If you cannot afford to keep everything, there is a mathematical frontier trading how much you keep against how much fidelity you lose — and the skill is choosing which distortions are cheap. Reach for it whenever summarizing, abstracting, or budgeting: the question is never whether to lose detail but which detail you can afford to lose.

models.name: Perfect Secrecy Requires Key, Not Obscurity
models.definition: Shannon proved that unbreakable secrecy demands a key as long and as random as the message itself, and that all lesser schemes leak information the longer the ciphertext runs. Reach for it to reject security-through-obscurity anywhere: assume your method is public, and ask what secret is actually doing the work.

models.name: Boolean Algebra of Switching Circuits
models.definition: Shannon's insight that relays and switches obey the algebra of logic — so any logical proposition can be built out of physical on/off parts, and any circuit can be simplified by symbolic manipulation rather than intuition. Use it as the template for the highest-leverage move in thought: find the formal system that already governs your messy domain and let it do the reasoning.

models.name: Shannon's Demon (Rebalancing Bonus)
models.definition: Systematically rebalancing between a volatile asset with zero expected drift and cash can grow wealth anyway, because the mechanical discipline harvests the swings themselves. Reach for it as the reminder that a rule applied without judgment can extract value that no forecast could — volatility is a cost only to those who must guess its direction.

models.name: Consilience
models.definition: The claim that the sciences, humanities, and arts describe one reality and must ultimately be made consistent with each other; an explanation earns confidence when independent disciplines converge on it. Reach for it when a claim in one field quietly contradicts what a neighboring field already knows.

models.name: Sociobiology
models.definition: Social behavior — aggression, hierarchy, cooperation, mating, parental care — is itself a product of natural selection and can be studied the same way anatomy is. Use it when explaining a persistent social pattern that no amount of culture or incentive design seems able to erase.

models.name: Island Biogeography (Species–Area Equilibrium)
models.definition: The number of species an island holds settles at an equilibrium between immigration and extinction, set mostly by its size and its distance from the mainland — smaller and more remote means fewer species, with constant turnover in which ones. Reach for it whenever a system is fragmented into isolated pockets: reserves, teams, markets, or codebases.

models.name: The Superorganism
models.definition: In eusocial species the colony, not the individual, is the unit that competes and survives: workers behave like cells, castes like organs, and the whole reproduces as one. Use it when the meaningful agent in a system is the group rather than any member, and individual-level analysis explains nothing.

models.name: Multilevel Selection
models.definition: Selection acts at more than one level at once: within a group, selfish members out-compete altruists, but groups of altruists out-compete groups of selfish members — so social life is a permanent tension, never a settled equilibrium. Reach for it to explain why cooperation keeps arising and keeps decaying inside the same organization.

models.name: Biophilia
models.definition: Humans carry an innate affiliation with life and lifelike processes — savanna views, water, greenery, animals — because those cues signaled survival for most of our history. Use it when designing environments meant to restore people rather than merely house them.

models.name: Gene–Culture Coevolution
aka: Genes Hold Culture on a Leash
models.definition: Culture invents freely but is constrained and biased by inherited predispositions, while culture in turn reshapes the selection pressures on those genes — a slow feedback loop, not one-way determinism. Reach for it when arguing whether some human pattern is 'just culture' and could be changed at will.

models.name: Character Displacement
models.definition: Two similar species differ more where their ranges overlap than where each lives alone, because direct competition selects hardest against resemblance. Use it to predict that rivals sharing a market will be pushed into sharper differentiation precisely where they meet head-on.

models.name: Coase Theorem
models.definition: If property rights are clearly defined and bargaining is costless, parties will trade until the resource ends up in its highest-value use — regardless of who was given the right initially. Reach for it to see that the real question is never 'who should have the right' but 'what transaction costs are blocking the trade.'

models.name: Reciprocal Nature of Harm
models.definition: Harm is not one party wronging another; it is two incompatible uses of the same resource, and stopping A's harm to B necessarily inflicts a cost on A. Reach for it whenever a dispute is framed as villain-and-victim, to reframe it as: which of the two costs is smaller?

models.name: Comparative Institutional Analysis
models.definition: Never judge a market, firm, or regulation against a frictionless ideal; compare the actual available arrangements, all of which are imperfect and costly. Reach for it when someone proves an arrangement fails by measuring it against perfection rather than against the next-best real option.

models.name: Blackboard Economics
models.definition: Coase's charge that much theory describes a system existing only in the theorist's diagram — with no firms, no contracts, no costs of doing anything — and so yields policy advice that cannot survive contact with the world. Reach for it when a model's elegance depends on assuming away the very frictions the problem is made of.

models.name: The Lighthouse Test
models.definition: The lighthouse was the textbook proof that some goods can only be publicly provided — until Coase checked the records and found centuries of private ones. Reach for it when a canonical example is doing the argument's real work: go verify that the example is actually true.

models.name: Coase Conjecture
models.definition: A monopolist selling a durable good competes against its own future self: buyers who can wait will wait for the price cut, so without a commitment device the price collapses toward marginal cost. Reach for it in any pricing, negotiation, or holdout situation where the other side's patience is your real constraint.

models.name: Tradable Rights over Administrative Allocation
models.definition: When a scarce resource is handed out by committee, hearing, or queue, the allocation reflects influence rather than value; define clear, transferable rights instead and let exchange sort it out. Coase's spectrum argument, dismissed for decades, became the FCC auctions.

models.name: Lifeboat Ethics
models.definition: Hardin's 1974 argument that when a resource system has a hard capacity ceiling, admitting everyone who needs help sinks the boat and helps no one — so allocation rules, not generosity, decide survival. Reach for it when a moral instinct to include everyone collides with a limit that does not negotiate.

models.name: Filters Against Folly
models.definition: Hardin's three-filter test for any claim: literacy asks what the words really mean, numeracy asks what the quantities are, and ecolacy asks "and then what?" — what happens after the first effect. Reach for it when a proposal survives one filter and its advocates hope you'll stop there.

models.name: No Technical Solution Problems
aka: Class of Problems With No Technical Solution
models.definition: Hardin's opening move in the commons essay: some problems admit no fix by better technology or more cleverness, and require a change in values, rules, or what people want. Reach for it before funding another gadget to solve what is actually a conflict of incentives.

models.name: Conscience Is Self-Eliminating
models.definition: Hardin argued that asking people to voluntarily restrain themselves in a commons selects against exactly the people who comply — the restrained take less and fade, the unrestrained take more and persist. Reach for it whenever a plan depends on good actors volunteering to lose.

models.name: Mutual Coercion, Mutually Agreed Upon
models.definition: Hardin's proposed escape from the commons: since voluntary restraint is self-defeating, the participants must jointly bind themselves to rules that bind everyone — coercion they choose in advance rather than have imposed. Reach for it when designing any regime where individual defection is rational but collective defection is fatal.

models.name: CC-PP Game: Commonize Costs, Privatize Profits
models.definition: Hardin's name for the strategy — not the accident — of arranging institutions so that gains land on you and costs land on everyone. Reach for it to ask, of any arrangement, who captures the upside and who is quietly holding the bill.

models.name: Never Globalize a Problem Solvable Locally
models.definition: Hardin's rule of policy: moving a problem to a wider jurisdiction severs the feedback between a decision and its consequences, so the deciders stop paying and stop learning. Reach for it when someone proposes escalating a local failure to a larger pool.

models.name: Attention as the Scarce Resource
models.definition: Information is not the bottleneck — attention is. Simon: "a wealth of information creates a poverty of attention," so any system that adds information must be judged by what it costs the finite attention of the people who must consume it. Reach for it when evaluating dashboards, alerts, meetings, reports, or any proposal that assumes more data is free.

models.name: Near-Decomposability (The Architecture of Complexity)
models.definition: Complex systems that survive are almost always hierarchies of subsystems whose internal interactions are strong and fast while interactions across subsystems are weak and slow. Reach for it to find the real seams in an organization, codebase, or market: cut where the coupling is already thin, and analyze each part short-run in isolation, long-run only in aggregate.

models.name: The Two Watchmakers (Stable Subassemblies)
models.definition: Two watchmakers build identical watches; Hora assembles stable ten-part subassemblies, Tempus builds each watch as one thousand-piece run that collapses on every interruption. In a world of interruptions, the builder who commits to intermediate forms that hold on their own finishes; reach for it when sequencing any long build, migration, or research program.

models.name: The Ant on the Beach
models.definition: An ant's path across a beach looks intricate, but the intricacy belongs to the beach, not the ant — the creature is simple and merely follows the terrain. Reach for it before attributing complex behavior to complex inner traits: rational behavior is cut by a scissors whose blades are the task environment and the actor's modest computing power, and the environment is usually the sharper blade.

models.name: Inner and Outer Environment (Design as Interface)
models.definition: An artifact is an interface between an inner environment (its substance and organization) and an outer environment (the world it must work in); if the inner is right for the outer, the artifact serves its purpose, and you can often predict behavior from the outer environment alone. Reach for it to separate what a thing is made of from what it must accomplish — and to notice when you're over-engineering internals the outer world can't see.

models.name: Means-Ends Analysis
models.definition: Solve problems by repeatedly measuring the difference between the current state and the goal state, then applying whatever operator is known to reduce that specific difference. Reach for it when a problem is too large to search exhaustively: name the gap precisely, and the gap itself tells you which move to try next.

models.name: Ill-Structured Problems
models.definition: Well-structured problems come with a defined goal, a known state space, and a test for a correct answer; ill-structured problems arrive with none of these, and most real ones are ill-structured. Reach for it when a team is stuck: the first work is not solving but structuring — imposing a goal, a space of moves, and a stopping test that the problem did not supply.

models.name: Decision Premises
models.definition: Organizations rarely control what people decide; they control the premises people decide from — the vocabulary, the information that reaches the desk, the options considered legitimate, the identification with a subunit's goals. Reach for it when you want to change behavior at scale: edit the premises upstream rather than reviewing decisions downstream.

models.name: Minimax (Worst-Case Optimization)
models.definition: In a conflict against an intelligent opponent, choose the strategy whose worst possible outcome is best — von Neumann proved that in two-person zero-sum games this floor and the opponent's ceiling meet at a single value. Reach for it when the other side is actively trying to hurt you and you cannot rely on their goodwill or on average-case luck.

models.name: Mixed Strategy (Deliberate Randomization)
models.definition: Against an adversary who studies you, any fixed pattern is exploitable, so the optimal play is often a randomized blend of moves — von Neumann showed the minimax value exists only once mixed strategies are allowed. Reach for it when being read is the real cost: bluffing, auditing, patrolling, pricing against a watching rival.

models.name: Zero-Sum vs Positive-Sum
models.definition: Some interactions divide a fixed pie so one side's gain is exactly the other's loss; most real ones create or destroy value, so cooperation and coalitions change the total. Diagnose which kind you are in before choosing tactics — zero-sum thinking inside a positive-sum game destroys value, and the reverse gets you exploited.

models.name: Expected Utility (von Neumann–Morgenstern)
models.definition: If your preferences over gambles are consistent (complete, transitive, continuous, independent), they can be represented as maximizing the expected value of a utility function — so rational choice under risk means weighting utilities, not dollars, by probability. Use it to price risk honestly and to notice when your own choices violate the axioms.

models.name: Stored-Program Architecture
models.definition: Put instructions in the same memory, in the same format, as the data they operate on — then a machine can modify, generate, and reason about its own programs, and one general machine replaces many special-purpose ones. Reach for it whenever encoding your rules as inspectable, editable data buys flexibility that hard-wiring them cannot.

models.name: Universal Constructor (Self-Replication)
models.definition: Anything that copies itself must carry a description of itself and both build from that description and copy it blindly — von Neumann derived this blueprint-plus-interpreter structure years before DNA and mRNA were understood. Use it to see what any self-propagating thing needs: a spec, a builder, and a copier that doesn't try to interpret.

models.name: Cellular Automata
models.definition: A grid of simple cells updating from purely local rules can generate behavior no one could read off the rules — growth, patterns, computation, life. Reach for it when you suspect a system's surprising global behavior comes not from a designer or a hidden cause but from many dumb agents following a short rulebook.

models.name: Destruction and Creation
models.definition: Any mental model, held closed and refined internally, drifts toward incoherence and mismatch with reality; the only escape is to shatter existing domains into their parts and synthesize a new whole from pieces drawn across unrelated fields. Reach for it when a framework that once worked keeps producing confident wrong answers.

models.name: Fast Transients
aka: Getting Inside the Adversary's Decision Cycle
models.definition: Victory comes less from speed than from abrupt, unpredictable changes of state that reset the opponent's understanding faster than they can rebuild it, folding them back inside themselves. Reach for it when you cannot outmatch a rival on resources but can outpace them on tempo and irregularity.

models.name: Energy–Manoeuvrability Theory
models.definition: Compare rival systems not by peak specifications but by specific energy rate — how quickly each can gain, spend, and trade energy to change its state across the whole envelope of conditions. Reach for it when headline numbers favor one option but real contests are decided by the ability to transition.

models.name: Orientation Shapes Observation
models.definition: Orientation — the sediment of genetic heritage, culture, prior experience and analysis — silently filters what you notice and decides what you do before conscious choice enters; it is the hidden engine of every decision cycle. Reach for it when smart people looking at identical evidence reach incompatible conclusions.

models.name: Moral–Mental–Physical Levels of Conflict
models.definition: Conflict is waged on three planes — physical (destroying resources), mental (sowing confusion and doubt), and moral (severing the bonds of trust and legitimacy) — and their power runs in ascending order, with moral defeat the most decisive and the cheapest to inflict. Reach for it when you are winning on materiel and still losing.

models.name: Isolation vs. Interaction
models.definition: Every contest is a fight over connection: prevail by cutting the adversary off from allies, information, and their own sense of reality while multiplying your own channels outward. Reach for it in any rivalry where the decisive asset is who stays plugged into the world.

models.name: To Be or To Do
models.definition: At every fork you must choose between being somebody — accruing rank, title, and standing — and doing something that changes the world, because the compromises that buy status are precisely the ones that forfeit the work. Reach for it when advancement requires you to stop saying the true thing.

models.name: Know Yourself, Know the Enemy
models.definition: Outcomes are determined by two assessments, not one: an honest audit of your own capabilities and a real understanding of your opponent's. Reach for it when a plan rests on confidence about your side while the other side is a caricature — most defeats come from knowing only one of the two, and self-knowledge is the half people skip.

models.name: Winning Without Fighting
models.definition: The supreme skill is subduing an opponent without battle — victory by position, reputation, alliance, or arrangement so favorable that the contest never happens. Use it as a check on any strategy whose only path to success is a costly direct confrontation you could have made unnecessary.

models.name: Attack the Strategy, Not the Walls
models.definition: There is a hierarchy of targets: best is to attack the opponent's plan, next their alliances, next their forces, and worst of all their fortifications. Reach for it when you find yourself besieging the hardest, most defended thing — the point where their strength is concentrated is almost always the wrong place to push.

models.name: Deception and Formlessness
aka: All Warfare Is Based on Deception
models.definition: All conflict runs on information, so appear incapable when capable, near when far, and above all be without discernible shape — an opponent cannot plan against a form they cannot read. Use it when your intentions are legible to a rival, and invert it to ask what a confident-looking adversary is concealing.

models.name: Cheng and Ch'i (Orthodox and Unorthodox)
models.definition: Every engagement pairs a conventional force that fixes and occupies the opponent with an unorthodox one that decides the issue from an unexpected angle; the two are endlessly interchangeable. Reach for it when a direct approach is stalemated — the orthodox move's job is to create the opening the indirect move exploits.

models.name: Shih (Strategic Momentum)
models.definition: Power comes less from the force applied than from the accumulated positional energy released — like round boulders rolled down a steep mountain, or water that has piled up behind a height. Use it when effort feels heroic: the work should go into building the position, so that the decisive act is almost effortless.

models.name: Ground and Terrain
models.definition: The character of the ground — accessible, entangling, constricted, desperate — dictates what moves are even available, so the terrain is chosen before the battle is joined. Reach for it when evaluating a fight on someone else's field: choosing where to compete does more work than how well you compete.

models.name: Strike Emptiness, Avoid Strength
aka: Avoid the Solid, Strike the Void
models.definition: Aim at what is undefended and withdraw from what is prepared — an opponent who must guard everywhere is strong nowhere, so force them to disperse and then arrive where they are not. Distinct from massing force: this is about target selection, the discipline of refusing an attractive fight at a defended point.

models.name: Culminating Point of Victory
models.definition: Every successful advance consumes the strength that produced it — supply lines stretch, troops tire, the enemy concentrates — so there exists a point beyond which further pushing converts victory into defeat. Reach for it when momentum is tempting you to press on: the question is not whether you can win more, but whether you have already passed the peak.

models.name: War as the Continuation of Politics by Other Means
aka: War as an Instrument of Policy
models.definition: War is never an autonomous activity with its own logic; it is an instrument of policy, and its aims, limits, and definition of success are set entirely by the political purpose it serves. Use it whenever a means starts generating its own goals — a fight, a lawsuit, a price war — to force the question of what the conflict is actually for.

models.name: The Remarkable Trinity
models.definition: War is a shifting balance of three forces: primordial violence and passion (the people), the play of chance and probability (the commander and army), and rational calculation (the government). Any theory that accounts for only one of the three collapses; use it to check whether your model of a conflict has left out emotion, luck, or reason.

models.name: Absolute War vs. Real War
models.definition: In pure logic, conflict escalates to extremes — each side must outdo the other, without limit — but real war is dragged back toward moderation by friction, politics, imperfect information, and the fact that fighting takes time. Reach for it when a theoretical model predicts runaway escalation: ask what real-world drag is missing from the model.

models.name: Coup d'œil
models.definition: The trained ability to grasp the truth of a situation at a glance, where deliberate analysis would be too slow or the data too incomplete to support it — an intuition earned through experience, not a substitute for it. Reach for it when a decision must be made faster than it can be justified.

models.name: Moral Forces Outweigh Material Ones
models.definition: The spirit of an army, the confidence of its commander, and the will of a people are decisive factors that resist measurement — and precisely because they resist measurement, analysts systematically underweight them in favor of countable things like troops and materiel. Use it as a corrective whenever a forecast rests entirely on what fits in a spreadsheet.

models.name: Theory Educates Judgment, It Does Not Supply Rules
models.definition: In any domain governed by friction, adversaries, and chance, theory's job is to train the practitioner's eye — to accompany him to the battlefield and then leave — not to hand down formulas to be applied. Reach for it when a doctrine, playbook, or framework starts being followed instead of understood.

models.name: Jobs to Be Done
models.definition: Customers don't buy products; they hire them to make progress in a specific circumstance, and they fire them when something does the job better. Reach for it when demographic or attribute-based segmentation stops predicting behavior — ask what struggle the purchase resolves, not who the buyer is.

models.name: The Innovator's Dilemma
aka: Good Management as the Cause of Failure
models.definition: Incumbents fail not from complacency but from doing exactly what good management prescribes: listening to their best customers, protecting margins, and investing where the numbers look best — all of which rationally forbid the small, low-margin bets that later eat them. Reach for it when a decision looks obviously correct on every metric you track and the threat you're ignoring is beneath those metrics.

models.name: Resources, Processes, and Values (RPV)
models.definition: An organization can only do what its resources, its processes, and its values allow; resources move but processes and values are what actually constrain, and they are the hardest to change. Reach for it before assuming a new mandate or a star hire will make an organization capable of something it has never done — often the only fix is a separate organization.

models.name: Value Network
models.definition: A firm sits inside a network of customers, suppliers, and cost structures that defines which performance metrics matter and which opportunities look worth pursuing; the people funding you effectively control what you're allowed to build. Reach for it to explain why a company can see a threat clearly and still be structurally unable to respond.

models.name: Performance Oversupply
models.definition: Technology improves faster than customers' ability to use it, so eventually the mainstream product is better than anyone needs on the old dimension — and the basis of competition shifts to price, convenience, or customization, where the cheap entrant already wins. Reach for it to time when a good-enough alternative becomes dangerous.

models.name: Law of Conservation of Attractive Profits
models.definition: When one layer of a value chain becomes modular and commoditized, attractive profits don't vanish — they migrate to the adjacent layer that is now the bottleneck and must be integrated to perform. Reach for it to predict where money will pool next as a stack standardizes.

models.name: Good Money vs. Bad Money
aka: Patient for Growth, Impatient for Profit
models.definition: Capital that demands early profit forces a venture to find a real business model while the cost of being wrong is small; capital that demands early scale funds a big bet on an unproven strategy and turns correction into catastrophe. Reach for it when deciding how much and how fast to fund something whose strategy isn't yet validated.

models.name: The Full Cost of "Just This Once"
aka: It's Easier to Hold to 100% Than 98%
models.definition: Marginal-cost reasoning makes a single exception look nearly free while hiding the full cost — the precedent, the identity, and every future exception it licenses. Reach for it whenever the argument for crossing a line is that this instance is small and special.

models.name: Schedules of Reinforcement
aka: Fixed vs Variable Ratio and Interval Schedules
models.definition: Behavior is governed less by whether a reward arrives than by the pattern in which it arrives — fixed or variable, tied to counts or to time. Reach for it when designing pay, praise, or product loops: the same total reward produces wildly different persistence depending on its schedule.

models.name: Variable-Ratio Reinforcement (Intermittent Reward)
models.definition: Rewards delivered unpredictably, after a varying number of attempts, produce the highest and most extinction-resistant response rates — far more durable than rewarding every time. Use it to explain gambling, notifications, and abusive relationships, and to notice when you are the one being pulled by an uncertain payoff.

models.name: Shaping by Successive Approximation
models.definition: Complex behavior that never occurs spontaneously can be built by reinforcing progressively closer approximations to it, rather than waiting for the finished act to appear. Reach for it whenever a target skill or habit is too far away to reward directly — lower the bar, reward the movement, then raise it.

models.name: The Three-Term Contingency (A-B-C)
models.definition: Any behavior sits inside a triad: the cue that sets the occasion, the act itself, and the consequence that follows. When you want to change behavior, diagnose all three — most failures come from attacking the act while leaving its cue and its payoff untouched.

models.name: Superstitious Behavior (Accidental Reinforcement)
models.definition: When reward arrives on its own schedule, whatever an actor happened to be doing gets reinforced, and a ritual forms around a cause that never existed. Reach for it to explain lucky socks, trading routines, and corporate rain dances — and to ask whether your own practices were ever tested against a control.

models.name: The Skinner Box (Engineered Contingency)
models.definition: Strip an environment down until only one lever, one reward, and one measurable rate remain, and the contingencies controlling behavior become visible. Use it both as a method — isolate the contingency to see it — and as a warning: many rooms you live in are already built this way, by someone else.

models.name: Punishment Suppresses, Reinforcement Builds
models.definition: Punishment reliably suppresses a behavior while it is present, but teaches no replacement and produces avoidance, concealment, and hostility toward the punisher. Reach for it when a rule keeps being broken more cleverly rather than less often — you have taught hiding, not compliance.

models.name: Selection by Consequences
models.definition: The same variation-and-selection logic that shapes species through natural selection shapes an individual's behavior through reinforcement and a culture's practices through survival — three levels, one causal mode. Use it to stop asking what a person or institution intends and start asking what has been paying off.

models.name: Natural Law (True Law Is Right Reason)
models.definition: There exists a standard of right reason, universal and unchanging, against which any written statute can itself be judged unjust — law is discovered, not merely enacted. Reach for it when a rule is procedurally valid but you need ground to stand on in saying it is still wrong.

models.name: The Honorable vs. the Expedient
models.definition: When the honorable course and the advantageous course appear to conflict, the conflict is an illusion produced by measuring the advantage too narrowly or over too short a horizon. Use it to interrogate any deal that requires you to say 'it's wrong, but it pays.'

models.name: The Five Canons of Rhetoric
models.definition: Persuasion decomposes into five separable stages — finding your arguments, ordering them, wording them, holding them in memory, and delivering them — each of which fails independently. Use it to diagnose which stage a flat presentation actually broke at, rather than blaming the whole.

models.name: Teach, Delight, Move (Officia Oratoris)
aka: The Three Duties of the Orator
models.definition: Any speech must do three distinct jobs — inform the audience, hold their attention, and stir them to act — and doing one brilliantly cannot substitute for skipping another. Reach for it when an argument is unanswerable yet nobody moves: you taught without delighting or moving.

models.name: The Four Personae (Role-Based Duty)
models.definition: What you owe in a given moment is the product of four layers: your shared human nature, your particular temperament, the circumstances fortune dealt you, and the role you chose to take on. Use it when a decision feels contradictory — usually two of the four layers are pulling apart.

models.name: Cui Bono? (Who Benefits?)
models.definition: When causes are hidden, work backwards from who gains: the beneficiary of an outcome is the best available prior on who produced it. Reach for it against stated motives, official explanations, and any account of events that leaves the winner unexamined.

models.name: Res Publica (The People's Property)
models.definition: A state is the property of its people — an assemblage held together by agreement on law and shared advantage — so rulers are custodians of something they do not own. Use it to test whether any institution still serves the constituency that constitutes it, or has begun serving its stewards.

models.name: Inter Arma Silent Leges
models.definition: Rules built for calm conditions go quiet exactly when danger arrives, and power expands into the silence. Reach for it when designing constraints: ask whether yours survive the emergency they were written for, because that is the only test that matters.

models.name: Concentrates the Mind Wonderfully
models.definition: A hard, near, irreversible terminus strips away trivia and recruits the whole intellect on the one thing that matters — "when a man knows he is to be hanged in a fortnight, it concentrates his mind wonderfully." Reach for it when attention is diffuse: manufacture a real deadline or a real consequence rather than exhorting yourself to focus.

models.name: Knowledge Is of Two Kinds
aka: Know It or Know Where to Find It
models.definition: Johnson's split: we either know a subject ourselves, or we know where we can find information upon it — and the second kind is real knowledge, not a consolation prize. Use it to allocate memory deliberately: internalize what must be fluent and instant, index the rest.

models.name: Nothing Attempted If All Objections Answered
models.definition: "Nothing will ever be attempted if all possible objections must first be overcome" — since objections are generated faster than they can be retired, demanding a clean sheet is a disguised veto. Reach for it when a committee, or your own caution, is killing a decision by inexhaustible caveat rather than by any single decisive flaw.

models.name: The Last Refuge of a Scoundrel
models.definition: When self-interest becomes indefensible on its merits, it wraps itself in the one cause no one may attack — patriotism, safety, the children, the mission. Reach for it as a detector: sudden escalation to an unimpeachable value is evidence the ordinary argument has already been lost.

models.name: Reminded More Than Informed
models.definition: Johnson's Rambler observation that people more frequently require to be reminded than informed — most failure comes from known truths gone inert, not from missing facts. Reach for it when designing teaching, memos, or culture: repetition of the essential beats novelty of the marginal.

models.name: The Triumph of Hope over Experience
models.definition: Johnson's verdict on a man's second marriage: the vividness of anticipated good overrides a personal evidentiary record of the same bet failing. Reach for it when someone — often you — re-enters an arrangement whose track record they themselves supplied.

models.name: I Refute It Thus (kicking the stone)
models.definition: Against Berkeley's proof that matter does not exist, Johnson kicked a large stone and said "I refute it thus" — some claims are unanswerable in argument yet demolished by contact with the world. Reach for it when a theory is internally airtight and externally absurd: stop debating the premises and go find the stone.

models.name: Fractal Geometry and Self-Similarity
models.definition: Many natural and social structures repeat the same pattern at every scale, so the part resembles the whole — coastlines, lungs, river networks, price charts. Reach for it when a system looks irregular at one zoom level and you suspect the same irregularity is generating it at all the others.

models.name: The Coastline Paradox
aka: How Long Is the Coast of Britain?
models.definition: The measured length of a rough boundary grows without limit as your measuring stick shrinks, so there is no single true answer — only an answer per ruler. Reach for it whenever a metric's value depends on the resolution of the instrument and someone quotes it as a fact.

models.name: Roughness Has a Dimension
models.definition: Irregularity is not the absence of structure but a measurable quantity — fractal dimension puts a number on how rough a thing is, letting you compare coastlines, cracks, and price series. Reach for it when you are tempted to dismiss messiness as noise instead of measuring it.

models.name: Wild vs. Mild Randomness
models.definition: In mild randomness no single observation moves the average much, so sampling and averaging work; in wild randomness one observation can dominate the entire record, and the usual statistics quietly lie. Reach for it before applying any tool that assumes a well-behaved average.

models.name: The Noah and Joseph Effects
models.definition: Two ways time series misbehave: the Noah effect is abrupt discontinuity — change arrives as a flood, not a drift — while the Joseph effect is long memory, where seven fat years really do tend to follow seven fat years. Reach for it when a model assumes changes are small and independent.

models.name: Volatility Clustering
models.definition: Large changes tend to be followed by large changes and small by small, so turbulence arrives in bunches rather than spreading evenly through time. Reach for it when a risk estimate assumes today's calm predicts tomorrow's, or when recent quiet is being read as safety.

models.name: Trading Time vs. Clock Time
models.definition: Markets do not move at the pace of the calendar — time runs fast during crises and nearly stops during lulls, so a month can hold less action than an afternoon. Reach for it when annualizing, averaging by period, or assuming that equal intervals carry equal information.

models.name: Descent with Modification (Common Descent)
models.definition: Every form is a modified copy of an earlier form, so present diversity is a branching tree rather than a set of independent creations. Reach for it when you want to explain a family of things — species, languages, codebases, companies — by reconstructing what they inherited from a shared ancestor rather than by their present function.

models.name: Artificial Selection (The Breeder's Analogy)
models.definition: Darwin's argument opens with pigeon fanciers: if a breeder choosing which parents reproduce can remake a species in decades, an indifferent nature choosing over eons can do far more. Reach for it when you control who or what gets to reproduce in a system — hiring, funding, promoting — because your selection criteria, not your intentions, determine what it becomes.

models.name: Principle of Divergence
models.definition: The variants that survive best are the most divergent ones, because they compete least directly with their own kin for the same resources — so life continually splits rather than converging on a single best form. Reach for it when crowding is the problem: differentiation is not a luxury, it is the escape from a fight you would otherwise lose.

models.name: Struggle for Existence (Superfecundity)
models.definition: Every population produces far more candidates than the environment can support, and that surplus — not any designer — is what makes selection bite. Reach for it when explaining why quality rises in any arena with more applicants than slots, and why generating abundant variation matters more than picking well upfront.

models.name: The Entangled Bank (Cats to Clover)
models.definition: Darwin traced clover yields to bumblebees to field mice to village cats — a chain showing that organisms are bound by causal links too indirect to guess from inspection. Reach for it before intervening in any ecology of interests: the effect you want travels through intermediaries you have not counted.

models.name: Rudimentary Organs as Evidence of History
models.definition: Useless leftovers — an ostrich's wings, a whale's pelvis — make no sense as design but perfect sense as inheritance, which is why Darwin treated bad design as his strongest evidence. Reach for it when auditing any inherited system: the parts nobody can justify are the clearest record of where it came from.

models.name: Coevolution (Reciprocal Adaptation)
models.definition: Species that depend on each other shape each other's form, so the structure of one is readable evidence about a partner you may not have seen — Darwin saw a foot-long nectar spur and predicted a moth with a foot-long tongue. Reach for it when a strangely specific feature exists: it is fitted to a counterpart, and the fit tells you what to go look for.

models.name: The Franklin Effect
aka: Ask a Favor to Make a Friend
models.definition: Having done you a favor, a person likes you more — we revise our feelings to justify what we have already done, so the act creates the affection rather than the reverse. Reach for it to convert a rival or a stranger into an ally: ask for a small favor instead of offering one.

models.name: Moral Algebra
models.definition: From Franklin's 1772 letter to Priestley: list pros and cons in two columns over several days as they occur to you, assign each a weight, then strike out items of roughly equal weight from opposite sides until what remains is the answer. Use when a decision has too many considerations to hold in the head at once and your view of it shifts by the hour.

models.name: The Virtue Ledger (Thirteen Virtues)
models.definition: Franklin's ruled chart of thirteen virtues, worked one per week in rotation, with a dot marked for every daily fault. It asserts that character is built by measurement and narrow focus rather than by resolve — reach for it when you want to actually change a behavior instead of merely intending to.

models.name: The Junto
models.definition: A small, permanent, rule-bound club of ambitious peers who meet on a fixed schedule to trade questions, criticism, contacts, and favors under norms that forbid contradiction and point-scoring. It asserts that a deliberately assembled dozen improves every member faster than any of them manages alone — reach for it when your ambition has outrun what solitary reading can supply.

models.name: Modest Diffidence in Assertion
aka: I Conceive, It Appears to Me
models.definition: Franklin banned "certainly" and "undoubtedly" from his speech in favor of "I conceive" and "it appears to me" — asserting less leaves your listener room to agree without surrendering, and room for you to be wrong without humiliation. Use when you are right and being visibly right is costing you the room.

models.name: Matching Funds
models.definition: Franklin's device for the Pennsylvania Hospital: make your contribution conditional on others contributing, so every pledge you seek carries the leverage of the pledge already in hand. Reach for it when you have some resource but not enough, and need other people's commitment more than you need your own.

models.name: Conventional Wisdom
models.definition: Galbraith's coinage for ideas that win acceptance because they are familiar, comfortable, and flattering to their audience rather than because they are true — and which are displaced not by argument but by events that make them untenable. Reach for it when a belief is defended by how widely and respectably it is held rather than by evidence.

models.name: Countervailing Power
models.definition: Concentrated power on one side of a market tends to call into being organized power on the other side — big buyers against big sellers, unions against employers, chains against manufacturers — so competition is often restrained laterally rather than by rivals. Reach for it when analyzing a lopsided market and asking who will organize against the strong party rather than who will out-compete them.

models.name: Private Affluence, Public Squalor
models.definition: Wealthy societies systematically over-supply privately marketed goods and under-supply public ones, because private wants are advertised and public needs are not, leaving gleaming private consumption amid decaying shared infrastructure. Reach for it whenever the spending that gets defended is the spending that has a salesman.

models.name: The Dependence Effect
aka: Producers create the wants they satisfy
models.definition: In an affluent economy, the wants that production satisfies are largely manufactured by the same process of production — through advertising and salesmanship — so urgency of demand cannot be used to justify the output that created it. Reach for it when someone argues that supplying a want proves the want was real.

models.name: The Technostructure
models.definition: Real power in a large modern organization rests not with owners or the nominal chief but with the collective of technical specialists, planners, and middle managers who hold the information decisions require. Reach for it when you need to find who actually decides — follow the knowledge, not the title or the share register.

models.name: Brevity of Financial Memory
models.definition: Financial disaster is forgotten within a couple of decades, so each new generation hails the same leveraged speculation as a brilliant innovation, with the few who remember dismissed as unable to grasp the new era. Reach for it when a market's defense of itself is that this time is different.

models.name: Innocent Fraud
models.definition: Institutions propagate untruths that no one consciously invents or intends — they persist because they serve the interest and comfort of those repeating them, and so escape the guilt and the scrutiny that deliberate lies attract. Reach for it when a false belief has no liar behind it but many beneficiaries.

models.name: Money Mistaken for Intelligence
models.definition: People reliably attribute to individuals an intelligence proportionate to their wealth, so the recently enriched are heeded as sages precisely when their fortune is the product of a rising market rather than of judgment. Reach for it before granting authority to anyone whose only demonstrated skill is having been paid.

models.name: Delivery, Delivery, Delivery (Hypokrisis)
models.definition: Asked to name the first, second, and third parts of oratory, Demosthenes answered "delivery" each time: the manner of transmission dominates the merit of the content in whether an argument moves anyone. Reach for it when a correct case keeps losing to a worse one that is better performed.

models.name: Beaten by the Event
models.definition: Demosthenes' charge against Athens: a body that only deliberates once news arrives is permanently subordinate to whoever acts, because the agenda is always set by the opponent. Reach for it when your organization's every meeting is a response to something already done to it.

models.name: Aggression by Increments
models.definition: The Philippics' core warning: an adversary who advances by small seizures is never resisted, because no single step is worth the cost of fighting, yet the accumulation is decisive. Reach for it when each concession is defensible on its own and the trend line is not.

models.name: Standing Force over Improvised Expedition
models.definition: Demosthenes argued Athens should fund a permanent, modest citizen force rather than voting emergency mercenary expeditions that always arrived after the crisis had resolved itself. Reach for it when an organization repeatedly pays a premium for late, improvised responses it could have prevented with cheap standing capacity.

models.name: Pleasant Counsel Drives Out Sound Counsel
models.definition: Advisers compete for a hearing, so audiences that reward flattery select for flatterers and the honest counselor is filtered out long before the crisis arrives. Reach for it when diagnosing why a leader or an assembly hears only agreeable forecasts — the failure is in the selection, not the advisers.

models.name: The Half-Shaved Head
models.definition: Demosthenes shaved half his head so shame would keep him underground studying, a self-imposed constraint that removed the option of quitting rather than relying on resolve. Reach for it when willpower has already failed and the fix is to engineer the exit shut in advance.

models.name: The Bought Insider
models.definition: In the Third Philippic, Demosthenes locates Philip's real weapon not in his army but in the paid partisans he keeps inside each city — no wall is worth anything once the men on it are his. Reach for it when assessing a rival's cheapest attack vector: co-opting the people already inside your perimeter.

models.name: Legal Parity Is the Trigger
models.definition: Bad money only drives out good when a rule compels both to be accepted at the same stated price; lift the forced equivalence and the good simply trades at a premium instead of disappearing. Reach for it whenever quality collapses in a system — look first for the rule that prices good and bad the same, not for a failure of character.

models.name: Exit of the Undervalued
aka: The Good Money Goes Into Hiding
models.definition: The good coin is never destroyed by the bad — it is hoarded, melted, or shipped abroad to a market that prices it correctly, so the visible circulation is bad only because the good has withdrawn. Use it to read any pool that looks uniformly mediocre: ask where the quality went and who is paying it properly.

models.name: Debasement as a Silent Tax
models.definition: A sovereign short of revenue can dilute the unit of account rather than levy an open tax, extracting real resources from everyone holding the unit without ever naming the charge. Reach for it when an issuer quietly changes the definition of the thing it owes — currency, credits, grades, shares — instead of raising the price.

models.name: Recoinage (Call In the Bad Units)
models.definition: A debased system cannot be repaired by issuing good units alongside the bad — the good will only hide again; you must withdraw the bad stock outright and absorb the loss of restoration. Use it whenever raising a standard inside a population already saturated with substandard items.

models.name: Credit Follows Coin
models.definition: A borrower's cost of funds tracks the perceived integrity of what it pays with and its record of paying on the day, so restoring the quality of the money and settling punctually does more for the rate than any negotiation. Reach for it when you are tempted to solve a financing problem by cleverness rather than by becoming visibly good for it.
